paragraphs.zh: 一个代表团被派去会见亚述指挥 官。指挥官告诉犹太人，他们可以 投降，成为奴隶，并被送到帝国的 其他地方。 否则，在这场围攻结束之前，他们 “只能吃自己的粪便，喝自己的 尿”。 西拿基立修书一封，嘲讽希西家的 宗教。信上是这么写的： “不要被你的神欺骗了，他说过’ 耶路撒冷不会落到亚述王的手里。’ 想必你已经听说了亚述诸王对所有 国家所做的事，完全摧毁了他们。 你会得到救赎吗？那些被我的前任 所摧毁的诸多国家，他们的神救赎 了他们的人民吗？”

paragraphs.en: nations that were destroyed by my predecessors deliver them?” At this point, Hezekiah must have been overwhelmed by fear. He tore his clothes and put on sack-cloth. Then he went to God's temple, spread out Sennacherib's letter before him and prayed. He ended his prayer by crying out: "Now, Lord our God, deliver us from his hand, so that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that you alone, Lord, are God.” And then...and then, on the verge of their final victory... ...theAssyrians turned around and left.And Jerusalem was spared. So what on earth happened? Well it is something of a historical mystery, even today.
paragraphs.zh: 此时，希西家一定是吓坏了。他扯 下衣服，披上麻袋，来到神庙中， 将西拿基立的信呈在自己面前，向 神祷告。他的祷告以下文的哭嚎结 束： “现在，主啊，我们的上帝，把我 们从他手中拯救出来，使地上所有 的王国都知道，主啊，你是上帝。” 然后，然后……亚述人在胜利的边 缘…… ……转身离开了。耶路撒冷得以幸 免。 那么，究竟发生了什么呢？ 直到今天，这还是个历史迷题。

paragraphs.en: But to get at the truth we can start by looking at theAssyrian side of story.After his victories, Sennacherib made a stone carving celebrating this war. He claims to have taken 46 fortified towns, and over 200 thousand prisoners of war. And he claims to have shut up Hezekiah "like a bird in a cage." BUT notice what he does NOT say. He does NOT claim that he actually captured Jerusalem or killed Hezekiah. That is theAssyrian source. What does the Jewish source say? Well, the Bible is the Jewish source, and here is what it says. Immediately after Hezekiah prays. God sends a message to his prophet Isaiah who tells Hezekiah that God
paragraphs.zh: 但为了了解真相，我们可以先看看 这个故事的亚述版本。取得胜利之 后，西拿基立制作了一块石雕以示 庆祝。他声称已经占领了 46 个设防 的城镇，以及超过 2 万的战俘。 他声称让希西家噤若寒蝉，“如同一 只笼中鸟”。 但请注意他没有说什么。他并没有 声称他实际上占领了耶路撒冷或杀 死了希西家。 这是亚述的版本，那犹太人是怎么 说的呢？ 《圣经》就是犹太人的版本，它是 这么说的： 就在希西家祷告之后，上帝向他的 先知以赛亚传达了一个信息，他告 诉希西家，上帝会

paragraphs.en: will. "Defend this city and save it for the sake of David my servant." And then the next verses tell us: "Then the angel of the LORD went out and put to death a hundred and eighty five thousand in theAssyrian camp. When the people got up the next morning- there were all the dead bodies! So Sennacherib king ofAssyria broke camp and withdrew. He returned to Nineveh and stayed there." In other words, the Bible says that God sent his angels and saved Jerusalem. This is such an important miracle in the Bible, that the Dutch painter Peter Paul Reubens did a
paragraphs.zh: “保卫这座城市，为了我的仆人大 卫而拯救它。” 然后下节经文告诉我们： “随后，耶和华派使者杀死了 18.5 万亚述士兵。次日早晨，当人们醒 来时，亚述军营里堆满了尸体。 于是亚述王西拿基立就破营而退 了。他回到尼尼微，守城不出。” 换句话说，根据《圣经》的说法， 是上帝派遣使者拯救了耶路撒冷。 这是《圣经》中的一大奇迹，荷兰 画家彼得·保罗·鲁本斯曾在 1614 年就此主题绘制宏伟佳作。

paragraphs.en: magnificent painting of it in 1614. But if we put asideAssyrian propaganda and Jewish theology, maybe we can figure out a scientific explanation based on where the sources agree. Both sources agree that Sennacherib attacked Judah in 701 BC. They agree that he destroyed many Judean cities including Lachish. But they also agree that theAssyrians did NOT take Jerusalem and Judah survived. One possible explanation is that Hezekiah did a good job cutting off the fresh-water supply. Without enough fresh water, a disease like dysentery broke out in theAssyrian camp and began killing Sennacherib's soldiers.
paragraphs.zh: 但如果我们将亚述的自我弘扬和犹 太的宗教神学放在一边，或许我们 可以根据基本史诗推测出一个合理 的解释。 双方都承认西拿基立在公元前 701 年袭击了犹大。双方一致认同西拿 基立摧毁了包括拉吉在内的许多犹 太城市。但是他们也同意亚述人没 有占领耶路撒冷和犹大。 一种可能的解释是，希西家在切断 淡水供应方面做得很好。 没有足够的淡水，亚述军营就爆发 了类似痢疾的疾病，大批士兵因此 丧命。

paragraphs.en: Sennacherib realized he was losing too many men, and so he agreed to leave Jerusalem in exchange for tribute. But it’s still a historical mystery. Nobody knows the full story. In any case, the Judeans survived the cataclysm and Jerusalem survived the war. Jerusalem would fall eventually, but not for another 100 years.And when it did, it would fall to another people, because Judah would actually outlive the mighty Assyrians. But that is a story for our next class when we discuss the Neo-Babylonians!
paragraphs.zh: 西拿基立意识到他的人员损失过 大，于是他同意离开耶路撒冷，以 换取贡品。 但这仍是一个历史谜题。没有人知 道究竟发生了什么。 无论如何，犹太人在这场灾难中幸 存了下来，耶路撒冷也在战争中幸 存了下来。 耶路撒冷终将陷落，但现在气数未 尽。当这一天来临时，犹大会败在 一个新的民族手中。因为，犹大比 强大的亚述寿祚更长久。 但这将是我们下节课的主题了。下 节课我们将学习新巴比伦人。

titleZh: 第六讲：新巴比伦|空中花园下的仰望

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Today we will learn about the Neo-Babylonians. We will be looking at the years between 626 and 539 BC, this is what that looks like on our timeline. Our story begins with the reign of Ashurbanipal, the last Great Assyrian King. Ashurbanipal was the grandson of Sennacherib, theAssyrian King who ravaged Judah and nearly captured Jerusalem. ByAshurbanipal's time, Judah was still governed by its own King, but they wisely paid annual tribute to theAssyrians.
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。今天我们要学习的是新巴比 伦。 我们将要讨论公元前 626-539 年间 的历史，这是它在时间线上的样 子。 故事要从亚述末代国王——亚述 巴尼拔开始讲起。 亚述巴尼拔是西拿基立的孙子。西 拿基立摧毁了犹大王国，几乎占领 了耶路撒冷。 在亚述巴尼拔时期，犹大王国仍然 由自己的国王统治，但他们明智地 选择了向亚述缴纳岁贡。

paragraphs.en: WhenAshurbanipal came to the throne in 668 BC, theAssyrian Empire controlled more territory than ever before. They even ruled Egypt. For a while the Empire was at peace and its power was unchallenged. This gaveAshurbanipal time to pursue his hobbies, such as hunting lions, a past-time which was vividly depicted in his palace relief carvings. And underAshurbanipal's rule, the capital city of Nineveh had become one of the biggest, richest and most sophisticated cities in the world. This wealth and prosperity -combined withAssyria's literate culture- meant thatAshurbanipal was one of the first Kings in history to amass a large personal library.
paragraphs.zh: 公元前 668 年，亚述巴尼拔登上王 位时，亚述的统治疆域到达了前所 未有的高度。他们甚至统治了埃 及。 有一段时间，帝国处于和平状态， 其权力没有受到挑战。这让亚述巴 尼拔有时间去追求他的爱好。宫殿 的浮雕上生动地刻画了他的消遣 活动：狩猎狮子。 在亚述巴尼拔的统治下，首都尼尼 微成为了当时世界上最大、最富 有、最复杂精致的城市之一。 这份繁荣与财富——再加上亚述 的文化修养——意味亚述巴尼拔 是历史上第一个积累了大量个人 藏书的国王之一。

paragraphs.en: Of course, since it was ancient Mesopotamia, the "books" were all written on clay tablets. In 1851AD, the English archaeologist SirAusten Henry Laylard discovered this library while on a dig in Iraq. His discovery ofAshurbanipal's library is probably the single most important find in the history of Mesopotamian archaeology. In the ruins of the library, Laylard found the tablets for the Enuma Elis, and Epic of Gilgamesh. The reason we know about these stories today, is because Ashurbanipal had copies of them in his library. But don't be deceived by Ashurbanipal's interest in literature
paragraphs.zh: 当然，由于这是在古美索不达米 亚，所有的“书”都是在泥板上写 成的 1851 年，英国考古学家奥斯汀·亨 利·莱拉德爵士在伊拉克的一次挖 掘中发现了这个图书馆。 他发现的亚述巴尼拔图书馆可能 是美索不达米亚考古史上最重要 的发现。 在图书馆废墟中，莱拉德发现了写 有《埃努玛·埃利什》和《吉尔伽 美什史诗》的泥板。 我们今天之所以能了解到这些故 事，是因为亚述巴尼拔在他的私人 藏书中保存了这些故事的副本。 但是，不要被亚述巴尼拔对文学和 高等文化的兴趣所欺骗，有必要

paragraphs.en: and higher culture, when necessary, he could be just as brutal as his predecessors. In 653 BC, rebellions broke out in Babylon and Elam. Ashurbanipal showed no mercy when putting down these rebellions. He destroyed the rebel armies in battle and then went on to devastate both Babylon and Elam. Here you seeAssyrian soldiers killing Elamite rebels. In fact,Ashurbanipal boasted about the conquests of these rebels. He wrote: For a distance of 1 month and 25 days march, I devastated the districts of Elam. I spread salt and thorn-bush there to injure the soil. I carried off the people as booty to
paragraphs.zh: 时，他可能会和前辈们一样残忍。 公元前 653 年，巴比伦和埃兰发生 了叛乱。 在镇压这些叛乱时，亚述巴尼拔毫 不留情。他在战斗中摧毁了叛军， 接着又摧毁了巴比伦和埃兰。 这是亚述士兵正在屠杀埃兰叛军。 事实上，亚述巴尼拔曾吹嘘他征服 了叛军。他写道： 我行军 1 月余 25 天，我摧毁了埃 兰。我在那里撒下盐和荆棘，损坏 了土壤，还把百姓掳到亚述去了。

paragraphs.en: Assyria. In a month of days, I subdued Elam in its whole extent, the voice of man, the steps of flocks and herds, the happy shouts of mirth; I put an end to them in its fields, which I left for the wild beasts. And the leaders of the rebellion received the most horrifying punishments. Some of them had their tongues torn out, while others were skinned alive, as you can see from thisAssyrian carving. To truly understandAshurbanipal's nature, take a look at this relief carving. It showsAshurbanipal reclining on a couch. But what else do you notice? Take a moment to study it. There isAshurbanipal on the right
paragraphs.zh: 在一个月的时间内，我全方位摧毁 了埃兰，人声、牛羊群的脚步声、 欢快的呼喊声统统消失不见；田地 荒芜，留待野兽。 叛军的首领受到了最可怕的惩罚。 有些人被割了舌头，而另一些人则 被活活剥了皮，正如你从亚述雕刻 品中看到的一样。 要真正理解亚述巴尼拔的本性，请 看看这个浮雕雕刻。亚述巴尼拔正 躺在卧榻上。但你还注意到了什 么？ 花点时间仔细看一看。 亚述巴尼拔在图片右侧。

paragraphs.en: side of the picture. But there on the left side, hanging from a ring on the wall, is the head of the Elamite King. And that woman sitting next to Ashurbanipal, you might at first think it is his Queen. But some historians think she might be the wife of the Elamite King, forced to be one ofAshurbanipal's concubines and serve him while the severed head of her husband hangs on the wall behind her. Terror is a powerful means of control. But terror inspires hate along with fear, and so it can make the power of a regime brittle. This is what happened to the Assyrians.After Ashurbanipal died, his two sonsAshur-etil-ilani and
paragraphs.zh: 但是雕塑左侧，挂在墙上的一个环 上，是埃兰国王的头。 那个坐在亚述巴尼拔旁边的女子， 你可能会认为那是他的王后。 但一些历史学家认为那可能是埃 兰国王的夫人，被迫成为了亚述巴 尼拔的小妾。丈夫的头颅挂在身 后，却不得不服侍仇人。 恐怖是一种强大的控制手段。但是 恐怖和恐惧一起激发了仇恨，所以 它可以使政权的力量变得脆弱。 这就是亚述人的遭遇。亚述巴尼拔 死后，他的两个儿子亚述-埃提尔- 伊兰尼和辛-舒姆-利希尔开始互

paragraphs.en: Sin-shumu-lishir began to fight each other, and anAssyrian Civil War broke out. Once theAssyrians began to show weakness, their many enemies rose up against them. The Babylonians rebelled, led by a new family of Kings, and the Medes invaded from the east. And so, for fifteen years from 627 until 612 BC, theAssyrian Empire was torn apart by civil war, domestic rebellion, and foreign invasion. The Babylonian rebels and the Median Invaders formed an alliance and attacked the divided Assyrians together. The war raged back and forth, but theAssyrians began to lose. And then, in 612 BC, the combined
paragraphs.zh: 相争斗，亚述内战爆发。 亚述一旦开始显示出弱态，他的许 多敌人就开始反抗。巴比伦人在一 个新王族的领导下开始反叛，米底 王国也从东边侵入了。 因此，公元前 627-612 的这 15 年 间，亚述帝国被内战、国内叛乱和 外国入侵所撕裂。 巴比伦叛军和米底入侵者组成联 盟，共同抗击四分五裂的亚述。战 争激烈地进行着，但亚述渐渐势弱 了。 到了公元前 612 年，米底、巴比伦

paragraphs.en: Median/Babylonian force fought 3 tremendous battles against the Assyrian army on the plains outside of Nineveh. And then, once theAssyrian army has been defeated, they attacked the walls of Nineveh itself, and after a siege, they broke through. And Nineveh, the capital city of Assyria, fell. This was a seismic event in the ancient world. Suddenly the all-powerful empire, which had terrified its neighbors for 300 years, was no more. The book of Nahum in the Bible records how the Jews felt about the fall of Nineveh, and the end of the empire which had caused their people so much suffering. The Lord has given a command
paragraphs.zh: 联军和亚述军队在尼尼微外的平 原上进行了 3 场大战。 随后，亚述军队被打败。联军包围 了尼尼微，长驱直入。 于是尼尼微，亚述的首都，倒下了。 这在古代世界是一个地震事件。这 个曾经让邻居们恐惧了 300 年的全 能帝国，突然间不复存在了。 《圣经》中的《那鸿书》记录了犹 太人是如何看待尼尼微的堕落的， 以及帝国的灭亡使其人民遭受了 如此多的苦难。 尼尼微啊！耶和华已经向你下了命

paragraphs.en: concerning you, Nineveh: “You will have no descendants to bear your name. I will destroy the images and idols that are in the temple of your gods. I will prepare your grave, for you are vile.” Woe to the city of blood, full of lies, full of plunder, never without victims! …Nothing can heal you; your wound is fatal.All who hear the news about you clap their hands at your fall, for who has not felt your endless cruelty? And now, suddenly, the superpower was gone.And in its place, the Middle East was divided between 4 regional powers: The Neo Babylonians, The 26th Dynasty of Egypt, the Median Kingdom, and the Kingdom of Lydia. The 26th Dynasty of Egypt lasted
paragraphs.zh: 令：你的名字必不再传于后世；我 必从你的神庙中，除掉雕刻和铸造 的偶像；我必为你预备坟墓，因为 你没有存在的价值。 祸哉，血之城，满是谎言，满是掠 夺，满是受害者！你的创伤将你毁 灭，没有什么能将你治愈。听闻你 的毁灭，所有人欢欣鼓舞。毕竟， 有谁没有感受过你那无穷无尽的 残酷呢？ 现在，突然间，这个超级大国消失 了。在它的位置上，中东被划分为 4 个地区大国：新巴比伦，埃及第 26 王朝，米底王国和吕底亚。 埃及第 26 王朝从公元前 664 年一

paragraphs.en: from 664 BC until 525 BC. They started off as vassals ofAssyria, and then broke free after the Empire fell. There were 6 Kings in that Dynasty, and they were the very last time that Egypt was ruled by Native Egyptians. The Kingdom of Lydia had existed since the fall of the Hittite Empire in the Bronze Age collapse. But they had remained small and weak for several centuries. Suddenly the fall ofAssyria gave them an opportunity to grow and expand. The Lydians were very rich, because they controlled the trade between the Mediterranean and the Black Sea. In fact, it was the Lydians who invented the world's first coins in the 8th century BC.
paragraphs.zh: 直持续到公元前 525 年。他们一开 始是亚述的诸侯国，帝国灭亡后， 获得自由。 这个王朝经历了 6 任国王，这是埃 及最后一次为埃及人所统治。 自赫梯帝国在青铜时代的崩溃后， 吕底亚就一直存在。但几个世纪以 来，他们一直非常弱小。亚述的突 然灭亡给了吕底亚发展和扩张的 机会。 吕底亚人非常富有，因为他们控制 了地中海和黑海之间的贸易。事实 上，他们是在公元前 8 世纪发明了 世界上第一批硬币的人。

paragraphs.en: The Median Kingdom was the great power in CentralAsia, in-between Mesopotamia and the Indus River. But for now we just need to know it exists. We will go into more detail about them next class. Finally, there were the Neo-Babylonians. They took over all theAssyrian land in-between the Medes and Egypt. Here is what that post-Assyrian world looks like on a map. For the rest of this lesson, let us focus on the Neo-Babylonians. The Neo-Babylonians Empire lasted for 87 years, from 626 until 539 BC. During that time, the Empire was led by seven rulers. Here are their names and years of rule on this slide.
paragraphs.zh: 米底王国是中亚地区的大国，位于 美索不达米亚与印度河之间。但现 在我们只需要知道它的存在，下节 课我们会详细讲解。 最后，还有新巴比伦人。他们占领 了米底和埃及之间所有的亚述土 地。 这就是后亚述世界在地图上的样 子。 剩下的时间里，让我们把注意力集 中在新巴比伦人身上。 新巴比伦帝国持续了 87 年，公元 前 626-539 年。在那段时间里，帝 国由 7 个统治者领导。这张幻灯片 是他们的名字和在位的年份。

paragraphs.en: The second and greatest of the Neo-Babylonian kings was Nebuchadnezzar II. He was a young man during the fall ofAssyria, and he helped his father Nabopolassar lead the armies of Babylon. Since their most important ally was the Medes, Nebuchadnezzar II marriedAmytis the daughter of the Median King. The picture on the left is from a Babylonian seal depicting Nebuchadnezzar. But we have no idea what Amytis actually looked like. So my picture on the right is just an imagined depiction of her by a 19th century sculptor. According to legend,Amytis missed the forest and hills of her childhood home back in the Zagros
paragraphs.zh: 最伟大的新巴比伦国王是尼布甲 尼撒二世。 亚述沦陷时，他是一个年轻人，曾 帮助父亲那波勃来萨领导巴比伦 的军队。 由于他们最重要的盟友是米底国， 他赢取了米底国王的女儿埃米提 斯。 左边的图片来自巴比伦的印章，描 绘的就是尼布甲尼撒二世。但我们 无法得知埃米提斯的长相。因此， 右边的照片是一个 19 世纪的雕刻 家想象中的埃米提斯。 据传说，埃米提斯想念她童年时的 扎格罗斯山脉的森林和山丘。巴比 伦是平坦的泛洪平原，与米底完全

paragraphs.en: mountains. Babylon was a flat floodplain, which looked nothing like Media. So Nebuchadnezzar built her a series of hanging gardens stocked with the plants and animals of her home. He hoped that these hanging gardens of Babylon would relieve her home-sickness. Of course, the Hanging Gardens of Babylon might just be a legend. But it is such a romantic story that it has captured the imagination of artists for generations. This painting from 1676AD imagines their construction. But some of the things that Nebuchadnezzar built were definitely real. Probably the most famous is the Ishtar gate, which is one display at the Pergamon
paragraphs.zh: 不同。 为此，尼布甲尼撒二世为她建造了 空中花园，里面摆满了来自她家乡 的动植物。他希望这些空中花园能 够减缓埃米提斯的思乡之苦。 当然，巴比伦的空中花园可能只是 一个传说。但这是一个如此浪漫的 故事，它吸引了一代又一代艺术家 的想象。这幅 1676 年的画描绘了 艺术家想象中的巴比伦空中花园。 但是尼布甲尼撒二世建造的有些 东西绝对是真实的。最著名的可能 是伊什塔门，它是德国佩加蒙博物 馆的展品之一。

paragraphs.en: museum in Germany. The gate is visually stunning because of its beautiful blue stone. But it was actually even more impressive in real life, because it sat at the end of a grand avenue, which displayed the power and might of Babylon. Nebuchadnezzar's own words about the gate have survived into the present: "I (Nebuchadnezzar) laid the foundation of the gates down to the ground water level and had them built out of pure blue stone. Upon the walls in the inner room of the gate are bulls and dragons and thus I magnificently adorned them with luxurious splendor for all mankind to behold in awe.” But Nebuchadnezzar was still an
paragraphs.zh: 这扇门因其美丽的蓝色石头而在 视觉上令人叹为观止。但在现实生 活中，它甚至更令人印象深刻，因 为它坐落在一条大大道的尽头，展 示了巴比伦的力量和威力。 尼布甲尼撒二世对这扇大门的描 述保存到了现在： “我（尼布甲尼撒二世），把大门 的地基打到了地下水位之下，用纯 蓝的石头建造了它们。在门内室的 墙壁上，有牛和龙，因此我用华丽 的华丽装饰它们，让所有的人都心 怀敬畏地仰望它们。” 但是尼布甲尼撒二世毕竟是一个

paragraphs.en: emperor, and despite his refinement and appreciation of beauty, he was just as ruthless as theAssyrians when his power was challenged. In 587BC Judah formed an alliance with the Egyptians and King Zedekiah led a rebellion against Babylonian control. That same year, Nebuchadnezzar sent an army to crush the rebellion and capture Jerusalem. This time there would be no miraculous salvation for the Jews. The Bible tells us what happened in the second book of Kings. We learn that the Babylonians came in 587 BC and put Jerusalem under siege. Instead of trying to knock down the walls, the Babylonians decided to starve the city into submission.
paragraphs.zh: 皇帝，尽管他很文雅、有审美，但 当权力受到挑战时，他和亚述人一 样冷酷无情。 公元前587 年，犹大与埃及人结盟， 西底家国王领导了一场反对巴比 伦控制的叛乱。 同年，尼布甲尼撒二世派了一支军 队镇压叛乱，占领了耶路撒冷。这 一次，犹太人不会得到奇迹般的救 赎。 《圣经》的《列王纪·下卷》讲述 了这个故事。 我们了解到巴比伦人是在公元前 587 年来到耶路撒冷的。巴比伦人 没有试图推倒城墙，而是决定让这 座城市投降。

paragraphs.en: After two years of siege and starvation, the Babylonians broke through and captured King Zedekiah. Zedekiah's punishment was especially cruel. The Babylonians killed his children in front of him, and then they cut out his eyes, so that the last thing he would ever see would be the deaths of his sons. Jerusalem had been taken. The city was pillaged and burned, and the Jews no longer had a homeland. Then Nebuchadnezzar did what the Assyrians had done to Israel 136 years before. He took the population of Judea captive and moved them to Babylon. Modern historians don't think
paragraphs.zh: 在经历了两年的围攻和饥饿之后， 巴比伦人成功突破，并俘虏了西底 家王。 西底家收到的惩罚尤其残酷。 巴比伦人在他面前杀了他的孩子， 然后挖掉了他的眼睛，这样他所能 看到的最后一件事就是他儿子的 死亡。 耶路撒冷被攻占了。这座城市被洗 劫一空，犹太人失去了自己的家 园。 随后，尼布甲尼撒二世对犹太人做 的事，一如136 年前亚述对以色列。 他把朱迪亚的人口掳到巴比伦去 了。 现代历史学家不认为尼布甲尼撒

paragraphs.en: Nebuchadnezzar moved every Jew out of Judea. Instead we think he just removed the elites, so that the Jews would be left without leadership. And Nebuchadnezzar also destroyed Solomon's temple. From 585 until 515 BC, the Jews lived in Babylon. This 70-year period is known as the "Babylonian Captivity" in Jewish history, and it was a critical time in their survival as a people and a religion. Many parts of the Bible deal with the fall of Jerusalem and the captivity, but one of the most famous is the 137th Psalm. “By the rivers of Babylon we sat and wept when we remembered Zion (Jerusalem).” …
paragraphs.zh: 二世转移了所有的犹太人。相反， 我们认为他只是移走了精英阶层， 这样犹太人就会失去领导。 尼布甲尼撒二世还摧毁了所罗门 的圣殿。 从公元前 585-515 年，犹太人住在 巴比伦。这 70 年被称为“巴比伦 囚虏”，在犹太人的历史中，这是 他们作为一个民族和一个宗教的 生存的关键时期。 《圣经》中有许多部分都是关于耶 路撒冷和巴比伦囚虏的，但其中最 著名的是诗篇第 137 篇。 我们曾在巴比伦的河边坐下，一追 想锡安（耶路撒冷）就哭了。 我们怎能在外邦唱耶和华的歌

paragraphs.en: How can we sing the songs of the Lord while in a foreign land? If I forget you, Jerusalem, may my right hand forget its skill. This picture illustrates the emotion felt in that psalm. Now the Jews were left without a home, a king, or a temple. How could they survive as a people? The answer was the written word. During the Babylonian captivity, the Jews began to collect their traditions, laws, stories, poems and proverbs, and they wrote them down. This was the birth of the Bible.And it was the Bible, more than anything else, that saved the Jews as a culture. Robbed of their homeland, they
paragraphs.zh: 呢？ 耶路撒冷啊，我若忘记你，情愿我 的右手忘记技巧！ 这张照片展示了诗篇中的情感。 现在犹太人没有了家，没有国王， 也没有了神殿。作为一个民族，他 们将如何生存？ 答案是：书面文字。在巴比伦被囚 禁期间，犹太人开始收集他们的传 统、法律、故事、诗歌和谚语，并 且写下来。 这就是《圣经》的诞生。正是圣经， 作为一种文化，比其他任何东西都 更能拯救犹太人。 他们被剥夺了家园，成为了“一本

paragraphs.en: became "a people of a book", and that book went on to change the entire Western World. But the Jews would not be captives forever.Asavior would arrive and set them free. But this savior came from an unexpected place. So please join us next time, as I tell you that story. Thank you very much!
paragraphs.zh: 书中的民族”，而这本书继而改变 了整个西方世界。 但是犹太人不会永远被俘虏。救世 主会降临，让他们重获自由。而这 个救世主来自一个意想不到的地 方。 下节课，我们将讨论这个故事。 谢谢大家！

paragraphs.en: Hello everyone, welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Today we will be discussing the Achaemenid Persians. We will be focusing on the years between 550 and 330 BC. Here is what that looks like on our timeline. In the last lecture we covered how 4 kingdoms rose up to fill the power vacuum left behind by the fall of the Assyrian Empire: The Neo Babylonians, the 26th Dynasty of Egypt, the Median Kingdom and the Kingdom of Lydia. Today I want to focus on the Median Kingdom. The Medes were an Indo-European people who arrived in CentralAsia
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。今天我们来了解阿契美尼德王 朝的波斯人。 这一时期主要处于公元前 550-330 年之间，这是它在时间轴上的位 置。 上节课我们介绍了新巴比伦王朝， 埃及第 26 王朝，米底王国和吕底 亚王国是如何崛起并填补了亚述 帝国灭亡后留下的权力真空的。 今天我想来重点讲讲米底王国。 据估计，米底人的前身是在公元前 1100 年左右来到中亚的印欧人。我

paragraphs.en: around 1100 BC, according to our best estimates. From the 800s to the 600s BC, we think they experienced a cultural fusion with the much older Elamite culture. Probably this happened through trade and intermarriage. You can see them —Medes— in purple on this map of Indo-European language groups. After the death ofAshurbanipal, the Median King Cyaxares led his people in a war against the Neo-Assyrians, and with the help of the Neo-Babylonians, they captured Nineveh in 612 BC, ending the Assyrian Empire once and for all. In 550 BC, the Median Empire was being ruled by Cyaxares' son Astyages. Astyages' grandson overthrew him
paragraphs.zh: 们认为他们在公元前 8-6 世纪，通 过贸易和异族通婚与更古老的埃 兰文明发生了文化融合。 你可以在这张印欧语系地图上的 紫色区域找到他们。 亚述巴尼帕死后，米底国王基亚克 萨雷斯领导人民与亚述帝国开战， 在新巴比伦人的帮助下，他们于公 元前 612 年占领了亚述首都尼尼微 城，彻底终结了亚述帝国。 公元前 550 年，米底帝国由基亚克 萨雷斯的儿子阿斯提阿格斯统治。 阿斯提阿格斯的孙子居鲁士推翻

paragraphs.en: and became the new Median emperor. This grandson's name was Cyrus. But Cyrus came from a part of the Empire called Persia, and he was descended from a Persian King calledAchaemenes. So under Cyrus the Median Empire was renamed theAchaemenid Persian Empire. When Cyrus took over the Median Empire on 550 BC, it looked like this. He decided to push its borders outwards. From 547 to 546 BC Cyrus led his forces against the Lydian Kingdom and conquered them. But when Cyrus defeated the Lydian King, he did not kill or torture him, instead he made the old
paragraphs.zh: 了他，成为了新的米底帝王。 居鲁士来自米底帝国的一部分— —波斯，他是波斯国王阿契美尼斯 的后裔。 在居鲁士的统治下，米底帝国被重 新命名为波斯阿契美尼德王朝。 当居鲁士在公元前 550 年接管米底 帝国时，他的版图是这样的。他决 定将边境向外扩张。 公元前 547-546 年间，居鲁士率领 军队攻打吕底亚王国并征服了他 们。 但是居鲁士打败吕底亚王之后，并 没有杀害或折磨他，而是让国王当 上了他的一个顾问。

paragraphs.en: king into one of his advisors. Unlike theAssyrians. It was Persian policy to rule through mercy and tolerance rather than terror and coercion. Then Cyrus turned his attention south towards Babylon. When the Persians invaded the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 540 BC, the Empire was ruled by King Nabonidas. Nabonidas had been trying to change the religion of Babylon from the worship of Marduk to the worship of the moon god Sin. This angered the priest-class and the religious traditionalists in the city. And this made Nabonidas an unpopular King. As he got older Nabonidas became
paragraphs.zh: 和亚述人不同，波斯人采用的是仁 慈和宽容的政策，而不是通过恐怖 和胁迫来进行统治。 随后，居鲁士将注意力转向了南面 的巴比伦。 公元前 540 年，波斯人向新巴比伦 帝国发动入侵。当时这个帝国由那 波尼德斯国王统治。 那波尼德斯一直想要将巴比伦的 国教由崇拜马杜克改为崇拜月神 “欣”。 这一举动激怒了城里的牧师阶层 和宗教传统主义者。这使得那波尼 德斯并不得民心。 随着年龄的增长，那波尼德斯对当

paragraphs.en: less and less interested in being a king. Instead he made long trips into the desert to dig up ruins or make religious pilgrimages. While Nabonidas was away, his son Belshazzar ruled the kingdom in his place. The Persians knew all of this and probably used it to their advantage when they captured Babylon. We have four sources that tell us about this Persian conquest of Babylon. The first is the Greek Historian Herodotus. The second is another Greek Historian named Xenophon. The third is the Bible. And the fourth is the Cyrus Cylinder, a Persian record.
paragraphs.zh: 国王的兴趣越来越小。相反，他会 长途跋涉到沙漠中去挖掘废墟或 进行宗教朝圣。 那波尼德斯不在的时候，他儿子伯 沙撒便接续他的王位进行统治。 波斯人知道这个情况，并且他们可 能利用了这一优势来占领巴比伦。 我们通过四个信息源来了解波斯 是如何征服巴比伦的。 首先是希腊历史学家希罗多德。 首先是希腊历史学家希罗多德。 第二个是希腊历史学家色诺芬。 第三个是《圣经》。 第四个是居鲁士圆柱，即波斯人的 记载。

paragraphs.en: All four agree that the city was captured without a large battle or siege. The cylinder only says that Babylon was taken peacefully, while the other three sources agree that it was captured while the Crown Prince Belshazzar was having a party or religious festival. I think the Bible tells the most interesting version of this story. This version of events comes from the Book of Daniel, chapter 5. "King Belshazzar gave a great banquet for a thousand of his nobles and drank wine with them... Suddenly the fingers of a human hand appeared and wrote on the plaster of the wall, near the lampstand in the royal palace. The king watched the hand as it wrote. His face turned pale and he was so
paragraphs.zh: 四者都显示这座城市是在没有经 过大规模战斗或围攻的情况下被 占领的。圆柱上只提到巴比伦是被 和平占领的。而其余三者都记载， 它是在王储伯沙撒举行聚会或宗 教节日时被占领的。 我认为《圣经》讲述的这个版本最 为有趣。 这个故事来自《圣经》的《但以理 书》第五章。 “伯沙撒王大排筵席，请了一千位 大臣与他们纵饮狂欢......正在 狂欢之际，突现有一只人手出现， 手指在灯台对面的墙上写字；王看 见了，脸色遽变，心中惊惶无比以 至双腿发软，双膝也在乱抖。”

paragraphs.en: frightened that his legs became weak and his knees were knocking." Then all the king’s wise men came in, but they could not read the writing or tell the king what it meant. So King Belshazzar became even more terrified and his face grew more pale. His nobles were baffled. The queen, hearing the voices of the king and his nobles, came into the banquet hall. “May the king live forever!” she said. “Don’t be alarmed! Don’t look so pale! Call for Daniel, and he will tell you what the writing means.” Daniel was a wise Jewish man who had been living in Babylon with the rest of the exiles. Belshazzar called him in and Daniel explained the meaning of the writing.
paragraphs.zh: 可是王的智者进来后，竟没有一人 能够看得懂，更不知道其中的意 思。伯沙撒王便愈发恐惧，各大臣 也困惑不已。 皇太后闻讯赶来，对伯沙撒说：“愿 王万岁！请不要惊惶失色，快召但 以理来，他一定能够告诉你这些字 的意思。”。 但以理是一个聪明的犹太人，和其 他流放者一起住在巴比伦。伯沙撒 召了他来，但以理解释了文字的意 思。

paragraphs.en: This is what Daniel told Belshazzar: You did not honor the God who holds in his hand your life and all your ways. Therefore, he sent the hand that wrote the inscription. “This is the inscription that was written: MENE, MENE, TEKEL, PARSIN “Here is what these words mean: Mene: God has numbered the days of your reign and brought it to an end. Tekel: You have been weighed on the scales and found wanting. Parsin : Your kingdom is divided and given to the Medes and Persians.” The Bible tells us that the Persians captured the city that very night and
paragraphs.zh: 但以理对伯沙撒说： 你的生命及所有道路都交付于神 之手，而你却未对此神致以敬意。 于是，他让这只手写下了这段文 字。 这是所写的文字： 弥尼，弥尼，提客勒，乌法珥新。 “这句话的意思是： 弥尼：上帝计算陛下统治的年日， 到此为止。 提客勒：陛下在上帝的天平内不足 秤。 乌法珥新：陛下的王国必要分裂， 归与米底人和波斯人。” 《圣经》中描述道，波斯人就在那 一夜成功夺城，伯沙撒被杀。

paragraphs.en: Belshazzar was slain. Now I don't know if that is exactly how it happened, BUT it is a great story, and it is the origin of the English phrase: "The Writing is on the wall," which means a warning of something bad about to happen. The Persians seized control of Babylon, and by 539 BC, Cyrus was now the leader of the largest empire the world had ever seen. Then Cyrus did something that changed the world forever: he sent the Jews home to Israel. The Jews returned to Israel and rebuilt both the Temple and the walls of Jerusalem. The rebuilding of the Temple was completed in 515 BC. This began a
paragraphs.zh: 我当然不知道当时具体发生了什 么， 但这是一个伟大的故事，也是一句 英语短语的起源： （字面含义：字已书于墙上）意思 是不祥的预兆。 波斯人控制了巴比伦，到公元前 539 年，居鲁士已经统治着当时全 世界最庞大的帝国。 居鲁士做了一个影响深远的决定： 他把犹太人送回了以色列。犹太人 回到以色列后，重建了圣殿和耶路 撒冷的城墙。 圣殿的重建工作于公元前 515 年完 成。这标志着犹太人历史中“第二

paragraphs.en: period in Jewish history known as the "Second Temple Period" which lasted until 70AD. If Cyrus had not rescued the Jews from their captivity, perhaps they would have disappeared as a religion or as a distinct people. Without the Jews, it is hard to imagine the existence of Christianity or Islam.And without those two religions, the world we live in today would be unrecognizable. For this reason, some historians think that Cyrus the Great may be the most influential man in history. He is certainly a revered figure by the Jews, and is the only non-Jew in history to be granted the title of "Messiah" or "Savior." But then, around 530 BC, Cyrus
paragraphs.zh: 圣殿时期” 的开始，这个时期一 直持续到了公元 70 年。 如果居鲁士没有把犹太人从囚禁 中解救出来，也许他们会作为一个 宗教或一个独特的民族消失在历 史长河中。 没有犹太人，很难想象基督教或伊 斯兰教的存在。如果没有这两种宗 教，我们今天生活的世界将完全不 同。 因此，一些历史学家认为居鲁士大 帝可能是历史上最有影响力的人。 他无疑备受犹太人的崇敬，也是历 史上唯一一个被授予“弥赛亚”或 者说“救世主”称号的非犹太人。 但在公元前 530 年左右，居鲁士犯

paragraphs.en: made his biggest mistake. He launched an invasion of the Central Asian Steppe, and tried to chase down a tribe of horsemen led by a Scythian Queen named Tomyris, who had been causing trouble for the Persians. According to the Greek historian Herodotus, Cyrus died in a battle with these horsemen, on the 4th of December 530 BC. According to Greek and Persian tradition, Cyrus is buried here in this tomb.According to the Roman historian Plutarch, his epitaph is as follows: "O man, whoever you are and wherever you come from, for I know you will come, I am Cyrus who won the Persians their empire. Do not therefore begrudge me this
paragraphs.zh: 下了一生中最大的错误。他出兵征 讨中亚大草原上的一个斯基泰人 游牧部落。这个部落由女王托米丽 司统领，长期以来她都是波斯人的 心头大患。 根据希腊历史学家希罗多德记载， 公元前 530 年 12 月 4 日，居鲁士 死于与这些游牧骑兵的战斗中。 根据希腊和波斯的传统，居鲁士葬 在了这座陵墓里。据罗马历史学家 普鲁塔克记载，他的墓志铭如下： “世人啊，无论你是谁，来自何方， 我都知道你会来的，我是居鲁士， 创建了波斯人的帝国。所以不要吝 惜这埋葬我的土。

paragraphs.en: bit of earth that covers my bones." After Cyrus died, the empire passed on to his son, Cambyses II, who ruled it for 8 years. During that time Cambyses launched an invasion of Egypt and brought it into the Persian Empire. According to one Greek historian, the Persians knew about the Egyptian reverence for cats, so they strapped cats to their shields and threw them at the Egyptian as a form of psychological warfare. But that may just be legend. It comes from the Macedonian author Polyaenus who wrote that story 700 years after it happened. In any case, all sources agree that the Persians had conquered Egypt by 525 BC. The Egyptians would
paragraphs.zh: 居鲁士死后，他的儿子冈比西斯二 世继承了这个帝国，并统治了 8 年。 在此期间，冈比西斯发动了对埃及 的入侵，并将其划入了波斯帝国的 版图。 根据一位希腊历史学家的说法，波 斯人知道了埃及人对猫的崇拜，所 以他们采用心理战，把猫绑在盾牌 上，并将其扔向埃及人。 但这可能只是传说。这个故事出自 马其顿作家波吕埃诺斯，他在 700 年后才写下的这个故事。 无论如何，可以确定的是波斯人在 公元前 525 年征服了埃及。埃及从 此不再是一个独立的文明。

paragraphs.en: never again be an independent culture. And then three years later in 522 BC, Cambyses died, and by September of that year, a man named Darius the First was the new Persian Emperor. His only claim to the throne was his marriage toAtossa, a daughter of Cyrus. This makes Darius the First something of a mysterious figure. Here is a family tree showing you Darius' relationship to Cyrus. As you can see, Darius was NOT a son of Cyrus. So how did this Darius fellow become the Persian Emperor if he was unrelated to Cyrus?At best he was Cyrus' 3rd cousin once removed.
paragraphs.zh: 三年后即公元前 522 年，冈比西斯 去世了，同年 9 月，大流士一世成 为了新的波斯皇帝。 他取得王位继承权的唯一依据是 他和居鲁士女儿阿托莎的婚姻。这 使得大流士成为了一个神秘人物。 这是一张家谱图，从中可以看到大 流士和居鲁士的关系。 如你所见，大流士并不是居鲁士的 儿子。 那么，如果这位大流士与居鲁士没 有血缘关系，他是如何成为波斯皇 帝的呢？他充其量只是居鲁士的 三代表亲。

paragraphs.en: To explain his story Darius made a carving into the side of a mountain called Behistun. The carving explains that Cambyses went crazy and killed his own brother Bardia, and then died in an accident.After that an imposter named Gautama pretended to be Bardia and seized the throne. So Darius and 6 other noblemen killed Gautama and restored order. And then they selected Darius as the next emperor. He quickly marriedAtossa and secured the throne. Of course, nobody knows if Darius was telling the truth at Behistun. That is one of histories unsolved mysteries. But the Behistun carving is one of the most important artifacts in
paragraphs.zh: 为了讲清楚他的故事，大流士在贝 希斯敦山的一侧制作了雕刻。 雕刻解释道，冈比西斯发疯了，杀 死了自己的兄弟巴尔迪亚，然后死 于一场意外。此后，一个名叫乔达 摩的冒名顶替者假扮巴尔迪亚，夺 取了王位。 于是大流士和其他 6 名贵族杀死了 乔达摩，恢复了秩序。然后他们推 举大流士为下一任皇帝。他很快就 迎娶了阿托莎，巩固了王位。 当然，没有人知道大流士在贝希斯 敦上记载的故事是真是假。这是一 个未解之谜。 但贝希斯敦雕刻是古代历史上最 重要的文物之一。它在楔形文字的

paragraphs.en: ancient history. In fact, it played a key role in the decipherment of Cuneiform writing. Also interesting is this figure here. This is the GodAhura Mazda. He is the god of the Zoroastrian religion. Zoroastrianism was the main religion of the ancient Persians.And it still exists today, although there are only 190,000 left. Zoroastrians believe that there is only 1 God namedAhura Mazda, and the world is a struggle between the forces of good and the forces of evil. The founder of the religion was a holy man named Zarathustra or Zoroaster. It’s unclear when Zoroaster lived exactly, probably sometime between 1500 and 1000 BC.
paragraphs.zh: 破译中起到了关键作用。 这个图案也非常有趣。这是阿胡 拉·马兹达神。他是琐罗亚斯德教 的神。 琐罗亚斯德教是古代波斯的主要 宗教。时至今日它仍然存在于世 上，尽管只剩下 19 万教徒。 琐罗亚斯德教认为阿胡拉·马兹达 是世上唯一的神，世界即是善与恶 的斗争。 该宗教的创始人是一位名为查拉 图斯特拉或琐罗亚斯德的圣人。琐 罗亚斯德确切的生活年代还不清 楚，大概是在公元前 1500-1000 年 之间。

paragraphs.en: The chief book of the Zorastrians was the Avesta, which is written in theAvestan language, an Indo-European language which is the predecessor to Old Persian. Zoroastrians believe that one of the most important virtues is honesty. Telling a lie was viewed as one of the worst things you could do. The Zoroastrians also practice a form of burial called the "sky burial." They place their dead in special buildings called "Towers of Silence" which are open to the sky. This allows the vultures to fly down and eat the flesh off the bones. The Zoroastrians also place a strong symbolic emphasis on the contrast between light and darkness. Fire is an important symbol in their religion.
paragraphs.zh: 琐罗亚斯德教的主要著作是波斯 古经《阿维斯塔》，它是用阿维斯 陀语写成的。阿维斯陀语是一种印 欧语系语言，古波斯语的前身。 琐罗亚斯德教徒认为最重要的美 德之一是诚实。说谎是人们所做的 最恶劣的事情之一。 琐罗亚斯德教的教徒有一种叫做 “天葬”的埋葬方式。他们把死者 安置在一种叫做“寂静之塔”的特 殊建筑中，这种建筑是露天的。这 样秃鹫就能飞下来吃掉骨头上的 肉。 琐罗亚斯德教非常重视光明与黑 暗对比的象征意义。火在他们的宗 教中是一个非常重要的标志。

paragraphs.en: In Zorastrian temples there is a special flame which must be kept constantly burning. Some of these fires have been burning continuously for centuries. So by includingAhura Mazda in the Behistun carving, Darius was trying to show that God supported him. But regardless of his claims, many people STILL regarded Darius as a usurper. For two years he had to put down many rebellions all across the empire. Then from 520 to 513, he took the Persian army and expanded the Empire's borders all the way to the Indus River and the northern coast of the Black Sea. But then in 499 BC, a rebellion broke out on Darius's western
paragraphs.zh: 在琐罗亚斯德教的庙宇里燃烧着 一种特殊的圣火，其必须保持不 灭。有的火焰已经持续燃烧了几个 世纪。 大流士想要说明自己是天选之人， 因此他在贝希斯敦雕刻中加入了 关于阿胡拉·马兹达的内容。 但不管他怎么解释，许多人仍然认 为大流士是篡位者。在接下来的两 年里，他不得不忙于镇压帝国各地 的叛乱。 随后的公元前 520 年到公元前 513 年，他带领波斯军队扩张帝国的边 界，将其一直延伸到印度河和黑海 的北部海岸。 但是在公元前 499 年，在波斯帝国 的西部边境，爱琴海的安纳托利亚

paragraphs.en: border, along theAnatolian coast of theAegean Sea. This rebellion went on for 6 years and even succeeded in burning down the provincial capital of Sardis, before the Persians finally crushed it. These rebels were Greeks who had been living in that part of the world for over 200 years. Today we call these Greeks the "Ionian Greeks." Eventually the rebellion was crushed. But something upset Darius. The Ionian Greeks had received outside help, from a Greek city called Athens. Darius had never heard ofAthens before, but he vowed that he would have his revenge on these outsiders who had meddled in his empire.
paragraphs.zh: 海岸，爆发了一场叛乱。 这场叛乱持续了 6 年，甚至导致省 会城市萨迪斯被烧毁了，但波斯人 最终将其镇压下去。 这些叛乱分子是在那个地区生活 了 200 多年的希腊人。今天我们称 他们为“爱奥尼亚希腊人”。 虽然最终叛变被镇压了，但有件事 使得大流士心烦意乱。爱奥尼亚希 腊人的叛乱得到了一个叫雅典的 希腊城邦的帮助。 大流士以前从未听说过雅典，但他 发誓要报复那些干涉他的帝国内 政的局外人。

paragraphs.en: In order that he would never forget. Darius told a servant to tell him "Sire, remember theAthenians" three times before each meal. So who were theseAthenians who meddled in Persian affairs? Well that is the topic of our next class! Thank you very much!
paragraphs.zh: 为了将此事铭记于心，大流士让仆 人在每顿饭前都对他说三次：“陛 下，记住雅典人。” 那么，这些插手波斯内政的雅典人 到底是谁呢？我们将在下节课里 学习到！ 谢谢大家！

titleZh: 第八讲：古风时期希腊人|英雄与诸神

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on World History, today we will be discussing the Archaic Greeks. We will be focusing on the years between 800 and 500 BC. Here is what that looks like on our timeline. The last time we looked at Greece, it was in the Mycenaean Era. In those days the economy was based around regional Kings who lived in palaces in fortified towns like Mycenae. This Mycenaean period lasted from around 1500 to 1200 BC. Then around 1200 BC, a catastrophe hit Greece and many of the Mycenaean settlements were destroyed. This was part of the
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。今天我们要讨论的是古希腊 人。 我们将把重点放在公元前 800-500 年之间。这是它在我们时间线上的 样子。 我们上一次讨论希腊的时候，讲的 还是迈锡尼时期。当时，经济是围 绕着国王建立起来的，他们住在像 迈锡尼这样的坚固城镇的宫殿里。 这一时期从公元前 1500 年持续到 公元前 1200 年。 大约在公元前 1200 年，一场灾难 袭击了希腊，许多迈塞尼人的定居 点也被摧毁。从更广阔的历史背景 来看，这场灾难其实属于青铜文明

paragraphs.en: broader BronzeAge Collapse, which struck the Eastern Mediterranean. After the Collapse, Greece entered a dark age which lasted for about 400 years. The Dark Ages ended around 800 BC. But after those 4 centuries, Greek society had changed. Now instead of the economy being organized around palaces, it was organized around cities and towns. In Greek a city or town was called a "Polis" and it was the center of Greek life and organization. At this time, the Greeks were divided into city-states, like the ancient Sumerians or Mayans. The Greeks were also divided into
paragraphs.zh: 崩溃的一环，这次大崩溃摧毁了整 个地中海东部地区。 青铜时代崩溃之后，希腊进入了一 个持续约 400 年的黑暗时代。 黑暗时代大约结束于公元前 800 年。然而，经历了这 4 个世纪的黑 暗时代之后，希腊社会发生了变 化。 此时，经济不再围绕着宫殿，而是 以城市和城镇为中心组织起来的。 在希腊语中，一个城市或城镇被称 为“城邦”，它是希腊生活和组织 的中心。 在这个时候，希腊被分散成了各个 城邦，就像古代的苏美尔人或玛雅 人一样。 希腊人也根据其方言被划分到了

paragraphs.en: different regions depending on the dialect of Greek which they spoke. Here is a map showing the names of these different Greek dialects and where they were spoken. As the DarkAges were ending, the economy began to improve, and the population started growing. During the 8th century the population of Greece doubled according to some estimates. This increase in population was a good thing, but it also created problems.As you can see in this picture, Greece is very mountainous, so there isn't much good land for farming. In some of the smaller "Polis" the people began to run out of space for farms. But the Greeks had always been
paragraphs.zh: 不同的区域。 这张地图展示了不同的希腊方言， 和它们的使用区域。 随着黑暗时代的终结，经济逐渐复 苏，人口也开始增长。 据估计，公元前 8 世纪，希腊的人 口翻了一番。 人口的增长是一件好事，但也带来 了问题。正如你在这幅图中所看到 的，希腊位于多山地区，所以没有 多少好土地可以耕种。在一些较小 的“城邦”中，人们开始为农场耗 尽空间。 但是希腊人一直都是好水手，所以

paragraphs.en: good sailors, so they solved this problem by leaving their home cities and setting up colonies. These colonies would maintain a cultural and economic relationship with their mother city called the "Meter-Polis" or "Metropolis" That's because in Greek "meter" means mother and "polis" means city. This is the origin of the English word "Metropolis" which means big city, since the mother city was almost always bigger than the colony. The Greeks set up colonies all across the Mediterranean. This map shows that spread during the colonial era. At the same time, the Phoenicians,
paragraphs.zh: 他们离开了自己的家乡，建立了殖 民地，解决了这个问题。 这些殖民地与其母城在文化与经 济上一脉相承，他们将母城称为 “大都会”。 那是因为在希腊语中，“meter”指 “母亲”，“polis”指城市。 这是英语单词“大都会”的起源， 意思是大城市，因为母城几乎总是 大于其殖民地。 希腊人在地中海各地建立殖民地。 这张地图展示了殖民时代的殖民 发展。 与此同时，腓尼基人——另一个伟

paragraphs.en: the other Great sailors were also setting up colonies. There was some conflict between the Phoenicians and the Greeks, but for the most part they coexisted peacefully and did business with each other. The Greeks tended to colonize the northern coast of the Mediterranean while the Phoenicians colonized the southern coast. And as the Greeks did more and more business with the Phoenicians, they learned about their most powerful technology, theAlphabet. The Greeks pretty quickly realized the value of theAlphabet.And since they had lost the Linear B writing system of the Mycenaeans during the DarkAge, they were open to adopting new ideas.
paragraphs.zh: 大的水手民族也纷纷建立殖民地。 腓尼基人和希腊人之间有一些冲 突，但在很大程度上，他们和平共 处，互相通商。 希腊人倾向于在地中海的北部海 岸建立殖民地，而腓尼基人则占领 了南部海岸。 随着希腊人越来越多地与腓尼基 人做生意，他们了解到腓尼基人最 强大的技术——字母表。 希腊人很快就意识到了字母表的 价值。由于他们在黑暗时期失去了 迈锡尼人的线性 B 书写系统，他们 对采用新思想持开放态度。

paragraphs.en: But the PhoenicianAlphabet was not perfectly suited to the Greek language. Phoenician is a Semitic language, so it has fewer vowel sounds than Greek, which is an Indo-European language. Phoenician also has several sounds which do not exist in Greek, and vice-versa. So the Greeks solved this problem by changing the sounds which some of the letters represented. For example, the circle symbol represented a "gh" sound in Phoenician, but that's not really a sound in Greek. So they changed its meaning and had the circle represent the "oh" sound instead. To distinguish the Greek letters
paragraphs.zh: 但是腓尼基字母表并不完美适配 希腊语。 腓尼基语是一种闪族语，其元音比 希腊语的要少，而希腊语则是一种 印欧语。腓尼基语也有一些在希腊 语中不存在的发音，反之亦然。 所以希腊人通过改变一些字母所 代表的发音来解决这个问题。 例如，一个圈形符号在腓尼基语中 代表“gh”的音，但在希腊语中并 没有“gh”这样的发音。 所以希腊人改变了它的意思，让圈 形符号代表“oh”的音。 为了区分希腊字母和腓尼基符号，

paragraphs.en: from the Phoenician symbols, the Greeks also rotated some of the letters, or flipped them in the opposite direction, or added a few more lines. Here are both alphabets side by side. As the Greeks rediscovered writing, with the help of the Phoenicians, the DarkAges came to a close, and we begin to have written records again. Of course, the Greeks had not given up on telling history during the DarkAges, instead they had relied on an oral tradition of story-tellers and poets. The Greeks living during the Dark Ages believed that the people living before them were stronger, smarter, braver and more heroic. So they
paragraphs.zh: 希腊人还旋转了一些字母，或者把 它们翻转到相反的方向，或增加了 一些线条。 这里把腓尼基字母和希腊字母并 排放在一起，你可以自行感受一 下。 当希腊人在腓尼基人的帮助下重 新发明文字后，黑暗时代结束了， 我们又开始有了书面记录。 当然，即便是在黑暗时代，希腊人 也没有放弃讲述历史。相反，他们 借由说书人和诗人之口来讲述传 统。 生活在黑暗时代的希腊人认为，他 们的先人更强壮、更聪明、更勇敢、 更有英雄气概。所以他们开始讲述 关于这些英雄祖先的浪漫故事。

paragraphs.en: started telling romantic stories about these heroic ancestors. Then when the Greeks got the alphabet, they wrote down these romantic stories. The most famous of these romantic storytellers was a blind Poet named Homer. We are not sure if Homer was one person or many people, but we think he lived in the 8th century BC, and he is the traditional author of the Illiad and the Odyssey, which are both Epic poems. The Illiad tells the story of a few weeks in the final year of the Trojan War. The Odyssey tells the story of the Greek King Odysseus and his 10-year long journey home from the
paragraphs.zh: 当希腊人拥有字母表之后，他们就 写下了这些故事。 最著名的浪漫故事讲述者是一位 名叫荷马的盲诗人。 我们尚不能确认荷马究竟是一个 人还是许多人，但我们认为他生活 在公元前 8 世纪，是《伊利亚特》 和《奥德赛》这两部史诗的作者。 《伊利亚特》讲述了特洛伊战争最 后一年中几个星期的故事。 《奥德赛》讲述了希腊国王奥德修 斯的故事，以及他在特洛伊战争战 争结束后返回家乡的 10 年漫长之

paragraphs.en: that War. In Europe, the Greeks were the first people to develop writing, so the Illiad and the Odyssey are the very beginning of European Literature. The other Greek author to emerge in this time period was Hesiod. Two of his books survive: Works and Days and the Theogony. Along with Homer's work, these are the oldest sources for many of the Greek myths. The Greek myths told stories about their gods and heroes. The Greeks worshipped 14 Great Olympian Gods: Zeus, Hades, Poseidon, Hera, Hestia, Demeter,Ares, Hephaestus, Aphrodite,Apollo,Artemis,Athena, Hermes and Dionysus.
paragraphs.zh: 旅。 希腊人是欧洲最早发展出文字的 人，所以《伊利亚特》和《奥德赛》 就是欧洲文学的开端。 这段时期还出现了另一位作家：赫 西奥德。他有两本书得以幸存：《劳 作与时日》与《神谱》。 和荷马的作品一样，这些都是许多 希腊神话最古老的来源。 希腊神话讲述了他们的神和英雄 的故事。 希腊人崇拜 14 位伟大的奥林匹亚 神： 宙斯，哈迪斯，波塞冬，赫拉，赫 斯提，得墨忒耳，阿瑞斯，赫菲斯 托斯，阿弗罗狄忒，阿波罗，阿耳 特弥斯，雅典娜，赫耳墨斯和狄奥

paragraphs.en: The Greeks built temples to these gods. First they were made out of wood. Later they were built out of stone as Greek culture became more sophisticated and wealthy. One of the most famous of these temples was the Temple toArtemis at Ephesus. As the Greeks sailed around the Mediterranean and met other people who were not Greeks, they developed a strong sense of themselves as "Greek." But theAncient Greeks didn't call themselves Greek, "Greek" is a Roman word. Instead the Greeks called themselves "Hellenes" since they believed that a man named "Hellen"
paragraphs.zh: 尼索斯。 希腊人为这些神建造了神庙。起初 是用木头做的，后来随着希腊文化 逐渐成熟富庶，便改用石头建筑。 其中最著名的寺庙之一是位于以 弗所的阿耳特弥斯神庙。 当希腊人在地中海周围航行，遇到 其他民族的时候，他们就形成了一 种强烈的民族认同感。 但是古希腊人并不称自己为希腊 人，“希腊”是罗马术语。 相反，希腊人自称为“希伦人”， 因为他们相信一个名叫“希伦”的 人是所有希腊人的祖先。

paragraphs.en: was the ancestor of all the Greeks. This concept of a national Greek community greater than the Polis is called "Pan-Hellenism" which means "All Greeks". One of the things that contributed most strongly to Pan-Hellenism was a tradition that began in 776 BC, to hold a religious festival honoring the gods at the town of Olympia. As part of that festival there was a 200-meter footrace. The winner of that race won great honor. This was the first of the Olympic games. Here is the stadium where the footrace was held. Atradition developed of holding the games every 4 years on the first full moon after the summer solstice.
paragraphs.zh: 一个比城邦更大的希腊社会的概 念被称为“泛希腊主义”，意思是 “所有的希腊人”。 对泛希腊主义贡献最大的要数一 项开始于公元前 776 年的传统：在 奥林匹亚镇举行宗教节日来纪念 众神。 该节日有一个项目时 200 米赛跑。 获胜者将获得极高的殊荣。 这就是奥运会的前身。 这是一个体育场，在那里举行了竞 走比赛。 这项传统逐渐成型：夏至之后的第 一个满月举办运动会，每四年一 次。

paragraphs.en: The games began as a local event, but by the 6th century BC, all of Greece was taking part. The Greeks also developed the tradition of the "Olympic Truce", which paused all wars so that people could travel safely. The games attracted many tourists, who brought a lot of money to Olympia. The custodians of the games used that money to build a giant statue of Zeus in 435 BC. This statue was one of the 7 wonders of the ancient world. At first, the footrace was the only Olympic event. But over time other sports were added to the games. After a while the Greeks started using the Olympics as a way of keeping records, which is very
paragraphs.zh: 奥运会最初是个地区性时间，但到 了公元前 6 世纪，所有的希腊人都 参与了进来。 希腊人还发展起了“奥林匹克休战 原则”，暂停所有战争，以保证人 们可以安全出行。 奥运会吸引了许多游客，为奥林匹 亚带来了财富。公元前 435 年，奥 运会的管理者用这些钱建造了一 座巨大的宙斯雕像。 这座雕像是古代世界七大奇迹之 一。 一开始，竞走是唯一的奥运项目。 但随着时间的推移，其他的运动也 被加入到奥运会中。 过了一段时间，希腊人开始把奥运 会作为一种记录方式，这对历史学 家来说是非常有用的。

paragraphs.en: useful to historians. The Olympic tradition survived for over 1000 years and 293 Olympiads were held, until the Roman Emperor Theodosius outlawed the games in 393AD. But Greek life was not all sports and trade. The Greek city states also fought wars with each other. The main soldier in these times was the hoplite, a heavily armored spearman. The hoplite fought with weapons made out of iron or bronze. He was equipped with a helmet, a heavy spear, a heavy wooden shield, a short sword, a breast-plate and sometimes a type of metal shin guard called "greaves." Unlike theAssyrians, the Greeks
paragraphs.zh: 奥林匹克传统延续了 1000 多年， 并举行了 293 届奥林匹克运动会， 直到罗马皇帝狄奥多西在公元 393 年宣布奥运会为非法。 但希腊人的生活并不全是体育和 贸易。希腊城邦还在互相打仗。 这个时期最主要的士兵叫做“古希 腊重装步兵”，是一种全副武装的 矛兵。他们战斗时使用铁或青铜制 成的武器。 每名士兵配有一个头盔、一支重 矛、一个沉重的木盾、一把短剑、 一个护胸甲，有时还佩戴护胫甲。 与亚述不同的是，希腊人负担不起

paragraphs.en: could not afford to maintain a professional army. So the hoplites consisted of all the men in a city who could afford the equipment, and were strong enough to fight. That meant that the Greek city-states were defended by citizen-soldiers drawn from the wealthy and middle classes. When on the Battlefield, the hoplite's would link their shields together to form a shield wall. Then the front ranks would put their spears forward towards the enemy while the rear ranks would point them into the air to deflect missile weapons. This type of formation was called the phalanx, and it was the backbone of Greek warfare for over 700 years.
paragraphs.zh: 维持一支专业军队的费用。因此， 古希腊重装步兵是由城中所有能 够负担装备、并且身体强健的男性 组成的。 这意味着，希腊城邦受到了来自富 裕阶层和中产阶级的公民军队的 保护。 在战场上，重装步兵会用盾牌连成 一个盾墙。前排士兵将长矛指向敌 人，使得军队向前推进，而后排则 将长矛指向空中，以便挡开射击武 器。 这种编队方式被称为“方阵”。700 多年来，它一直是希腊战争的中坚 力量。

paragraphs.en: By the 5th century BC, two cities had emerged as the most powerful in Greece: Athens and Sparta. The Spartans were led by two Kings and a council of elders. They placed a strong emphasis on military strength and power. TheAthenians were different. They had been ruled by Kings until 508 BC, but then they overthrew their King and established the first Democracy in history. Just ten years into this Democratic experiment, the Ionian Greeks sent Ambassadors toAthens asking for support in their rebellion against Persia. And theAthenians, moved by a feeling of Pan-Hellenic patriotism, decided to send 20 ships to help the Ionians.
paragraphs.zh: 到公元前 5 世纪，两个城市成为希 腊最强大的城市：雅典和斯巴达。 斯巴达人由两个国王和一个长老 委员会领导。他们非常重视军事实 力和权力。 雅典人却有所不同。他们在公元前 508 年被国王统治，但后来他们推 翻了国王，建立了历史上第一个民 主国家。 就在这场民主实验开始的第十个 年头，爱奥尼亚的希腊人派大使到 雅典请求支援他们对波斯的反抗。 雅典人，被一种泛希腊的爱国主义 情绪所感动，决定派出 20 艘船去 帮助爱奥尼亚人。

paragraphs.en: And this of course got the attention of the Persian emperor Darius the Great, so the Greeks and the Persians began moving towards conflict. So please join me next time as we learn about that conflict in the Persian-Greek Wars! Okay everyone, I hope you enjoyed that lesson and want to learn more. I have some recommendations for you. First I strongly recommend The History of Ancient Greece Podcast by Ryann Sitt. And I also recommend The Greeks by Roy Burrell with Illustrations by Peter Connolly. I had this book as a boy and loved it. The Extra Credits Youtube channel
paragraphs.zh: 这当然引起了波斯皇帝大流士大 帝的注意，所以希腊人和波斯人开 始走向冲突。 下节课，请和我一起了解波斯-希 腊战争。 好了，希望大家喜欢这节课。如果 你想要了解更多，我可以向你推荐 一些资源。 首先，我强烈推荐由里恩·西特所 著的古希腊历史播客。 我还推荐罗伊·伯勒尔所著、彼 得·康诺里插画的《希腊人》，我 小时候就读过这本书，并且非常喜 欢。 “游戏选修课”的 Youtube 频道在

paragraphs.en: also did a great series on the history of theAlphabet and the Greek/Phoenician connection. And if you can, take a look at the Illiad and the Odyssey. Or the works of Hesiod. Thank you very much!
paragraphs.zh: 字母表的历史和希腊/腓尼基的联 系上做了一个了不起的系列。 如果可以的话，读一读《伊利亚特》 和《奥德赛》，或赫西奥德的作品。 谢谢大家！

paragraphs.en: Hello everyone and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Today we will be learning about the Persian-Greek Wars. These took place from 499 to 479 BC. Here is what that looks like on our timeline. After crushing the Ionian rebels in 493 BC, Darius turned his attention towards Mainland Greece. He had a servant remind him about theAthenians every day so that he would not forget them. Since Darius is a big character in this story, and these lessons are so visual, we need a good picture of him. The best image we have is the
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。今天我们来讲讲波斯与希腊之 间的战争。 本课的内容主要发生在公元前 499-479 年的这段时间里。在时间 轴上是这样的。 在公元前 493 年击败爱奥尼亚叛军 后，大流士的注意力转向了希腊大 陆。 他甚至安排了一个仆人每天提醒 他关于雅典人的事情，好让自己不 会忘记他们。 由于大流士是这个故事里的一个 重要角色，为了让课程更加直观， 我们需要更好地了解他的形象。 现存最完好的肖像是贝希斯敦石

paragraphs.en: Behistun rock carving, I have a picture of it on the left. But that's hard to see clearly. So instead I'll be using this artists' idea of Darius on the right because it is more colorful. In 492 BC Darius the Great sent an army into Greece. The army moved overland while a fleet sailed alongside and resupplied the troops. As the Persians moved through the area, all of the local kingdoms submitted to them. But then as the Persian fleet tried to make its way around the Mount Athos Peninsula, strong winds wrecked the ships. Now without the support of their ships, the PersianArmy was forced to turn back.
paragraphs.zh: 刻，也就是左边的这张照片。但它 其实不太清晰。 因此，我采用了右边这张艺术家对 大流士的假想图，因为它更生动多 彩。 公元前 492 年，大流士大帝派遣军 队进入希腊。军队从陆路出发，一 支舰队沿岸航行，为部队提供补 给。 波斯人经过这一地区时，各个城邦 都俯首称臣。 但当波斯舰队在绕行阿索斯山半 岛时，因为遭遇大风而失事了。 没有了舰队的后勤支援，波斯军队 不得不打道回府。

paragraphs.en: But the Greeks knew that they would return. Darius then began preparing for the second invasion. The next year, in 492 BC, Darius sent Persian ambassadors to all of the different Greek city states. They asked the Greeks to give them Earth and Water, the ritual symbols of submission to the Persian King. And most of the Greek cities gave it to them. But two cities refused:Athens and Sparta. In those cities the ambassadors were thrown into a muddy pit as the Greeks taunted them by saying: "THERE you may have your earth and water!"
paragraphs.zh: 但希腊人知道，他们是不会就此罢 休的。 大流士随后便开始为第二次入侵 做准备。 第二年，即公元前 492 年，大流士 向每个希腊城邦都派遣了波斯使 节。 他们要求希腊人献给他们土和水， 这是屈服于波斯国王的仪式象征。 大部分的希腊城邦都照做了。 但是有两个城邦拒绝了:它们就是 雅典和斯巴达。 当地的希腊人把使者们扔进了泥 坑里，还嘲笑道: “你要的泥土和水在这呢!”

paragraphs.en: These pits were so deep that the fall killed the Persian ambassadors. There was no turning back for Athens and Sparta now!And in 490 BC, the Persians came a second time. This time Darius sent 600 ships which supported and carried a force of 27,000 soldiers. First they sailed to Rhodes and then to Samos. These were Greek islands that had submitted to the Persians, so they were not harmed. But the Greek settlements at Naxos, Karystos and Eretria HAD resisted. And so the Persian army destroyed these towns and sold their people into slavery. Then the Persians moved on to Athens, and inAugust of 490 BC,
paragraphs.zh: 这些坑非常深，因此波斯的使者们 被直接摔死了。 这下雅典和斯巴达可没有退路了！ 公元前 490 年，波斯人第二次大军 压境。 这次，大流士派出了 600 艘船，船 上载有 27,000 名士兵和补给。 他们先航行到罗兹岛，随后又到了 萨摩斯岛。这些希腊岛屿已经屈服 于波斯人，因此波斯人没有伤害他 们。 但是位于纳克索斯岛、卡里斯托斯 以及埃瑞特里亚这些地区的希腊 城邦进行了抵抗。于是波斯军队摧 毁了这些城镇，并把他们的人民卖 为奴隶。 随后波斯人向雅典进发。公元前 490 年 8 月，他们在距雅典 42 公里

paragraphs.en: they landed at Marathon Bay, just 42 kilometers from the city. Here is what Marathon beach looks like today. The Persians brought their ships up onto that beach and began to unload their soldiers and equipment. TheAthenians knew they were in trouble, so they asked for help. TheAthenians sent a runner named Pheidippides to Sparta for reinforcements. Pheidippides ran as hard and as fast as he could. He ran 240 kilometers without rest, and reached Sparta the next day after a 30 hour non-stop run. Unfortunately, the Spartans were in the middle of the festival of Carnea.
paragraphs.zh: 的马拉松湾登陆 这是马拉松海滩的现状。 波斯人把他们的船只拖到海滩上， 开始卸下士兵和装备。 雅典人知道自己情况危急，所以他 们赶紧求援。 雅典人派了一个叫费迪皮迪兹的 人前往斯巴达请求增援。 费迪皮迪兹拼了命地奔跑。 他日夜不休地跑了 240 公里，经过 30 个小时的不间断奔跑后，他在第 二天到达了斯巴达。 不幸的是，此时正值斯巴达人的卡 内亚节。在下一次满月之前，他们

paragraphs.en: Their religion forbade any military activity until the next full moon! They told Pheidippides that they would not be able to send their soldiers for another ten days. TheAthenians were on their own. So theAthenians put their army together. Remember, theAthenian army was not a professional force, it was made up of all the men in the city strong enough to fight and rich enough to afford the equipment. TheAthenians only had about 10,000 soldiers. They would be outnumbered by the Persians by about 3 to 1. TheAthenian army marched to Marathon, but unlike the Persians they had no horses, so they stayed
paragraphs.zh: 的宗教都禁止进行任何军事行动! 他们对费迪皮迪兹说，未来的十天 内他们都不能派出援军。 雅典人现在孤立无援了。 所以雅典人自己集结了一支军队。 要知道，雅典军队并不是一支专业 的军队，而是由城里所有具备战斗 力、并且买得起装备的男性组成。 雅典人只有 1 万名士兵。波斯军队 数量是他们的三倍。 雅典军队向马拉松地区出发，因为 不像波斯人有马匹，所以他们只能 远离平原地区，在山地和树林以免

paragraphs.en: off of the plain and up in the hills and trees to protect themselves from a cavalry attack. For the next five days they watched the Persians but did not attack. This is what the battlefield looks like. The Persians were on the beach, and theAthenians were up in the hills watching them. Between them was a flat plain, about 1500 meters wide. And then, for some reason, perhaps overconfidence, the Persian cavalry was sent off on a mission during the sixth day. TheAthenian general, Militiades realized that he had a golden opportunity. He put his men into formation, and then onAugust 12th, 490 BC, he gave the signal:
paragraphs.zh: 受到骑兵的攻击。在接下来的五天 里，他们都只是观察波斯军队，并 没有发起进攻。 这就是当时的战场。波斯军队驻扎 在海滩上，雅典军队则在山上观望 着他们。 双方之间是一片平原，大约有 1500 米宽。 然而，或许是过于自信，波斯骑兵 在第六天被派出去执行任务了。 雅典统帅米太亚德意识到，这是一 个绝好的机会。他命令士兵列队， 在公元前 490 年 8 月 12 日，他发 出了信号：

paragraphs.en: "At them" and theAthenian army began to march. There were 1500 meters of flat ground between the two armies. TheAthenians crossed that land at a swift march. Then in the last 200 meters they came within range of Persian archers and the arrows began to fall upon them. So theAthenians broke into a run and crossed those last 200 meters at top speed. The Persians thought their arrows would quickly kill the Greeks. But then they realized with growing horror that the Greek shields and armor were protecting them. And then the two armies collided.
paragraphs.zh: “前进” 雅典军队开始进发。 两军之间是 1500 米宽的平地。 雅典人快速行进，穿过那片空地。 在最后 200 米的时候，他们进入了 波斯弓箭手的射程，弓箭开始落向 他们。于是雅典人开始冲锋，以最 快的速度通过了最后 200 米。 波斯人认为他们的弓箭能很快消 灭希腊人。但随后他们越来越恐惧 地意识到，希腊人的盾牌和盔甲给 他们提供了保护。 然后两军相遇了。

paragraphs.en: Unlike the Greeks, the Persian spearmen did not have armor, and their shields were made of wicker instead of wood. Now the Greeks were too close for the Persians to use their arrows, and their spearmen began to have difficulty fighting the better equipped Greeks. And then fear overtook the Persians and they panicked. The men began throwing down their equipment and ran for the ships. TheAthenians pressed forward and chased them into the water. There are stories of Greek soldiers becoming so excited by the victory that they waded into the ocean and even tried to grab the Persian ships and pull them back.
paragraphs.zh: 与希腊人不同，波斯的长矛手没有 盔甲，他们的盾牌是用柳条而不是 木头做成的。 现在希腊人距离太近，波斯人无法 使用弓箭，他们的长矛手也难以和 装备更好的希腊人作战。 恐慌很快笼罩了波斯人，他们惊慌 失措。士兵们丢盔弃甲，向船只跑 去。 雅典人乘胜追击，把他们赶到了海 里。 有故事说，希腊士兵因胜利而兴奋 不已，他们涉水入海，甚至想要徒 手抓住波斯船只并将其拉回来。

paragraphs.en: When it was all over, nearly 6000 Persians lay dead on the beach, while theAthenians had lost only 200 men. The Persians tried to sail around the Attic Peninsula and reach Athens while theAthenian army was still at Marathon, but the Greeks saw what the Persians were doing and they ran 40 kilometers in full armor to protect their city. The Persians saw that they had missed their chance and decided to retreat. TheAthenians had won! This battle is the origin of the Marathon foot race. We will talk more about that in the questions section. The Persians retreated, but Darius
paragraphs.zh: 战斗结束后，将近 6000 名波斯人 死在了海滩上，而雅典军队只损失 了 200 人。 波斯军队还想趁雅典军队在马拉 松地区时，绕过阿提卡半岛偷袭雅 典，但希腊人识破了波斯人的计 划，他们全副武装地急行军 40 公 里，赶回来保护他们的城市。 波斯人知道大势已去，决定撤退。 雅典人胜利了！ 这场战役正是马拉松长跑的起源。 我们在后面还会聊到这件事。 波斯人撤退了，但大流士发誓要重

paragraphs.en: vowed to re-organize and come back for a 3rd invasion, which he would lead himself. But then Egypt rebelled in 486 BC. So Darius had to divert his attention to that problem. And then Darius died that same year from old age. As he was dying, he made his son Xerxes promise to carry on the invasion. And Xerxes kept that promise. It took him some years to secure his throne, and organize the army, but in 480 BC, Xerxes returned to Greece, intending to conquer the whole country. Xerxes put together a force four times bigger that the one that had
paragraphs.zh: 新筹备，再次回来进行第三次入 侵，而且这一次，他还准备御驾亲 征。 但是在公元前 486 年，埃及发生了 叛乱。 所以大流士不得不把注意力转移 到这个问题上来。 由于年岁已高，大流士在当年去世 了。 在他弥留之际，他让儿子薛西斯答 应继续执行他的入侵计划。 薛西斯遵守了诺言。他花了数年时 间来巩固他的权力、组织军队。公 元前 480 年，薛西斯再度出征，意 图征服整个希腊。 薛西斯集结的军力是投入马拉松 战役的四倍。

paragraphs.en: fought at Marathon. He organized 100,000 soldiers and launched a land invasion of Greece, one supported by hundreds of ships. Xerxes force was so large that it drank rivers dry as it marched. When he needed to cross the Hellespont, he constructed a bridge of ships. To avoid the disastrous winds at Mt. Athos, he dug a canal through the peninsula instead! Athens, Sparta, Corinth, Thespiae, Arcadia,Aegina, and several other Greek city-states combined into an alliance to fight for their survival. They decided to block the Persian advance on land at a narrow pass called Thermopylae, and to block the Persian navy at a narrow straight
paragraphs.zh: 他组织了 10 万名士兵，对希腊发 动了地面入侵，还派出了数百艘船 只支援。 薛西斯的军队非常庞大，以至于在 行军时把整条河流都喝干了。 当他需要穿越达达尼尔海峡时，他 直接用船搭建了一座桥。 为了避开阿陀斯山地区灾难性的 大风，他甚至在半岛上挖了一条运 河！ 雅典、斯巴达、科林斯、特斯匹伊、 阿卡狄亚、埃伊那岛以及其他几个 希腊城邦结为联盟，为他们的生存 而战。 他们决定在一个叫做“温泉关”的 狭窄隘口抵挡波斯军队登陆，并在 一条叫做阿忒米西翁的狭长海峡 阻击波斯海军。

paragraphs.en: calledArtemisium. The Persian fleet was actually led by a very capable female commander namedArtemisia of Caria. So this site here is where the Greeks decided to meet the Persians. Today Thermopylae is a wide plain. But Back in 480 BC, the shoreline was much closer. There was only a 100-meter wide strip of land between the mountains and the ocean. Here at this point, 10,000 Greeks built a short wall, and set up a strong defensive position. Then the battle began. The Persians attacked the Greek line. But the Greek soldiers were better at hand-to-hand combat, and their
paragraphs.zh: 波斯舰队实际上是由一位非常有 能力的女指挥官领导的，她叫做卡 里亚的阿尔特米西亚。 这就是希腊人与波斯人决战的地 方。今天的“温泉关”是一片广阔 的平原。 但在公元前 480 年，海岸线可比现 在近多了。群山和海洋之间只有 100 米宽的陆地。 此时，一万多名希腊人已经筑起了 一道短墙，建立了一个坚固的防御 阵地。 战斗开始了。波斯人向希腊防线发 起进攻。 但是希腊士兵更擅长肉搏战，他们 的阵地位置使得薛西斯无法利用

paragraphs.en: position meant the Xerxes could not surround them and use his superior numbers to his advantage. So for a whole day the Greeks held off an army that was 10-times their size. But then a Greek traitor named Ephialtes told the Persians about a path through the mountains which would allow them to get behind the Greek force. Xerxes sent 20,000 men down that path and the Greeks realized that they would soon be surrounded. At that point the Greek commander, King Leonidas of Sparta, ordered his army to retreat. But he decided to stay behind with 300 Spartans and 1200 other men. He did this to give the rest of the
paragraphs.zh: 数量优势来包围他们。 因此，在整整一天内，希腊人抵挡 住了十倍于他们规模的军队。 但希腊叛徒埃菲亚尔特斯告诉了 波斯人一条穿过山脉的小路，用以 绕到希腊军队的后方。 薛西斯派了两万名士兵沿路出发， 希腊人意识到他们马上就要被包 围了。 希腊军队的指挥官，斯巴达国王列 奥尼达当即下令撤退。 但是为了给大部队争取逃离的时 间，他决定和 300 名斯巴达人以及 1200 名其他士兵留下殿后。

paragraphs.en: army time to escape. This romantic painting shows Leonidas' calm and noble courage while all the other Greeks around him are agitated and excited. Now outnumbered 100 to 1, the Greek rearguard fought ferociously as they were attacked on all sides. The fought and fought until their spears broke, and then they switched to their swords, and when their swords broke they used their hands and their teeth. They fought until every last Greek soldier was dead. Years later, the Greeks erected a monument with an epitaph to honor these men. It stands above their common grave. The message reads: "Go tell the Spartans, thou who
paragraphs.zh: 这幅浪漫主义绘画表现了列奥尼 达的沉着冷静和高尚的勇气，周围 的希腊人则激动不已。 面对着百倍于自身的敌人，腹背受 敌的希腊后卫士兵奋勇迎战。 他们用长矛猛刺，长矛折断了，又 拔出佩剑劈砍，佩剑断了，就冲上 去用拳头和牙齿同敌人肉搏。 他们抗争到了最后一刻，没有一个 人生还。 多年以后，希腊人在他们的公墓上 建立了一座丰碑作为纪念。上面的 碑文写道: “异乡的过客啊，请告诉斯巴达的

paragraphs.en: passest by, That here, obedient to their laws, we lie" The last stand of the Spartans at Thermopylae gave the rest of the Greek army time to get away. But they were still badly outnumbered. The Greeks decided to organize their next defense at the Isthmus of Corinth.Athens was evacuated. The Persian army reachedAthens a few days later. Meanwhile theAthenian fleet retreated to the Bay of Salamis. And then Xerxes kept his promise to his father, and he burnedAthens to the ground. The Greek army fortified the Isthmus of Corinth, while the Greek navy decided to fight it out at the
paragraphs.zh: 人民，我们踏实地履行了诺言，长 眠于此。" 斯巴达人在温泉关的战斗给其他 希腊军队争取到了逃离的时间。但 希腊人的兵力仍然远远落后于对 方。 希腊人决定在科林斯地峡组织他 们的下一次防御。他们疏散了雅典 城。 几天后，波斯军队到达雅典。 同时雅典舰队撤退到了萨拉米斯 湾。 薛西斯遵守了他对父亲的诺言，把 雅典城付之一炬。 希腊陆军加强了科林斯地峡的防 御，而希腊海军则决定在萨拉米斯 岛附近的埃莱夫西纳湾与敌人决

paragraphs.en: Gulf of Elefsina near Salamis Island. Here is a picture of the Corinthian Isthmus. And here is the Gulf of Elefsina near Salamis Island. Xerxes now made his fatal mistake. He planned to destroy the Greek fleet at Salamis and then attack Corinth. But this is exactly what the Greeks wanted him to do. TheAthenianAdmiral Themistocles decided to lure the Persians into a trap. He moved the Greek fleet into the bay of Elefsina and then let the Persians block the entrances. Now the Persians had the Greek fleet trapped and outnumbered them 3 to 1. They felt certain of victory.
paragraphs.zh: 一死战。 这是一张科林斯地峡的照片。 这是萨拉米斯岛附近的埃莱夫西 纳湾。 薛西斯犯了一个致命的错误。他计 划在萨拉米斯击败希腊舰队，然后 再进攻科林斯。 但这正中希腊人的下怀。 雅典舰队指挥官地米斯托克利决 定把波斯人引入陷阱。他命令希腊 舰队转移到埃莱夫西纳湾，并让波 斯人封锁了入口。 现在波斯人把希腊舰队困住了，兵 力也是对方的 3 倍。他们认为胜券 在握。

paragraphs.en: But Themistocles stayed calm and ordered his fleet to assemble in a U-shape.As the Persians approached he told his ships to back up. The Greeks backed up and up and up, until they almost touched the shore... And then they charged forward ramming into the Persian fleet. Suddenly in the cramped space of the bay, the Persians realized that they were unable to use their superior numbers to their advantage. Xerxes sat on a nearby hill watching the battle, so Persian ship captains rushed into the fight eager to impress their emperor. But these incoming ships collided with damaged ships trying to get
paragraphs.zh: 但地米斯托克利非常镇定，他让舰 队排成了 u 型阵列。当波斯人逼近 时，他命令舰队后退。 希腊舰队不停地后撤，几乎要退到 海岸边上了…… 然后他们向前冲锋，撞向波斯舰 队。 在海湾狭窄的空间里，波斯人突然 开始意识到他们无法发挥出自身 数量上的优势。 薛西斯就坐在附近的山上观看战 斗，所以波斯舰队的船长为了给他 留下印象，都急于冲进战场。 但这些接踵而来的战船与前方试 图后撤的受损船只纷纷相撞，这场

paragraphs.en: away, and soon the whole battle turned into a disaster for the Persians. When it was all over the Persians had lost 300 ships, compared to only 40 on the Greek side. Now the Persians no longer had an effective fleet.And without that, Xerxes could not keep his huge army supplied and fed. So the Persians were forced to retreat. Greece had won! The Persian Empire never again invaded Greece. And now,Athens and Sparta had emerged as the clear leaders of Greece, what would they do with this power now that they had it? We will find out next time as we discuss Classical Greece!
paragraphs.zh: 战斗很快变成了波斯人的灾难。 战斗结束时，波斯人损失了 300 艘 船，而希腊方面只损失了 40 艘。 现在波斯人的舰队已经起不了多 大作用了。没有舰队，薛西斯就无 法维持他庞大军队的供给。 所以波斯人被迫撤退。希腊获得了 胜利! 波斯帝国后来再也没有入侵希腊。 现在，雅典和斯巴达在希腊的领导 地位已经形成，他们会如何处理手 中的权力呢？ 我们将在下节关于古希腊的课程 里找到答案！

titleZh: 第十讲：古希腊|雅典与斯巴达的相爱相杀

paragraphs.en: Hello everyone and welcome back to our lecture series on world history. Today we are going to learn about the Classical Greeks. The Classical Era of Greek history mainly covers the years between 500 and 400 BC, here is what that looks like on our timeline. The last Persian armies retreated in 479 BC. The two leading cities of Sparta andAthens had organized a successful resistance and fought them off. But now divisions began to develop between these two cities about what to do next. TheAthenians wanted to carry on the war and liberate the Ionian Greeks who were still under Persian
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。今天我们来了解古希腊人。 古 希 腊 时 期 主 要 处 于 公 元 前 500-400 年之间，这是它在时间轴 上的位置。 波斯军队于公元前 479 年撤退。斯 巴达和雅典成功地领导了抵抗行 动并击退了他们。 但现在，这两个城市在接下来的安 排上出现了分歧。 雅典人想要继续这场战争，解放仍 处于波斯控制下的爱奥尼亚希腊 人。

paragraphs.en: control. The Spartans argued that this was impractical, and that these Ionian Greeks should instead be resettled in Europe. This led to the first disagreements between theAthenians and Spartans following the Persian Wars:Athens wanted more war, and Sparta wanted peace. The reason for this disagreement can be found in the different structure ofAthenian and Spartan society. In the late Dark-Ages and early Archaic period, the Spartans had conquered all of their neighbors and created something of a mini-empire. These defeated neighbors were made into slaves called "Helots"
paragraphs.zh: 斯巴达人认为这是不切实际的，让 这些爱奥尼亚希腊人在欧洲重新 安置就可以了。 这导致了雅典人和斯巴达人在波 斯战争后的第一个分歧：雅典人想 要战争，斯巴达人想要和平。 造成该分歧的主要原因是雅典和 斯巴达的不同社会结构。 在黑暗时代末期和古风时期的初 期，斯巴达人征服了他们所有的邻 邦，建立了一个小型帝国。 这些战败的邻邦成为了奴隶，被称 为“希洛人”。这样一来，每个自

paragraphs.en: Eventually there were about 6 Helot-Slaves for every Free Spartan Citizen. The advantage of this was that the Spartans no longer needed to do any work. The citizens of Sparta became a landed aristocracy, that could lead lives of leisure. But the downside was that the Spartans lived in constant fear of a slave revolt. Since the slaves outnumbered the citizens so greatly, the Spartans knew that a slave rebellion would be very dangerous for them. So rather than do the sensible thing and just end slavery, the Spartan citizens devoted themselves to military training. If a Spartan baby had a birth defect or illness, he or she would be killed
paragraphs.zh: 由的斯巴达公民大约有 6 个奴隶。 这样做的好处是斯巴达人不再需 要做任何工作。斯巴达的市民变成 了地主贵族，过着悠闲的生活。 但不利的一面是，斯巴达人不得不 时刻担忧着奴隶起义。由于奴隶的 数量大大超过了公民，斯巴达人知 道奴隶起义对他们来说是非常危 险的。 但斯巴达人没有明智地结束奴隶 制，而是专注于投身军事训练。 如果一个斯巴达婴儿有先天缺陷 或疾病，他就会在出生时被杀死。

paragraphs.en: at birth. Then the Spartan boys would leave their families at age 7 and spend the next 13 years in military training. When they became men, they would eat in mess-halls with the other members of their regiment, rather than at home with their families. In other words, the Spartans became a warrior culture in which all free men were entirely dedicated to the military. But they did all this, just to maintain slavery! Of course this made the Spartans very good soldiers, but it also made them surprisingly conservative about war. In Sparta there were only about
paragraphs.zh: 斯巴达男孩会在7岁时离开他们的 家庭，在接下来的 13 年里接受军 事训练。 当他们成年后，他们将和军团里的 其他成员在食堂吃饭，而不是和家 人在家里进餐。 换句话说，斯巴达人形成了一种战 士文化，在这种文化中，所有的自 由公民都完全献身于军队当中。 但他们这样做，仅仅为了维持奴隶 制而已！ 当然，这使得每一个斯巴达人都成 为了优秀的战士，但他们对战争却 出奇的保守。 斯巴达只有大约 3 万名男性公民，

paragraphs.en: 30,000 male citizens, compared to about 180,000 slaves. Since the Spartans became warriors to control their slaves, they were very cautious about sacrificing their soldiers in a battle. Because if too many of their soldiers died fighting a foreign enemy, then the Helots could rebel and bring down their whole society. For this reason, the Spartans were eager to end the war with Persia. The Spartan King and council of elders wanted to stop fighting. ButAthens was very different. There were slaves inAthens, but they were just 15% of the population instead of 85% like in Sparta. So theAthenians had less fear of a slave revolt. Additionally,Athens had been a
paragraphs.zh: 而奴隶却达到了 18 万人。 由于斯巴达人成为战士的目的是 控制他们的奴隶，所以他们非常注 意战斗中的牺牲。这是因为如果军 队在与敌人的战斗中伤亡过多，奴 隶们就可能会造反，从而导致社会 秩序的崩溃。 因此，斯巴达人渴望结束与波斯的 战争。斯巴达国王和长老会议希望 停战。 但雅典的情况则完全不同。雅典的 奴隶仅占人口的 15%，而不是斯巴 达的 85%。因此雅典人并不那么担 心奴隶起义。 此外，雅典从公元前 508 年起就是

paragraphs.en: democracy since 508 BC. It was a flawed democracy, only free Athenian men could vote, but it was still a system which responded to public opinion. And public opinion was in favor of protecting the Greeks inAnatolia out of a sense of Greek patriotism. This meant thatAthenian politicians could gain public support and get elected if they favored continuing the war with Persia. This disagreement over Persian policy caused theAthenians and Spartans to break apart in 478 BC. TheAthenians formed a new alliance called the Delian League. And the Spartans formed an alliance called the Peloponnesian League in response.
paragraphs.zh: 民主国家。这个民主制度并不完 善，因为只有自由的雅典人才可以 投票，但它仍然是一个回应民意的 制度。 出于希腊爱国主义，公众舆论支持 保护爱奥尼亚的希腊人。 这意味着雅典的政治家们可以通 过主张继续与波斯作战，来获得民 众的支持。 在波斯政策上的分歧导致雅典人 和斯巴达人在公元前 478 年决裂。 雅典人组建了一个新的联盟，叫做 提洛同盟。 斯巴达人也组建了伯罗奔尼撒同 盟来作为回应。

paragraphs.en: Tensions rose, but the two alliances maintained an uneasy peace for the next 47 years. During the Era of the Delian League, two men ledAthens. The first was a man named Cimon. He had fought bravely at the Battle of Salamis, and then built his political career on his reputation as a war hero. He was the leading politician in Athens from 476 BC until 462 BC. Cimon strongly supported cooperation with Sparta. But in 462 BC, the Spartans had a slave revolt. Cimon sent soldiers to help them, but the Spartans were worried that theAthenians would join the Helots instead, so they sent the soldiers back toAthens.
paragraphs.zh: 虽然紧张局势加剧，但这两个联盟 在接下来的 47 年里一直维持着不 稳定的和平状态。 在提洛同盟时期，雅典经历了两位 领导人。 第一个叫做西门。他曾在萨拉米斯 战役中英勇作战，之后凭借战争英 雄的名声开始了自己的政治生涯。 从公元前 476 年到公元前 462 年， 他都是雅典的主要政治家。西门强 烈支持与斯巴达合作。 在公元前 462 年，斯巴达发生了奴 隶起义。西门派出军队增援，但斯 巴达人担心雅典人会加入希洛人 的阵营，所以将士兵遣返回了雅 典。

paragraphs.en: This insult angered theAthenian public, and it allowed Cimon's political rivals to falsely claim that he was a traitor toAthens and had taken bribes from Sparta. His chief rival, Pericles, then got Cimon exiled fromAthens and became the new leader of the city. Then Pericles ledAthens for the next 32 years from 461 BC until his death in 429 BC. The Delian League was ostensibly organized for the common defense against Persia, so the League gave each of its members a choice. They could either contribute money for buying weapons, or contribute ships. Most of theAllies chose to contribute money, butAthens contributed ships.
paragraphs.zh: 这一侮辱性行为激怒了雅典公众， 也让西门的政治对手们得以污蔑 他是雅典的叛徒，收受了斯巴达的 贿赂。 他的主要竞争对手伯里克利随后 将他放逐出了雅典，并成为雅典的 新领袖。 从公元前 461 年，直到公元前 429 年去世，伯里克利领导雅典长达 32 年。 提洛同盟表面上是为了共同防御 波斯而设立的，所以联盟要求每个 成员要么出资用于购买武器，要么 捐献战船。 大多数盟国选择捐款，雅典则提供 了船只。

paragraphs.en: This system began to change in 454 BC when Pericles moved the League's treasury from Delos to Athens. Now theAthenians would have control over all that money! For many historians this event marks the moment when the Delian League went from being a defensive military alliance to anAthenian Empire. Of course, theAthenians were supposed to use the money from the other cities to pay for their common defense. But of course, now that Pericles had control of the treasury in his own city, the temptation to use it to increase his popularity inAthens was too much for him to resist.
paragraphs.zh: 公元前 454 年，这一制度发生了变 化，伯里克利将联盟的财产从提洛 岛转移到了雅典。 现在雅典人得到了所有钱的控制 权！ 许多历史学家认为，这一事件标志 着提洛同盟从一个防御性的军事 联盟开始向雅典帝国转变。 当然，这笔来自其他城邦的资金本 应让雅典人用来支付共同防御的 费用。 但现在伯里克利控制了财产，用这 笔钱来增加他在雅典的声望，对他 来说是难以抗拒的的诱惑。

paragraphs.en: He spent that money on turning Athens into a glorious city.And his most famous project was rebuilding the temples on top of theAcropolis. From 447 to 438 BC, Pericles had theAcropolis re-built with money from the Delian League that was supposed to be spent on the military. The greatest building that Pericles had constructed, and probably the most famous building in all of Greece, was the Temple toAthena: The Parthenon. Today the Parthenon is a ruin, because the Turks and Venetians accidentally blew it up 1687 while they were fighting a battle with each other. But this shows you what it looked like in 438 BC.
paragraphs.zh: 他用这笔资金把雅典变成了一座 辉煌的城市。其中最著名的工程就 是在卫城顶上重建庙宇。 从公元前 447-438 年，伯里克利用 提洛同盟的资金——这笔原来应 该用于防御经费的钱——重建了 雅典卫城。 伯里克利建造的最伟大的建筑，同 时也大概是全希腊最著名的建筑， 便是这座雅典娜女神的帕台农神 庙。 今天的帕台农神庙是一片废墟，这 是因为土耳其人和威尼斯人在 1687 年的一场战争中不慎将其炸 毁了。 但从图中可以看到它在公元前 438 年的样子。

paragraphs.en: The 32 years when Pericles led Athens are also known as the Athenian GoldenAge. This was the generation whenAthens was at the peak of its political, military and cultural greatness. When the popular culture imagines "Ancient Greece", this is the period that they are usually thinking of. The great artist Phideas lived during this time, and the "Classical style" of Greek sculpture reached a peak under him. Phideas and his students were so influential that artists in the west have been copying them for the last 24 centuries. Here is an imaginative painting of Phideas showing off his Parthenon carvings to his friends.
paragraphs.zh: 伯里克利领导雅典的这 32 年也被 称为雅典的黄金时代。这一代人见 证了雅典政治、军事和文化的鼎盛 时期。 每当大众文化想象“古希腊”时， 脑海中通常都会浮现出这一时期 的景象。 伟大的艺术家菲狄亚斯就生活在 这个时期，希腊雕塑的“古典风格” 在他的带领下达到了顶峰。 菲狄亚斯以及他的学生的影响非 常深远，西方艺术家在过去的 24 个世纪里一直在模仿他们。 这幅极具想象力的画作，描绘了菲 狄亚斯向他的朋友们炫耀他的帕 台农神庙雕刻时的场景。

paragraphs.en: And here are more statues carved either by Phideas or in imitation of his style. This was also the GoldenAge of Greek theater. This ruined auditorium in Delphi should give you some sense of what Greek theaters looked like. The four greatest Greek playwrights:Aeschylus, Sophocles, Euripides andAristophanes all lived and wrote at this time. Here are some of their most famous plays: Oedipus Rex, Medea, Lysistrata, and Prometheus Bound. These plays are still performed today! This time was also a great period of Philosophy. I could teach a whole
paragraphs.zh: 这些雕像均出自于菲狄亚斯以及 他的模仿者之手。 这也是希腊戏剧的黄金时代。这个 位于特尔斐的废弃礼堂应该能让 你对希腊剧院的外观有一些了解。 希腊最伟大的四位剧作家: 埃斯 库罗斯、索福克勒斯、欧里庇得斯 和阿里斯多芬尼斯都生活在这个 时期并进行戏剧创作。 这些是他们最著名的几部戏剧： 《俄狄浦斯王》、《美狄亚》、《吕西 斯特拉忒》和《被缚的普罗米修 斯》。 这些戏剧直到今天还在演出！ 这也是一个属于哲学的伟大时代。 这些希腊哲学家我用一节课都讲

paragraphs.en: class on the Greek philosophers. But here are just a few who lived at this time: There isAnaxagoras who first explained solar eclipses. Democritus, who first proposed the idea of the "atom." Empedocles, who was a vegetarian and theorized that the Universe was made up of 4 elements. Isocrates, who made contributions to the art of rhetoric. And Parmenides, who pondered the nature of reality as did Heraclitus. When people talk about "Ancient Greek philosophers" they are usually referring to this period.
paragraphs.zh: 不完。这里介绍几位生活在这个时 代的人： 阿那克萨哥拉是第一个解释日食 的人。 第一个提出“原子”概念的是德谟 克利特。 恩培多克勒是一位素食主义者，他 提出了宇宙由 4 个元素组成的理 论。 伊苏克拉底对修辞艺术做出了贡 献。 巴门尼德和赫拉克利特一样，对事 物的本质作出了一番思考。 当人们说起“古希腊哲学家”时， 通常指的就是这个时期。

paragraphs.en: This era was also the origin of history-writing. The three great Greek historians of Herodotus, Thucydides and Xenophon all lived at this time. And most of what I've taught you since our lesson on the Neo-Babylonians can be traced back to something these men wrote. They are our primary sources for this period. Unfortunately, this GoldenAge had to come to an end. Eventually the uneasy peace between Athens and Sparta broke down, and the two cities went to war.And since they were the leaders of their respective leagues, they dragged the whole country into an awful civil war which went on for 27 years.
paragraphs.zh: 这个时代也是历史文献的起源。 三位伟大的希腊历史学家希罗多 德，修昔底德和色诺芬都生活在这 个时期。 从关于新巴比伦的课程开始，我讲 的大部分内容都可以追溯到这些 人的作品。它们是这段时期史料的 第一手来源。 遗憾的是，这个黄金时代很快结束 了。雅典和斯巴达之间那不稳定的 和平关系最终破裂，两座城邦之间 爆发了战争。由于他们又领导着各 自的联盟，因此整个希腊被拖入了 一场持续了 27 年的可怕内战。

paragraphs.en: We know about the Peloponnesian war in great detail because of the work of the Great Historian Thucydides who was anAthenian general in the war and an eyewitness to all of it. Also unlike Herodotus, who mixed rumors, legends, and magic in with his history, Thucydides was extremely careful, about verifying his facts and searching for human explanations of events not supernatural ones. For this reason, Thucydides is known as the "Father of Scientific History" During the war, thousands of people crowded intoAthens for safety.And in these crowded, unhygienic conditions a plague broke out. We still don't know the pathogen which
paragraphs.zh: 伟大的历史学家修昔底德的工作 让我们得以对伯罗奔尼撒战争有 非常详细的了解。他是雅典的一位 将领，也是整场战争的目击者。 修昔底德和希罗多德不同，他没有 将谣言、传说和魔法混在自己的历 史作品中，而是非常谨慎地验证文 字的真实性，从人类自身而不是超 自然力量中寻找对事件的解释。 因此，修昔底德被称为“科学史学 之父”。 战争期间，成千上万的人为了躲避 战乱而涌入雅典。在这拥挤且不卫 生的环境里，一场瘟疫爆发了。我 们不知道这是由什么病原体引起 的，但这场灾难夺去了雅典大约一

paragraphs.en: caused it, but it was terrible for Athens and killed about half of the population, including Pericles himself. The plague robbedAthens of any chance of a quick victory, and turned the war into an awful 27-year long war of attrition. Towards the end of the fighting, the armies were made up of many soldiers who had not even been born when the war began. Eventually Sparta won andAthens surrendered unconditionally. Sparta's allies of Corinth and Thebes wanted to seeAthens utterly destroyed and its population enslaved. But Sparta refused to be so merciless to its old ally during the
paragraphs.zh: 半的人口的性命，包括伯里克利本 人。 这场瘟疫让雅典失去了迅速取得 胜利的机会，并将这场战争变成了 长达 27 年的可怕消耗战。 等到战争接近尾声时，军队中的许 多士兵甚至是战争开始后才出生 的。 最终斯巴达获得胜利，雅典宣布无 条件投降。 斯巴达的盟友科林斯和底比斯想 要彻底摧毁雅典并奴役其人民。 但在斯巴达并没有对它在波斯战 争期间的老盟友如此无情。

paragraphs.en: Persian Wars. Instead they strippedAthens of its overseas possessions, its fleet and its walls.And then they declared thatAthens "Was to have the same friends and the same enemies as Sparta." Afew slides ago, when I mentioned the Greek philosophers I left one out: Socrates. Socrates was born in 470 BC, so he lived during the Golden Age of Athens and the Peloponnesian War. He even fought as a soldier early in the war. The problem was that Socrates was a relentless social critic, and one of his students was a man named Alcibiades. Alcibiades had been the main
paragraphs.zh: 他们只是剥夺了雅典的海外财产、 舰队，并拆毁了其城墙。然后他们 宣布，雅典“和斯巴达有同样的朋 友和敌人”。 我在之前讲到希腊哲学家时，漏掉 了一个人：苏格拉底。 苏格拉底出生于公元前 470 年，所 以他生活在雅典的黄金时代和伯 罗奔尼撒战争期间。在战争早期， 他甚至以士兵的身份参战。 问题在于，苏格拉底是一个无情的 社会批评家，同时他有一个学生叫 做亚西比德。 在公元前 413 年，亚西比德策划了

paragraphs.en: politician responsible for a plan to invade Sicily in 413 BC. This invasion failed andAthens suffered its most disastrous defeat in the war, losing 200 ships and thousands of soldiers at a single stroke. This failure madeAlcibiades hated inAthens. But theAthenians could do nothing to him, because he died in 404 BC, the last year of the war. So after the war was over the Athenians were bitter and Alcibiades' many enemies were looking for people to blame. They focused onAlcibiades' teacher: Socrates. This anger overAlcibiades combined with Socrates' constant social criticism had made the philosopher many enemies in
