paragraphs.zh: 这就是“得不偿失的胜利”一词的 起源。 得不偿失的胜利是指你赢得了战 斗，却失去了太多的士兵，所以这 几乎等同于失败。 更宽泛的意思就是你实现了目标， 却付出了过多的代价，因此得不偿 失。 皮拉斯离开意大利后，罗马征服了 南部和北部的最后几个伊特鲁里 亚城邦。 到公元前 264 年，罗马控制了意大 利阿尔诺河以南的整个地区。 罗马人已经征服了意大利北部的 大部分地区，于是他们第一次把目 光投向了海外。他们的下一个目标 自然是西西里岛。

paragraphs.en: Sicily. There was only one problem: Sicily was already partially occupied by the other major power in the Western Mediterranean: Carthage. Rome and Carthage were moving towards war. Please join me next time as I tell you that story. Thank you very much! I hope you enjoyed that lesson and want to learn more about ancient Rome.So I have some recommendations for you. There is the Cartoon History of the Universe.Book 2 by Larry Gonick.The third part of the book is all about Rome. There is the History of Rome podcast by Mike Duncan.He goes into great detail about the history,all
paragraphs.zh: 现在只剩下一个问题：地中海西部 的另一个大国：迦太基部分占领了 西西里岛。 罗马和迦太基正走向战争。欲知后 事如何，请看下节课分解。 谢谢大家！ 希望你能喜欢这节课，我还有更多 的资料推荐给你们。 这是拉里·高尼克的《漫画宇宙 史·第二册》。书的第三部分是关 于罗马的。 还有迈克·邓肯的《罗马历史》播 客。他详细地讲述了从罗穆卢斯到 最后一个罗马皇帝倒台的这段历

paragraphs.en: the way from Romulus until the fall of the last Roman Emperor. There is SPQR by Mary Beard,one of the top Roman Historians today. And if you want to learn more about Rome’s legendary origins,you can read theAeneid by Virgil. Okay,that’s all for now!Thank you very much!
paragraphs.zh: 史。 还有玛丽·比尔德的《SPQR》，她 是当代最杰出的罗马历史学家之 一。 如果你想了解更多关于罗马传说 的起源，你可以阅读维吉尔《埃涅 伊德》。 好的，今天就到这里吧！非常感谢！

titleZh: 第十六讲：战争与奴隶制|摧毁迦太基！驱逐自由

paragraphs.en: Hello everyone! Welcome back to our lecture series on world history. Today we will learn about how Rome became an Empire and the downsides of that change. In this lesson, we will be focusing on the years between 264 and 144 BC. By 264 BC, most of the Italian Peninsula was under Roman control. So they began to look overseas for the first time in their history. Their first and most obvious objective would be the Island of Sicily. But Sicily was already occupied. Half of the island was settled by
paragraphs.zh: 大家好！欢迎回到我们的世界历史 系列课程。 今天我们要学习的是罗马是如何成 长为帝国以及这种变化所带来的副 作用。 在这节课中，我们将把目光转向公 元前 264-144 年这段时间。 到了公元前 264 年，罗马人已经统 治了大部分的意大利半岛。 所以他们在历史上首次将目光投向 海外。 他们的第一个、也是最显然的目标 是西西里岛。 然而彼时的西西里岛已经被占领 了。岛的一半被希腊人占领，另一

paragraphs.en: Greek colonists, and the other half was occupied by Carthage. The Carthaginians has started off as a Phoenician colony. In fact, the city was founded in 814 BC by Phoenician refugees who were fleeing theAssyrians. Because the Phoenicians were such good sailors, Carthage quickly became the dominant naval and trading power in the Western Mediterranean. And by 264 BC, the Carthaginians controlled the northAfrican coast, southern Spain, the Island of Sardinia, parts of the Islands of Sicily and Corsica, and all of the small islands in that part of the Mediterranean. Now Rome began to challenge the power of Carthage, and the two
paragraphs.zh: 半被迦太基人占领。 迦太基起初是腓尼基人的殖民地。 事实上，这座城市是逃离亚述的腓 尼基难民于公元前 814 年建立而成 的。 由于腓尼基人是如此优秀的水手， 迦太基得以迅速成长为西地中海地 区的海军强国和贸易大国。 到了公元前 264 年，迦太基人控制 着北非海岸、西班牙南部、撒丁岛、 部分西西里岛和科西嘉群岛，以及 地中海地区所有的小岛屿。 现在，罗马人开始挑战迦太基人的 权威，于是两个国家开战了。

paragraphs.en: countries began to fight. The Romans called the Carthaginians "Punic", which has the same root as the word "Phoenician" . So these wars between Rome and Carthage are known as the Punic Wars. The first Punic War was a difficult 23-year long struggle for control of Sicily. Eventually the Carthaginians became exhausted and gave up, giving Rome control of the Island. The 1st Punic war ended in 241 BC. Before the first Punic war, Carthage controlled the Islands of Sicily, Corsica and Sardinia. After the 1st Punic War, the Romans controlled those islands. For the next 23 years, the
paragraphs.zh: 罗马人称迦太基人为“布匿”，“布 匿”与“腓尼基人”词根相同。 罗马与迦太基之间发生的战争就是 著名的布匿战争。 第一次布匿战争是为争夺西西里岛 控制权而进行的长达 23 年的艰难 斗争。 最终，迦太基人精疲力竭， 不得不放弃控制权，罗马人获胜。 第一次布匿战争于公元前 241 年结 束。 第一次布匿战争前，迦太基控制着 西西里岛、科西嘉岛和撒丁岛。 第一次布匿战争后，罗马人占领了 这些岛屿。 在接下来的 23 年里，罗马与迦太基

paragraphs.en: relationship between Rome and Carthage was tense: the two powers deeply distrusted each other. The King of Carthage even made his son Hannibal swear a sacred oath that he "Would never be a friend of Rome." Unfortunately for the Romans, Hannibal was a military genius. He is probably one of the 10-greatest generals in all of human history. And now he began a military campaign that took the Romans by surprise. He organized an army of 40,000 soldiers and 38 war elephants in New Carthage, Spain and then began his march towards Rome in the spring of 218 BC. By September he had reached the
paragraphs.zh: 的关系十分紧张：两个大国对彼此 都充满了不信任。 迦太基国王甚至让他的儿子汉尼拔 郑重宣誓“永远不与罗马做朋友”。 对罗马人来说不幸的是，汉尼拔是 军事奇才。他可能是人类历史上最 伟大的十名将军之一。 汉尼拔发起了一场让罗马人出其不 意的军事行动。 公元前 218 年春，汉尼拔在西班牙 的新迦太基城组织起一支拥有 40000 名士兵与 38 头战象的军队， 然后率领军队向罗马进击。 同年九月，汉尼拔到达了罗讷河，

paragraphs.en: Rhone river, so he moved north until he came to the foothills of the Alps. And then Hannibal did something that the Roman thought impossible. He crossed theAlps with his war elephants in October of 218 BC. The crossing was tough, and Hannibal lost thousands of men doing it, but once he reached northern Italy, thousands of Gauls joined his army. The panicked Romans sent an army north to stop him. They met at the Battle of the Trebia in December 218 BC. But Hannibal used his superior generalship to beat the Romans in battle. After routing their cavalry, he
paragraphs.zh: 接下来他向北移动来到阿尔卑斯山 山脚。 汉尼拔接下来做了一件在罗马人看 来绝无可能的事情——公元前 218 年 10 月，他带领战象翻越了阿尔卑 斯山。 翻越山岭十分艰难，汉尼拔损兵折 将数千人，但当他最终抵达意大利 北部的时候，数千高卢人加入了军 队。 罗马人惊慌不已，急忙向北派军队 去阻止他。 两军于公元前 218 年 12 月在特雷比亚交战，即特雷比亚 战役。 这一次，汉尼拔用卓越的军事能力 击败了罗马人。 他还一路跟踪罗马人的骑兵，借此

paragraphs.en: attacked the Roman infantry and drove them into the river. It was a complete defeat for the Romans. The beginning of winter forced both sides to wait for a few months. Hannibal spent the winter camped out with the Gauls of Northern Italy. The next summer, the Romans sent another army out to stop Hannibal. They met in a battle on June 21st, 217 BC next to a lake called Trasimene. But once again, Hannibal caught the Romans by surprise and used his superior tactics to destroy another Roman army. Then Hannibal moved into southern Rome.
paragraphs.zh: 袭击了罗马步兵，让他们葬身河水。 这对于罗马人来说，是个实打实的 败仗。 入冬了，严寒迫使两支军队休战数 月。在此期间，汉尼拔军队与高卢 人在意大利北部扎营休整。 次年夏天，罗马人又派遣了一支军 队去阻击汉尼拔。 两军于公元前217年6月21日在特 拉西米恩湖附近交战。 然而，汉尼拔又一次以卓越的军事 才能打败了罗马军队，让罗马人大 吃一惊。 汉尼拔接下来向罗马南部进攻。

paragraphs.en: Finally, in the spring of 216 BC, the Romans sent all of their available forces east in another attempt to stop Hannibal. The met in battle near the town of Cannae. Even though Hannibal was outnumbered, he managed to outmaneuver the Romans on the battle field. And then he surrounded the Romans. Now the Romans were trapped in a giant mob, with the soldiers on the inside unable to fight or even move. For the next several hours Hannibal slaughtered the Romans. Though Hannibal lost 8000 men, the Romans lost 80,000. Hannibal had completely destroyed their army. But Hannibal still was not strong
paragraphs.zh: 最后，在公元前 216 年春天，罗马 人将所有有生力量派往东部企图再 次阻止汉尼拔。双方于坎尼镇附近 交战。 尽管汉尼拔以少敌多，但在战场上 他还是成功打败了罗马人。 接下来，汉尼拔包围了罗马。 罗马人被庞大数量的对手包围，圈 中的士兵无法作战，甚至无法移动。 接下来的几个小时中，汉尼拔屠杀 了罗马人。 汉尼拔损失了 8000 人，罗马损失了 80000 人。汉尼拔大胜罗马军队。 但是汉尼拔仍然没有足够的实力直

paragraphs.en: enough to attack Rome directly; its walls were too strong. So he needed to get the other Italian cities to join him. Meanwhile, after the disaster at Cannae, the terrified Romans elected a man named Quintus Fabius Verrucosus as their dictator. Fabius decided that they could not face Hannibal in any more battles, and must wear him down over the long-term. So a 9-year stalemate began on the Italian Peninsula. This is the origin of the term "Fabian Strategy" when you try to achieve a goal gradually, rather than all at once. Eventually the stalemate was broken when Rome finally got a
paragraphs.zh: 接攻打罗马；罗马的城墙太厚实了。 所以他需要争取另一个意大利城市 的支持。 同时，被坎尼之难吓坏了的罗马人 挑选昆塔斯·费边·威卢克斯作为 主将。 费边决定不正面与汉尼拔斗争，而 是采取持久战消耗敌方力量。 由此，一场长达 9 年的僵局在意大 利半岛开始了。 这一战略就是术语“费边战略”的 起源，意指循序渐进实现目标，而 不是想要一蹴而就。 直到罗马人终于找到了可以媲美汉 尼拔的将领：西比奥·阿非利加努

paragraphs.en: commander who was able to match Hannibal: ScipioAfricanus. Scipio was only 25 years old when he took command of Roman forces in 211 BC. He decided to go on the offensive and take the war out of Italy. He led a daring assault on New Carthage in 209 BC and captured the city. And then he launched an attack on Carthage itself in 204 BC. This attack on his homeland forced Hannibal to leave Italy, and the two armies met in the Battle of Zama in 202 BC. Zama was Hannibal's last battle.As in earlier fights, he had war elephants with him. So the battle began with an elephant charge.
paragraphs.zh: 斯，这一僵局才被打破。 当西比奥于公元前 211 年接过罗马 军队指挥权的时候，他年仅 25 岁。 他决定转守为攻并将战场转移出意 大利。公元前 209 年，他大胆出击 新迦太基城，并成功占领了这座城 市。 公元前 204 年，他对迦太基城发动 攻击。 后院失火，汉尼拔不得不离开意大 利，回程路上，公元前 202 年，两 军在扎马交战。 扎马之战是汉尼拔所打的最后一 战。和之前的战争一样，他有战象。 所以这场战斗由战象当先锋。

paragraphs.en: But unlike horses, charging elephants cannot turn. So Scipio's men made a path for them through the ranks, and then they killed the poor beast with spears once they reached the rear of the Roman line. By this point in the war, the Roman cavalry was better than the Carthaginian cavalry and they drove off Hannibal's horsemen. And then the Roman cavalry turned and attacked the Carthaginian infantry from behind. After the defeat at Zama, Carthage gave up. Carthage was allowed to continue as an independent country. But she had to give up her navy and all of her overseas territory to Rome. This map shows you the
paragraphs.zh: 但是和马不一样，冲锋的战象不能 转身。所以西比奥的战士们列队为 大象空出一条路，当大象冲进罗马 军队后方时就用长矛刺死它。 这样一来，罗马骑兵比迦太基骑兵 更有优势，他们击退了汉尼拔的骑 兵。 然后罗马骑兵转身从后面袭击了迦 太基步兵。 扎马一战后，迦太基投降了。 迦太基仍然是一个独立的国家，但 不得不对罗马交出海军力量和所有 的海外领土。 这张地图显示了战前的地中海格

paragraphs.en: Mediterranean before the war. And here it is after the war. Then the Romans turned their attention east, and from 172 until 146 BC, they fought a series of wars that gave them control of Greece and Macedonia. Meanwhile, some Roman leaders thought that ScipioAfricanus had been too merciful to Carthage. One Roman Senator named "Cato the Elder" even ended all of his speeches by saying "Cartago Delenda Est" which means "Carthage must be destroyed!" So 55 years after the end of the last war, the Romans found an excuse to start a third war with Carthage and attacked the city in 149 BC. This time it wasn't a fair fight; the
paragraphs.zh: 局。 而这是战后的地图。 然后，罗马人将注意力转向东方， 公元前 172-146 年间，他们打了一 系列战争，占领了希腊和马其顿王 国。 与此同时，一些罗马领导者认为西 比奥对迦太基过于仁慈了。 一位名叫“老加图”的罗马参议员 甚至以“Cartago Delenda Est”作 为演讲结束语，即“迦太基必须被 摧毁！” 所以在最后一战结束 55 年后，罗马 人找借口挑起了与迦太基的第三次 战争，并于公元前 149 年攻击了迦 太基城。 这一次战争并不公平；迦太基人甚

paragraphs.en: Carthaginians didn't even have a proper army anymore. After a three-year siege, the Romans broke through the city, and desperate house to house fighting began. The Carthaginian people were either killed or enslaved, and their city was burned to the ground. In 146 BC, Cato's demand came true, and Carthage was destroyed. Now Roman power was undisputed. Look at the change: In 260 BC, around the beginning of the first Punic War, Rome only controlled the Italian Peninsula. But four generations later, in 130 BC, they ruled most of the Mediterranean.
paragraphs.zh: 至没有像样的军队。 经过三年的围攻，罗马人突破了城 墙，恐怖的巷战开始了。 迦太基人要么被杀，要么被奴役， 他们的城市也被烧毁了。 公元前 146 年，加图的要求实现了， 迦太基被摧毁了。 现在，罗马的实力是不容置疑的。 看看这一变化。公元前 260 年，第 一次布匿战争开始前后，罗马仅仅 控制着意大利半岛。 到了第四代后，公元前 130 年，罗 马人统治着几乎整个地中海地区。

paragraphs.en: As you can imagine, these conquests brought an enormous amount of wealth into Rome. In ancient warfare, defeating another people meant you could steal all of their valuable things. And that's what the Romans did. As Rome conquered more and more land, all of the treasures of the Mediterranean flowed back to the city as plunder. The leaders of those successful wars became very rich men. The upper classes grew wealthier and wealthier from this plunder, and they purchased very large farms. But at the same time, many middle class Romans were getting poorer and poorer, especially in the Italian countryside.
paragraphs.zh: 正如你能想象的，这些征服为罗马 带来了大量的财富。 在古代战争中，击败一个民族意味 着你可以拿走他们所有值钱的东 西。 而罗马人正是这么做的。 随着罗马征服越来越多的土地，地 中海所有的珍宝被掠夺到罗马城。 打了胜仗的领导者都变得非常富 有。 上流阶层从掠夺中变得越来越富 有，于是他们购买了非常大的农场。 与此同时，中间阶层的罗马人变得 越来越穷，尤其是意大利郊区的人。

paragraphs.en: This is how it happened. Before Rome became an empire, the majority of Romans were small-scale farmers. But when the wars happened, many of the men were called away for military service, leaving their wives and children behind. But these wars could go on for years.And soldiers were only paid in plunder, not regular wages. So their farms and businesses back in Italy began to have financial problems without the husbands and fathers around to help. To make ends meet, these poor families often borrowed money from their richer neighbors. As the wars went on, the middle class got deeper and deeper into
paragraphs.zh: 事情就是这样发生的。在罗马成为 帝国之前，大多数罗马人都是生产 规模小的农民。 但是当战争打响后，许多农民离开 妻儿，应召入伍。 战争有可能绵延数年。而士兵的报 酬只有掠夺所得，并不按时发放工 资。 因此，缺少丈夫和父亲的意大利农 场生计开始出现财务问题。 为了维持生计，这些贫困家庭经常 从富裕的邻居那里借钱。 战争持续不断，中间阶层的人也在 债务中越陷越深。

paragraphs.en: debt. And if they couldn't pay their debts, the rich took their land as repayment. In Italy, this created one class of huge land-owners, and another giant class of the landless poor. But this situation was made even worse by another consequence of Empire. That second consequence was slavery. When the Romans conquered the Mediterranean, they enslaved hundreds of thousands of people. In fact, the Romans even had a practice called "Passing Under the Roman Yoke" by which defeated enemies were stripped of their clothes and made to walk under a
paragraphs.zh: 如果他们无法偿还借款，富人将拿 走他们的土地作为偿款。 于是在意大利形成了两类人，一类 是拥有大量土地的富人，另一类是 没有土地的穷人。 这种情况在帝国引发的另一后果作 用下愈加糟糕。 帝国产生的第二个后果就是奴隶 制。 当罗马人征服地中海时，他们俘虏 了成千上万的人为奴。 事实上，罗马人甚至还有一种名为 “在枷锁下穿行”的刑罚：落的敌 人被剥了衣服，在枷锁下行走，以 此表明他们屈服于罗马。

paragraphs.en: yoke, in order to show their submission to Rome. And these hundreds of thousands of captive people were sent back to Rome as slaves. And there they were sold by the thousands at slave markets. The slaves worked all sorts of jobs. Some of the slaves were domestic slaves who helped with household chores like grinding grain and baking bread. They might do work around the house as domestic servants for the rich. If a slave was very unlucky, they would be sent to work in a mine, which was basically a death sentence. Or they might do construction work.
paragraphs.zh: 这成千上万的俘虏作为奴隶被送回 罗马。 在奴隶市场，一大批奴隶被卖掉。 奴隶们从事各种各样的工作。一些 奴隶是家庭奴隶，他们帮助做家务， 比如磨谷子和烤面包。 他们作为富人的家庭佣人，在主人 的屋子周围做一些家务活。 如果非常不走运，奴隶们将被派往 矿井工作，这基本上是死刑。 或者他们也可能做一些建造工作。

paragraphs.en: We know about Roman slaves, because the Romans were not shy about discussing their slaves in their writing, or about depicting them in art. Here is a stone carving of a soldier leading two slaves in chain. These men are probably prisoners of war. Here is a Roman mosaic of two slaves serving wine to two boys. Here are two female domestic slaves, helping their mistress with her make-up and hair. But one of the biggest jobs for slaves in Italy was working on the farms of the rich. The rich ran their farms on slave labor. Perhaps 40% of Italy's population were slaves by 140 BC. Most of them were agricultural
paragraphs.zh: 我们知道罗马奴隶，是因为罗马人 并不羞于在文章中讨论他们的奴 隶，或是在艺术中描绘奴隶。 这是一个雕刻着士兵给两个奴隶上 镣铐的石头。 这些人可能是战俘。 这是两个奴隶为两个男孩倒酒的罗 马式马赛克。 这里展示了两各女家庭奴隶帮女主 人梳妆打扮的场景。 但是意大利的奴隶最主要的工作之 一是在富人的农场里干活。 富人们依靠奴隶耕种农场。到了公 元前 140 年，意大利 40％的人口可 能是奴隶。他们大多数是农场的农 工。

paragraphs.en: workers on farms. And about half of all the slaves were owned by just the top 1% of Roman society. This created a problem for the Middle Class. Remember all those small farmers who lost their land to the rich? Now they couldn't even work for those rich farmers, because the slaves were doing all the jobs. Slavery and Empire destroyed the Roman Middle Class. So these dispossessed Roman farmers moved to the City of Rome looking for work. There they formed a huge underclass called the proletariat, who had no property and often no
paragraphs.zh: 大约一半的奴隶都归罗马社会的前 1％的人所有。 这给中间阶层的人们带来了一个问 题。 还记得那些将土地赔付给富人的小 农吗？ 现在，他们甚至不能为富人工作， 因为有奴隶去做所有的工作。 奴隶制和帝国摧毁了罗马的中间阶 层。 因此，这些无法维持生计的罗马农 民进入罗马城市寻找工作。 在那里，他们组成了一个庞大的下 层阶级：无产阶级，他们没有财产， 也没有工作。

paragraphs.en: jobs. But the destruction of the Italian Middle Class created yet another problem. When Rome was a small city, you had to own land in order to serve in the army. But by the end of the Punic wars, only 2000 families in Italy owned enough land to qualify for military service. Ironically, Just as Rome needed a bigger army than ever to defend its new empire, it was running out of land-owners to fill it! The best source for this time period is the Roman writer Plutarch. Here is how he describes the situation: "Then the poor, who had been ejected from their land, no longer
paragraphs.zh: 但对意大利中间阶层的破坏又带来 了另一个问题。 当罗马还是一个小城市的时候，你 必须拥有土地才能服兵役。 但到布匿战争结束时，意大利只有 2000 个家庭拥有足够的土地，有资 格服兵役。 讽刺的是，正当罗马需要一支比以 往任何时候都大的军队来保卫新帝 国时，却没有足够的有资格的土地 所有者去加入它! 这个时期最好的资料源自罗马作家 蒲鲁塔克。他是这样描述这种情况 的: “穷人被驱逐出自己的土地，他们 不再渴望入伍，他们忽略了孩子的

paragraphs.en: showed themselves eager for military service, and neglected the bringing up of children, so that soon all Italy was conscious of a dearth of freemen, and was filled with gangs of foreign slaves, by whose aid the rich cultivated their estates, from which they had driven away the free citizens." There were some people who understood this problem and saw that Rome needed reform. The most important of these leaders were the brothers Tiberius and Gaius Gracchus, who were the sons of a remarkable woman named Cornelia, and the grandsons of ScipioAfricanus himself. They were trying to solve the problems brought on by empire. But, when they did, they came up against powerful people who would do anything to
paragraphs.zh: 抚养，所以很快地，整个意大利意 识到自由公民的缺乏。外国奴隶帮 助富人耕种庄园，驱逐了自由的公 民。” 有一些人发现了这个问题，认为罗 马亟需改革。 其中最重要的人物就是盖尤斯和提 比略·格拉古斯兄弟。他们的母亲 是一位伟大的女性，名曰科妮莉亚， 而他们同时也是第二次布匿战争中 的英雄西比奥·阿非利加努斯的亲 外孙。他们试图解决帝国带来的种 种问题。不过，与此同时，他们也 将遇到一群权势滔天的人所带来的 阻力，这些人将不惜一切代价阻止 他们的改革。

paragraphs.en: stop them. So Join me next time as we learn about this crisis and how it brought down the Roman Republic. Thank you very much! I hope you enjoyed this lesson and would like to learn more. I highly recommend Dan Carlin's podcast Hardcore history and his series on the Punic Wars called "Punic Nightmares". Mike Duncan's book The Storm Before the Storm also does a thorough job examining this period. Thank you all very much!
paragraphs.zh: 下节课，我们就来看看这场危机以 及它是如何终结了罗马共和国的。 十分感谢！ 我希望你喜欢这一课，并且想学习 更多。 我强烈推荐丹·卡林的播客《硬核 历史》以及他关于布匿战争的系列 节目《布匿噩梦》。 迈克·邓肯的《暴风雨前的暴风雨》 一书也对这一时期进行了全面的解 读。 十分感谢！

paragraphs.en: Lecutre 17:Fall of Hello everyone and welcome back to our lecture series on world history. Today we will learn about the fall of the Roman Republic. This lesson will focus on the years between 144 and 78 BC. As we learned last time, the plunder and slaves that Rome gained from becoming an empire mostly benefitted the rich. While middle class Romans often found their farms and businesses ruined by having their men away for many years in the army. This problem combined with slavery to destroy the Roman middle class. Aside for the social injustice of this situation, it was also a national
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到我们的世界历史 系列讲座。今天我们要学习的是罗 马共和国的陨落。 这节课，让我们把目光转向公元前 144——78 年这一时期。 正如我们上节课讲到的，在罗马成 长为帝国的过程中，战争掠夺和奴 隶的主要受益者都是富人。而中间 阶层的罗马人由于男人们的长期入 伍，导致农场生计无法维持。 这个问题与奴隶制相结合，摧毁了 罗马的中间阶层人群。 这不仅是社会不公，也是一个国家 安全的问题，因为只有拥有土地的

paragraphs.en: security problem, since you needed to be a land-owner to serve in the army. But there were reformers who understood this problem, and the most prominent of them was Tiberius Gracchus. Tiberius and his brother Gaius were the grandsons of ScipioAfricanus through their mother Cornelia, who was one of the most respected and admired women in all of Rome. CorneliaAfricana was really a remarkable person. She was the daughter of the man who saved Rome during the Punic wars and the mother of Rome's greatest reformers. It is said that after her husband died, she even turned down a marriage proposal from the King of Egypt.
paragraphs.zh: 人才有资格入伍。 幸而，有改革者意识到了这一问题， 这其中的佼佼者就是提比略·格拉 古。 提比略的弟弟是盖尤斯，母亲是科 妮莉亚，外祖父是西比奥·阿非利 加努斯，科妮莉亚是罗马最受尊崇 的女性之一。 科妮莉亚·阿非利加努斯实在是个 了不起的人。她的父亲是在布匿战 争中拯救罗马的人，她的两个儿子 是罗马最伟大的改革者。 据说在她丈夫死后，她甚至拒绝了 埃及国王的求婚。

paragraphs.en: And given the courage and integrity of her sons, she must have been a phenomenal mother. Once a friend asked Cornelia why she dressed very simply and did not wear jewelry, since that was the usual style for rich Roman women. To answer her friend, Cornelia turned, pointed at her sons Tiberius and Gaius and said: "Here are my jewels." When Cornelia's oldest son Tiberius grew up, he recognized the National Security problem created by destroying Rome's middle class. But the richest families in Rome and the Senate was getting even richer off of these policies and they wanted no change. The problem was that the rich
paragraphs.zh: 考虑到她儿子们的勇敢正直，想必 她也是一个非凡的母亲。 有一位朋友问科妮莉亚为什么她穿 得那么简单朴素，不像别的罗马富 有女性一样穿金戴银。 科妮莉亚指着自己的儿子提比略和 盖尤斯回答道：“这就是我的珠宝首 饰。” 当科妮莉亚的长子提比略长大后， 他意识到破坏罗马中产阶级所造成 的国家安全问题。 但罗马最富有的人和元老院正凭这 些政策不断敛财，所以他们不想改 变。 问题是，这些富人们控制着政府。

paragraphs.en: controlled the government. When there was an election all the male Roman citizens who wanted to vote would go to the Field of Mars in the northwest corner of the city. There were different types of assemblies to elect different government officials. Two of the most important were theAssembly of the Centuries and theAssembly of the Tribes. The Senate and the Consuls were elected by theAssembly of the Centuries, and in that assembly the rich got more votes than the poor. In fact, the richest 5% of Roman citizens got the majority of the votes. So the Senate and Consuls would always favor the interests of the
paragraphs.zh: 选举时，所有想去投票的男性罗马 公民都要前往罗马城西北角的战神 广场。 选举不同的政府官员采用有不同类 型的集会。其中最重要的两个集会 是百人团大会和部族大会。 元老院和执政官由百人团大会选举 产生，在这种集会中，富人获得的 选票多于穷人。 事实上，前 5%的富人获得了大多数 的选票。 所以元老院和执政官会一直为富人 们谋求利益。

paragraphs.en: rich. But there was anotherAssembly called theAssembly of the Tribes, which was more fair. In that Assembly every man got just 1 vote. So the poor could control that Assembly. ThisAssembly elected the office of "Tribune of the People". The Tribune of the people had a special power called the "Veto" that allowed him to block the passage of any law. Tiberius realized that he could use THIS office as a power base for his reforms. So even though Tiberius came from the Roman nobility, he ran for office as a champion of the poor and middle classes.
paragraphs.zh: 而另一种集会——部族大会相对而 言更加公正。在这一集会中，每人 只有一张选票。所以穷人可以控制 这一集会。 通过这一集会选出来的人被称为 “平民保民官”。 平民保民官有一项特殊的权利：否 决权，可以用来反对任一法律通过。 提比略意识到他可以利用这一职位 作为改革的权力基础。 就这样，尽管提比略出身罗马贵族， 但他以穷人和中间阶层的支持者身 份竞选保民官一职。

paragraphs.en: During the campaign he gave speeches like this: "The wild beasts that roam over Italy have their dens, each has a place of repose and refuge. But the men who fight and die for Italy enjoy nothing but the air and light; without house or home they wander about with their wives and children." And those poor and middle-class voters were deeply moved by seeing this young, well-spoken member of the nobility fighting for them. So they voted overwhelmingly for Tiberius in the election of 133 BC. Once in power, Tiberius proposed a land-reform law. No Italian farm would be allowed to be bigger than 125 hectares.
paragraphs.zh: 他作了以下的演讲： “在意大利游荡的野兽有自己的窝 点，有休息和避难的地方。但为意 大利或战或亡的人，只有空气和光； 没有房子，没有家庭，只能与妻儿 一起游荡。” 那些穷人和中间阶层的投票人被这 个年轻的、雄辩的、虽出身贵族但 选择为他们抗争的人深深打动了。 公元前 133 年的选举中，他们纷纷 将选票投给了提比略。 刚走马上任，提比略就提出了土地 改革法。 所有意大利的农场都不得超过 125 公顷。

paragraphs.en: Any land-owners who owned more than that much would have their extra land confiscated by the government, but with compensation. Then that land would be divided- into small 8-hectare plots and given to the poor. In response this, two political parties developed. The "Populares" were led by Tiberius and favored reform. The "Optimates" opposed reform and controlled the Senate. The land reform proposal made the rich senators very angry because Tiberius was threatening their wealth with his reforms. That year, in 133 BC there were several months of intense political struggle between the Optimates and
paragraphs.zh: 超出的土地将被政府没收，并予以 补偿。 收缴的土地会被划分成一个个 8 公 顷的土块，并分给穷人。 基于此，形成了两个派别。由提比 略领导的“民众派”支持改革。 另一边，以元老院为首的“贵族派” 反对改革。 土地改革提案让富有的执政官非常 愤怒，因为提比略的改革中威胁到 了他们的财富。 公元前 133 年那一年，贵族和民众 之间进行了数月激烈的政治斗争。

paragraphs.en: the Populares. Tiberius used his veto power to bring the Roman government to a standstill. No normal business could get done, because Tiberius would veto it. Nobody had ever used the powers of the Tribune in this aggressive and innovative way, and it infuriated the Senate and wealthy classes. Often the Tribune was from the Middle class and was intimidated by the rich Senators. But Tiberius was from the most noble of all Roman families, and so he was not intimidated by the other members of his social class. Eventually the Senators decided to stop Tiberius by any means necessary. So they organized a mob and beat him to death on the streets
paragraphs.zh: 提比略利用他的否决权使罗马政府 陷入停滞。日常业务无法开展，因 为提比略会进行否决。 此前从没人以这种霸道又创新的方 式动用保民官的权力，此举激怒了 元老院和富人。 保民官通常出身中间阶层，会被富 有的执政官吓倒。但提比略是罗马 贵族出身，所以不会惧怕其他社会 阶层的人恐吓。 最终，执政官决定无论如何都要阻 止提比略。 就这样，他们组织了一 群暴徒，在罗马的街头打死了提比 略

paragraphs.en: of Rome. Then the Senate's mob went on a rampage and killed 300 of the Populare leaders. But these killings did not stop the reform movement. As you know, Tiberius had a brave younger brother named Gaius Gracchus. Ten years later, Gaius decided to take up his brother's cause and continue the push for economic reform. In 123 BC Gaius Gracchus was elected to serve as Tribune of the People. Gaius was an excellent public speaker, and he was able to move large crowds of thousands of people with his rhetorical skills.
paragraphs.zh: 然后元老院的暴徒继续施暴，一共 杀死了 300 名民众派的领导者。 但是这些杀害依旧没能阻止改革的 脚步。 就像你知道的，提比略有一个年轻 饮用的弟弟：盖尤斯·格拉古。 10 年之后，盖尤斯接过哥哥未竟的 事业，继续推行经济改革。 公元前 123 年，盖尤斯·格拉古被 选为保民官。 盖尤斯是一位出色的演说家，他运 用修辞技巧的演讲能够吸引成千上 万的观众。

paragraphs.en: Gaius began promoting his own reforms. He changed the legal system to make it possible to try Senators for corruption. He made it the responsibility of the state rather than the soldiers to provide the army with clothes, weapons and equipment. He created food subsidies for the poor in the city of Rome. And he extended citizenship to all the Latin-speaking Italian cities, not just Rome. And Gaius also used his veto power aggressively to fight for his reforms. But the same thing happened to Gaius. There were two years of intense political struggle in Rome,
paragraphs.zh: 盖尤斯开始推行自己的改革。 他改变了法律制度，允许审判腐败 的执政官。 他让国家而不是士兵负责向军队提 供服装、武器和装备。 他为罗马的穷人提供食品补贴。 他将公民身份从罗马推广开来，覆 盖了所有讲拉丁语的意大利城市。 盖尤斯也以自己的否决权为武器积 极推行改革。 但同样的事情发生也在盖尤斯身 上。两年激烈的政治斗争后，罗马 的执政官组织了一群暴徒谋杀了盖

paragraphs.en: and then the Senators organized a mob and murdered Gaius Gracchus as well. The murder of the Gracchus brothers stopped the efforts at economic reform, but it created two dangerous problems. 1) Violence against your political enemies had become an accepted part of Roman government. 2) There was still no solution to the shortage of landowners needed for the army. The army problem was solved a few years later by a Roman general named Gaius Marius. Marius declared that anybody would be welcome to serve in his army, not just the land-owners. And in return Marius promised to
paragraphs.zh: 尤斯·格拉古。 对格拉古兄弟的谋杀使得经济改革 停滞下来，同时它也造成了两个危 险的问题。 1）罗马政府公认允许对政治敌人使 用暴力。 2）土地所有者数量短缺，军队人手 不够的问题仍无法解决。 几年后，一位名叫盖尤斯· 马留的 罗马将军解决了军队问题。 马留宣称欢迎任何人为他的军队服 务，而不仅仅是土地所有者。 作为回报，马留承诺给士兵提供武

paragraphs.en: provide his soldiers with their weapons, clothing, equipment, payment and pensions. With this offer, Gaius Marius had no problem finding enough soldiers for his army. And it was a good thing for Rome that he did this, because Rome soon needed his army. From 106 to 101 BC, Rome was threatened by a giant tribe of 200,000 Germans called the Cimbri. These Cimbri even destroyed a Roman army of 80,000 soldiers at the battle ofArusio on October 10th, 105 BC. So Rome begged Marius for help and elected him consul 5-years in a row. So Marius trained and equipped his
paragraphs.zh: 器、服装、装备、报酬以及养老金。 开出如此优渥的条件，盖尤斯·马 留不会再遇到找不到足够的士兵这 一问题了。 马留这样做，对罗马来说也是件好 事，因为罗马很快就会需要他的军 队。 公元前 106-101 年，罗马饱受辛布 里人的威胁，那是一个拥有 20 万日 尔曼人的大型部落。 这些辛布里人甚至在公元前 105 年 10 月 10 日的阿鲁西奥战役中摧毁 了一支由 8 万名士兵组成的罗马军 队。 所以罗马人请求马留帮助，并连续 5 年选举他作为执政官。 于是，马留率领训练有素、装备齐

paragraphs.en: army and went out to face the Cimbri. And he beat them at two key battles: the battle ofAquae Sextiae and the battle of Vercella. Marius saved Rome from destruction! During the Cimbri wars, one of Marius's officers named Sulla distinguished himself as a very good soldier. Sulla saw how Marius had raised an army and he did the same thing. He opened his army to anybody, and promised to provide all his soldiers with equipment, weapons, regular pay and pensions. In 91 BC, there was a big rebellion inAnatolia and Greece led by a King named Mithridates.
paragraphs.zh: 全的军队迎战辛布里人。 他赢得了两场关键战役：阿克韦- 塞克斯提亚之战和韦里克拉之战。 马留拯救了罗马，使其免遭毁灭！ 在与辛布里人战争期间，马留的一 位名叫索拉的军官表现十分突出。 索拉看到了马留是如何组建军队 的，于是他做了同样的事情。 他也欢迎任何人加入其军队，并承 诺向所有士兵提供装备、武器、定 期工资和养老金。 公元前 91 年，安纳托利亚和希腊发 生可一场由国王米特拉达梯领导的 大叛乱。

paragraphs.en: So the Senate asked Sulla to go crush this rebellion with his army. Sulla accepted because there would be good opportunities for plundering and getting rich. But while Sulla and Marius saved Rome from several military threats, they created a new problem. Before 107 BC, the RomanArmy had been made up of landowners who were loyal to the Roman state. But after Marius and Sulla, Roman armies were made up of poor men who were loyal to their generals not Rome. Because it was their generals who were responsible for paying them. And this problem became clear in 88 BC when a civil war broke out
paragraphs.zh: 所以执政官要求索拉带领军队去平 定叛乱。 索拉认为这是一个掠夺发财的好机 会，于是他接受了这一请求。 然而，当索拉和马留数次拯救罗马 于军事威胁之后，新的问题产生了。 在公元前 107 年之前，罗马军队由 对国家忠心耿耿的土地所有者组 成。 但在马留和索拉出现后，罗马军队 由穷人组成，而这些穷人对他们的 将军忠心耿耿，而非罗马。 因为是他们的将军负责支付他们薪 水。 公元前 88 年，民众派和贵族派之间 的内战爆发时，这一问题愈发明显。

paragraphs.en: between the Populares and the Optimates. Marius was a Populare and Sulla was an Optimate, so now both sides had an army. First violent fighting broke out in Rome between the two factions. Ever since the murder of the Gracchi brothers, violence had become a bigger and bigger part of politics. So now terrible fighting and killing began happening in the streets of Rome as the two sides fought each other. Then Marius came to Rome and with his help the Populares killed hundreds of Optimates. But then Marius died suddenly of old age in early 86 BC.
paragraphs.zh: 马留是民众派，而索拉属于贵族派， 现在，两边都拥有军队。 两派在罗马爆发了第一场激烈的战 斗。 自从格拉古兄弟被谋杀以来，暴力 已成为政治中越来越大的一部分。 所以当双方开战时，罗马街头发生 了可怕的战斗和杀戮事件。 然后马留来到罗马，在他的帮助下， 民众派杀死了数百名贵族派的人。 但是，在公元前 86 年初，上了年纪 的马留突然离世了。

paragraphs.en: Next it was Sulla's turn, after he finished crushing the rebellion in the east, he took his army and marched on Rome. Once Sulla was in Rome, his political allies took control of the Senate and made him "Dictator for Life!" Then Sulla and his allies began killing the Populares and all of their political enemies by the thousands. Every day Sulla and his supporters would put up lists of people to be killed. These lists were called proscriptions. If a name appeared on the proscription list and you killed that man for Sulla, then you got to keep some of his property. It was a reign of terror in the city of
paragraphs.zh: 接下来轮到索拉了，在平定东部叛 乱之后，他率领军队前往罗马。 索拉一到达罗马，他的政治盟友就 控制了元老院并推举他为“终身独 裁官”！ 然后，索拉和他的盟友开始铲除异 己，杀害了数千名民众派的人以及 所有的政治敌人。 索拉及其支持者每天都会列出要杀 的人名单。 这些名单被称为公敌宣告。如果一 个人的名字出现在公敌宣告名单 上，一旦你为索拉杀了那个人，你 就有权保留他的部分财产。 这就是罗马的恐怖统治时期。

paragraphs.en: Rome. To help him with his killing, Sulla had two assistant: Gnaeus Pompey and Marcus Licinius Crassus. Both were young men, and they relished their job of killing Sulla's enemies. They also were learning an important lesson: laws and traditions do not matter, force and violence are the only things that count. At one point another Roman criticized Pompey for violating ancient laws and customs by killing his fellow Romans.And Pompey replied: "Don't speak of laws to men who carry swords!" But there were some people who escaped Sulla's killing.
paragraphs.zh: 索拉找了两个助手帮忙杀人：格涅 乌斯·庞培和马库斯·李锡尼·克 拉苏。这两个年轻人很享受他们的 工作——杀害索拉的敌人。 他们也学到了重要的一课：法律和 传统不算什么，武力和暴力才是王 道。 有一次，一位罗马人批评庞培杀害 同胞违背了祖先的法律和习俗。庞 培回答到： “不要和佩剑的男人讲法律！” 但是，依然有一些人逃脱了索拉的 杀害。

paragraphs.en: During the Proscriptions, while all of Rome was terrified, Sulla hauled in a 16-year old boy named Julius Caesar. Caesar was the nephew of Gaius Marius and the son-in-law of another one of Sulla's enemies. So Sulla demanded that Caesar divorce his wife. But the young man looked Sulla right in his face and refused. Sulla wanted to have Caesar killed. But his own advisors begged him to spare the boy, since he was from one of Rome's most noble families. Sulla agreed, but he told them: "Alright, but I warn you! In that man goes many a Marius." Sulla killed all his enemies, undid
paragraphs.zh: 在实行公敌宣告的过程中，整个罗 马都陷入了恐慌。索拉抓来了一个 16 岁的男孩，名叫尤利乌斯·凯撒。 凯撒是盖尤斯·马留的侄子，也是 索拉的另一个敌人的女婿。 所以索拉要求凯撒和他的妻子离 婚。但是这个年轻人直直地看着凯 撒，拒绝了这一要求。 索拉想杀掉凯撒。但他的劝告者却 恳求他不要这样做，因为这个男孩 出身于罗马最高贵的家庭之一。 索拉同意了，但是他告诉下属： “好吧，但我警告你们！这是养虎 为患，留下他，以后会出现许许多 多个马留。” 索拉杀死了他所有的敌人。在过去

paragraphs.en: all the liberal reforms of the past 50 years, and then retired to his estate. He lived another 4 years and died in 78 BC. After he died, his lieutenants Pompey and Crassus became the most powerful men in Rome. Next time our lesson will focus on these three men: Pompey, Crassus and Julius Caesar, because these men belong to the final generation of the Roman Republic. Under them the Roman Empire will become fully formedAND the Roman Republic will finally be destroyed and replaced by a Monarchy. So please join me next time for that story! Okay everyone, I hope you enjoyed
paragraphs.zh: 的 50 年里，他废除了一切的自由主 义改革，退位后他又活了 4 年，于 公元前 78 年去世。 在他死后，他的副手庞培和克拉苏 成为了罗马最有权势的人。 下节课我们将集中讨论这三个人： 庞培，克拉苏和尤利乌斯·凯撒， 因为他们是罗马共和国的最后一代 人。 在他们的统治下，罗马帝国将完全 成形，罗马共和国将被彻底摧毁， 取而代之的是君主制。 所以下次请和我一起来听这个故 事！ 好了，我希望你们每个人都喜欢这

paragraphs.en: that lesson and want to learn more. If you liked that story about Gaius and Tiberius and Marius and Sulla and want to hear it again in much more detail, then I highly recommend the podcast "Hardcore History" by Dan Carlin and the series "Death Throes of the Republic" I also recommend Mike Duncan's book "Storm before the Storm" which focuses on this exact period in Roman history. Thank you very much and see you next time!
paragraphs.zh: 节课，并且想了解更多知识。 如果你喜欢关于盖尤斯和提比略以 及马留和索拉的故事，并想要更详 细地了解，那我强烈推荐丹·卡林 的播客《硬核历史》，以及《共和国 的垂死挣扎》系列内容。 我还推荐麦克·邓肯的《暴风雨前 的暴风雨》一书，该书恰恰讲述了 这一时期的罗马历史。 十分感谢，期待下期再见！

titleZh: 第十八讲：罗马帝国|没有人可以与凯撒抗衡

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on world history. Today we will learn about the beginning of the Roman Empire. We will be focusing on the years between 78 BC and 64 AD. Here is what that looks like on our timeline. We ended our last lesson by discussing Lucius Sulla. When Sulla came to Rome in 81 BC, he massacred the Populares faction in Rome and was declared Dictator for Life, and he undid all of the liberal reforms from the past 50 years. When Sulla died in 78 BC, his assistants Gnaeus Pompey and Marcus Crassus became the most powerful men in Rome.
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。今天我们要学习的是罗马帝国 的起源。 让我们把目光投向时间轴上公元 前 78 年到公元 64 年的这段时间。 我们上节课讲到卢基乌斯·苏拉就 结束了。 苏拉于公元前 81 年来到罗马，他 清除了罗马的平民派系，公开宣布 成为终身独裁者，并废除了过去 50 年来的所有自由主义改革。 苏拉在公元前 78 年去世后，他的 助手格涅乌斯·庞培和马库斯·克 拉苏成为了罗马最有权势的人。

paragraphs.en: Before we go any further, we need to talk once again about slavery in Rome. As I told you two lessons ago, hundreds of thousands of people were captured in Rome's wars, taken back to Roman cities and sold at slave markets. These slaves did all sorts of work. They did domestic chores, they worked in mines, they built things or they worked on farms. But I didn't tell you that the slaves also fought each other. Some of the strongest male slaves were chosen as gladiators, and their job was to fight each other in Roman stadiums for the entertainment of the crowd. About 10% of these fights ended in
paragraphs.zh: 讲到这里，我们需要再次讨论一下 罗马的奴隶制问题。 我在前两节课上讲过，成百上千的 人在罗马战争中被俘后，被带回了 罗马城市，并在奴隶市场上被贩 卖。 这些奴隶承担了各种各样的工作。 他们做家务，在矿山、在工地或者 在农场工作。 但我没有告诉你们奴隶还要战斗。 一些最强壮的男性奴隶被选为角 斗士，他们的工作就是在罗马的体 育场里为观众提供娱乐。 大约有 10%的战斗会以其中一名角

paragraphs.en: the death of one of the gladiators. In 73 BC, several slave-gladiators escaped from a gladiator school in Capua, Italy. They were led by a man named Spartacus. They defeated a small force sent to capture them, and soon slaves all across Italy began running away to join Spartacus and his army. By 71 BC this had turned into a full-scale slave revolt, and Spartacus had amassed a following of 120,000 run-away slaves. This slave army even defeated two legions sent against them. The rebellion was finally put down by Pompey and Crassus, who joined forces to raise a large army. They chased down Spartacus and defeated his army in battle.
paragraphs.zh: 斗士的死亡而结束。 公元前 73 年，几名角斗士从意大 利卡普亚的角斗士学校逃了出来。 他们的领头人叫做斯巴达克斯。 他们打败了前来抓捕他们的一支 小部队。很快，意大利各地的奴隶 们都开始逃亡，前来加入斯巴达克 斯和他的军队。 到公元前 71 年，这已演变成为了 一场全面的奴隶起义，斯巴达克斯 集结了 12 万名逃亡奴隶。 这支奴隶军队甚至击败了两支前 来攻击他们的军团。 庞培和克拉苏联合组建了一支庞 大的军队，才得以把起义镇压下 去。 他们追击斯巴达克斯，在战斗中击 败了他的军队。

paragraphs.en: Then they crucified 6000 slaves and placed their bodies at intervals along the Appian Way, the main road in Italy. This was done to send a message to any other slaves who might think about fighting back. "If you try to rebel, this is what will happen to you!" Then, in 71 BC, there was another rebellion in the East. This time the Senate asked Pompey to go put it down. So he did, Pompey went east and crushed the rebellion in Anatolia and Greece. But then Pompey kept going east where he conquered Syria and Israel.
paragraphs.zh: 然后他们把 6000 名奴隶钉在十字 架上，并将这些尸体沿着意大利的 主要道路——亚壁古道进行间隔 放置。 这样做是为了向其他想要反击的 奴隶传递一个信息。 “这就是造反的下场！” 随后在公元前 71 年，东部地区又 发生了一次叛乱。 这次，元老院要求庞培前去镇压。 就这样，庞培向东出发，镇压了安 纳托利亚和希腊的叛乱。 但随后庞培继续向东，征服了叙利 亚和以色列。

paragraphs.en: He turned the Kingdom of Israel into a vassal state of Rome. The Jews would continue to have their own King, but now he would have to take orders from a Roman governor. Pompey's returned to Rome in 61 BC with a huge amount of treasure that he had plundered in his wars. For this he was rewarded with a giant triumphal parade on his 45th birthday. The next year a third man joined Pompey and Crassus. He was Julius Caesar, the same man who had stood up to Sulla when he was just 16-years old. But now Caesar was 40 years old and determined to make his mark on the world.
paragraphs.zh: 他把以色列王国变成了罗马的附 属国。 犹太人的国王可以继续在位，但现 在他将不得不听从罗马统治者的 命令。 公元前 61 年，庞培带着他在战争 中掠夺的大量财富回到了罗马。 他也因此在盛大的凯旋游行中度 过了自己的 45 岁生日。 第二年，第三个人加入了庞培和克 拉苏的行列。 这个人就是尤利乌斯·凯撒，他在 16 岁的时候就跟着苏拉了。 现在凯撒已经 40 岁了，他决心要 在这个世界上留下自己的印记。

paragraphs.en: He got the financial support of Crassus, and in 60 BC, he won the election and became one of Rome's consuls. To help you understand Caesar better, here is his family tree. As you can see for yourself, Caesar married his daughter Julia to Pompey in order to strengthen their political alliance. Julia was only 17 years old at the time, and Pompey was 46. This age difference might seem a little barbaric to modern people, but it was typical for the ancient Romans. You can also see that Julius Caesar was the nephew of Gaius Marius through marriage.
paragraphs.zh: 他得到了克拉苏的财政支持，并在 公元前 60 年赢得了选举，成为罗 马的执政官之一。 你可以通过凯撒的家谱来更好地 了解他。 可以看到，凯撒为了加强他们的政 治联盟，将自己的女儿朱莉娅嫁给 了庞培。 当时朱莉娅只有 17 岁，庞培已经 46 岁了。 这种年龄差异在现代人的眼里或 许是一种野蛮未开化的行为，但在 古罗马人的眼中再正常不过了。 你也可以看到，尤利乌斯·凯撒通 过结婚，和盖乌斯·马略成了叔侄 关系。

paragraphs.en: Once Caesar joined in 60 BC, Rome was essentially ruled by three men: Pompey, Crassus and Caesar. These men formed a political alliance called the first Triumvirate. Although the Senate and other republican institutions still existed, real power now rested in the hands of these men, and the money and soldiers that they controlled. Then in 58 BC, Caesar used the help of his political allies to be appointed the governor of Cisalpine Gaul. That is the part of Gaul on the Italian side of the Alps. (Today this area is Southern France and Northern Italy) With his authority as governor, Cesar was able to raise troops. He borrowed even more money to
paragraphs.zh: 凯撒在公元前 60 年入局后，罗马 基本上由庞培，克拉苏和凯撒三个 人统治。 他们组成了一个政治联盟，被称为 第一个“三头统治”。 尽管元老院和其他共和制度的机 构仍然存在，但真正的权力、财富 和军队现在掌握在他们的手中。 公元前 58 年，凯撒在他的政治盟 友的帮助下，当上了南阿尔卑斯的 高卢地区的总督。这个地区是高卢 位于阿尔卑斯山意大利一侧的部 分。（今天这里则是法国南部和意 大利北部） 凯撒利用总督的权力，得以扩军。 他甚至借了更多的资金来装备军 队。

paragraphs.en: strengthen these armies. Then Caesar invaded Gaul. For the next 6 years he fought the Gauls in battle after battle. Cesar was a brilliant general so he was able to win all of his battles, even though he was usually outnumbered. Eventually the Gallic King Vercingetorix was able to unite the different Gallic tribes against Rome, but Caesar defeated him in 52 BC at the Battle ofAlesia. This painting shows you Vercingetorix's surrender to Cesar. These conquests added Gaul to the Roman territory, and the plunder paid off Cesar's debts. These victories also made Caesar the most popular man in Rome.
paragraphs.zh: 随后凯撒入侵高卢。在接下来的 6 年里，他与高卢人进行了长期的战 争。 凯撒是一位杰出的将领，所以他总 能够在人数处于劣势的情况下赢 得战斗。 最终，高卢领袖维钦托利联合了各 个高卢部落前来对抗罗马，但是凯 撒在公元前 52 年的阿莱西亚战役 中击败了他。 这幅画描绘的是维钦托利向凯撒 投降时的场景。 高卢被划入了罗马的领土，凯撒也 用他掠夺的财富还清了债务。 这些胜利也使得凯撒在罗马大受 欢迎。

paragraphs.en: But you should also remember something. Over the course of those 6 years Caesar's armies probably killed about 1 million people. And he enslaved another million, mostly the women and children. Caesar then wrote a history of these wars so that people back in Rome could read about them. The goal was to make Caesar famous and increase his reputation in Rome. It was a 1st century BC publicity campaign! Because Caesar wanted as many people as possible to understand his story, he wrote in a very simple and direct style. As a result, for the last 60 generations, his account of the Gallic Wars is often one of the first
paragraphs.zh: 但你也应该知道，在这 6 年里，凯 撒的军队大概杀死了 100 万人。 另有 100 万人被卖作奴隶，其中大 部分是妇女和儿童。 凯撒写了一本关于这些战争的史 书，来让罗马的人们阅读。 凯撒此举的目的是为了名留青史， 并增加他在罗马的声望。 这可是公元前 1 世纪的一次宣传活 动！ 因为凯撒希望更多人了解他的故 事，所以他的写作的风格非常简 单、直接。因此，在过去 60 代人 的时间里，他对高卢战争的描述常 常是拉丁学生在学习语言时阅读 书籍的首选！

paragraphs.en: books that Latin students read when they are learning the language! While Caesar was winning all those victories in Gaul, his patron Crassus became jealous and decided that he ALSO wanted to win military victories. So Crassus raised an army and went East, intending to conquer the Persian Empire, just like Alexander the Great. But the Persians were quite prepared to defend themselves against Roman aggression, and they attacked Crassus once he entered their territory. Unfortunately, unlike Caesar and Pompey, Crassus was NOT a good general. The Persians destroyed his army and killed him at the battle of Carrhae.
paragraphs.zh: 凯撒在高卢获得的胜利，让他的赞 助人克拉苏为之嫉妒，也希望赢得 一场军事胜利。 于是，克拉苏组建了一支军队，向 东进军，打算像亚历山大大帝一 样，征服波斯帝国。 但是波斯人已经做好了抵抗罗马 人侵略的准备，克拉苏进入他们的 领土后立刻遭受了攻击。 不幸的是，与凯撒和庞培不同，克 拉苏并不是一个好将军。波斯人在 卡雷战役中击垮了他的军队并杀 死了他。

paragraphs.en: With Crassus dead, the alliance between Pompey and Cesar became unstable. And at roughly the same time another thing happened to break the two men apart. The previous year, in 54 BC, Julia died while giving birth. She had been the bond that held Cesar and Pompey together, and with her death, the two men began to fall apart. From 54 to 49 BC, Julius Caesar's enemies became more and more frightened of his power. Eventually they convinced Pompey to join their side, and Rome slid into a civil war. Cesar and Pompey fought each other for 4 years from 49 to 54 BC.
paragraphs.zh: 克拉苏之死使得庞培和凯撒之间 的联盟变得不稳定起来。 大约在同一时期，碰巧发生的另一 件事使得两人分道扬镳了。 在前一年即公元前 54 年，朱莉娅 在分娩时难产死去了。 朱莉娅一直以来都是维系凯撒和 庞培关系的纽带，随着她的去世， 两个人的关系破裂了。 从公元前 54 年到公元前 49 年，尤 利乌斯·凯撒的敌人越来越惧怕他 的权力。最终，他们说服庞培加入 其阵营，罗马陷入了内战。 从公元前 54 年到公元前 49 年，凯 撒和庞培打了 4 年的仗。

paragraphs.en: This period in Roman history is called "Caesar's Civil War". The key battle of that war happened in Central Greece at the Battle of Pharasalus onAugust 9th 48 BC. Even though Pompey had 58,000 soldiers compared to Caesar's 33,000, it turned out that Caesar was the better general. Caesar destroyed Pompey's army at this battle. Cesar won the civil war and defeated Pompey. He was then the most powerful man in Rome. After that, nobody was strong enough to defy Caesar. In 46 BC, he was named dictator for 10 years. And then in 44 BC he was named dictator for life, just like Sulla 35 years before.
paragraphs.zh: 这段时期在罗马历史上被称为“凯 撒内战”。 公元前 48 年 8 月 9 日，发生在希 腊中部的法萨罗战役是这场战役 的关键一战。 尽管庞培有 58000 名士兵，而凯撒 只有 33000 人，但事实证明，凯撒 的军事才能更胜一筹。 凯撒在这场战役中击垮了庞培的 军队。 凯撒打败庞培，赢得了内战。他成 为了当时罗马最有权势的人。此役 之后，再没有人可以同凯撒抗衡。 公元前 46 年，他被任命为 10 年的 独裁者。在随后的公元前 44 年， 他和 35 年前的苏拉一样，被任命 为终身独裁者。

paragraphs.en: But unlike Sulla, Caesar did not kill his enemies. He showed them mercy and spared the life of anybody who surrendered to him. That proved to be a big mistake, because on March 15th, 44 BC, he went to a meeting at the Senate to discuss business. At that meeting several Senators pulled out knives and stabbed Caesar to death. Then the assassins left the Senate, confident that they had destroyed a tyrant who would make himself into a king. But they had miscalculated. Caesar had two successors who would lead his people. The first was his nephew and heir, Octavian Caesar. The second was his lieutenant, Mark Antony, who was also the consul in
paragraphs.zh: 但与苏拉不同的是，凯撒并没有处 死他的敌人。他展示了仁慈之心， 放过了所有的投降者。 这在后来被证明是一个巨大的错 误。公元前 44 年 3 月 15 日，他前 往元老院开会讨论事务。 在那次会议上，几位参议员拔出刀 子刺死了凯撒。 然后暗杀者离开了元老院，他们确 信自己消灭了一个即将称帝的暴 君。 但他们失算了。凯撒有两位继任者 将继续领导他的人民。第一个是他 的侄子和继承人，屋大维·凯撒。 第二个是他的副手马克·安东尼， 他也是当年的执政官。

paragraphs.en: that year. 5 days later on March 20th, Mark Antony delivered a stirring speech at Caesar's funeral. And he revealed that Caesar had left his land to the common Roman people to be made into parks and gardens. This surprised and moved the common people of Rome who had come to the funeral to say goodbye to Caesar. Caesar had always been a Populare, like his uncle Marius or the Gracchi brothers before that. So when the common people of Rome realized that yet another one of their champions had been murdered by the rich, they rioted, and began attacking the houses of the Senators involved with the conspiracy.
paragraphs.zh: 5 天后的 3 月 20 日，马克·安东尼 在凯撒的葬礼上发表了激动人心 的演讲。他透露凯撒把自己的土地 留给了普通的罗马民众，用于建造 公园和花园。 这使前来葬礼向凯撒告别的罗马 平民感到惊讶和感动。 凯撒和他的叔父尤利乌斯或之前 的格拉古兄弟一样，一直都深受人 们欢迎。 罗马民众意识到，他们的又一个拥 护的人被富人阶级谋杀了。因此人 们开始暴动，并攻击参与阴谋的参 议员的住宅。

paragraphs.en: The assassins had to flee Rome, leaving Mark Antony and Octavian in control of the capital. Then the two men organized Caesar's troops and went after their enemies. From 44 to 42 BC, Octavian and Mark Anthony hunted down and killed all of Caesar's assassins. They defeated the last resistance to their rule at the Battle of Phillipi in 42 BC. Then, in order to strengthen their alliance, Mark Antony married Octavian's older sister named Octavia Minor in 40 BC. Here is a family tree for Octavian to help you understand how everyone is related. Then Octavian and Mark Antony
paragraphs.zh: 暗杀者不得不逃离罗马，马克·安 东尼和屋大维得以控制首都。 两人组织了凯撒的军队，去追击他 们的敌人。 从公元前 44 年到 42 年，屋大维和 马克·安东尼追杀了所有参与暗杀 凯撒的人。 他们在公元前 42 年的腓力比战役 中，击败了对他们统治的最后一丝 抵抗。 为了加强两人的联盟，马克·安东 尼在公元前 40 年娶了屋大维的妹 妹--小屋大维为妻。 这是屋大维的家谱，你可以从中了 解各个人之间是怎样的关系。 然后，屋大维和安东尼在公元前 39

paragraphs.en: split the Empire between them in 39 BC. Octavian ruled the western provinces, and Antony ruled the eastern ones. But then, when Antony moved to the East, he fell in love with Cleopatra, the Queen of Egypt. In case you forgot, Cleopatra ruled Egypt, but she was actually Greek. She was the descendant of Ptolemy I Soter, Alexander the Great's general. So then Mark Antony divorced Octavian's sister and married Cleopatra. Octavian took this as an insult to his family and the alliance between the two men fell apart in 33 BC. A war began between Antony and
paragraphs.zh: 年将帝国一分为二。 屋大维统治着西部的省份，安东尼 则统治着东部省份。 但是，当安东尼前往东部后，他爱 上了埃及女王克利奥帕特拉。 别忘了，克利奥帕特拉虽然是埃及 的统治者，但她实际上是希腊人。 她是亚历山大大帝的将军托勒密 一世的后代。 于是马克·安东尼和屋大维的妹妹 离婚，娶了克利奥帕特拉。 屋大维认为这是对他家族的侮辱， 两人的联盟在公元前 33 年破裂了。 屋大维和安东尼开战了。这场战争

paragraphs.en: Octavian. It lasted from 32 to 30 BC and it is known as the "Final War of the Roman Republic." Octavian raised an army and sailed south to fight MarkAntony. The key battle in the war was the Battle of Actium on September 2nd, 31 BC. Octavian won the Battle and Mark Antony's forces were destroyed. Cleopatra and Mark Antony fled, but Octavian chased them down, eventually they were trapped in Alexandria, Egypt and it became clear that they would both be captured. So on August 1st, 30 BC, Mark Antony stabbed himself with his sword, and died in Cleopatra's arms. That same day Octavian's forces
paragraphs.zh: 从公元前 32 年持续到公元前 30 年，被称为“罗马共和国的最后一 场战争”。 屋大维召集军队南下与马克·安东 尼开战。 这场战争的关键战役是发生在公 元前 31 年 9 月 2 日的亚克兴战役。 屋大维击败了马克·安东尼的部 队，赢得了战斗。 克利奥帕特拉和马克·安东尼逃跑 了。但在屋大维的追捕下，他们最 终被困在了埃及的亚历山大，无路 可逃。 所以在公元前 30 年 8 月 1 日，马 克·安东尼用剑自尽，死在克利奥 帕特拉的怀里。 就在同一天，屋大维的军队攻破了

paragraphs.en: broke into the palace and captured Cleopatra. When Octavian went in to meet her, she told him: "I will not be led in a triumph" And then she killed herself a few days later with poison that a loyal servant secretly gave her. With the deaths of Cleopatra and Mark Antony, there was nobody left to oppose Octavian's power. And Egypt was added to the Roman territory. In 27 BC, Octavian made a show of returning all power to the Senate. But behind the scenes he made sure that he was in control of all the money and all the armies of Rome. Now the word "king" had had a
paragraphs.zh: 宫殿，抓住了克利奥帕特拉。 屋大维进去见她时，她说:“我不 会看着你们胜利的。” 几天后，她用一名忠实的仆人偷偷 给她的毒药自杀了。 克利奥帕特拉和马克·安东尼死 后，再没有人能够对抗屋大维的权 力。埃及也被划入了罗马的版图。 公元前 27 年，屋大维表面上做出 了一副将权力归还给元老院的姿 态。 但在幕后，他把财富和军队牢牢控 制在手中。 “国王”这个词从塔克文国王开

paragraphs.en: negative connotation in Rome ever since the Tarquin Kings, so Octavian did not call himself a "King." Instead, he used the title "imperator" which was an army term that meant "commander." This is the origin of the English word "emperor" . Rome was now a true Empire with an emperor. On January 16th, 27 BC, the Senate awarded Octavian the Title of "Augustus" which means "Venerable." During Augustus' life, there was a calendar reform and the Romans added two months to their year. These were the month of July, (named after Julius Caesar) and the month of August (named after
paragraphs.zh: 始，在罗马就有了负面的含义，所 以屋大维并自称为“国王”。 相反，他使用的头衔是“皇帝”， 这是一个军事术语，意思是“指挥 官”。 这也是英文单词“emperor”的起 源，这也标志着罗马现在已经成为 由皇帝统治的帝国。 公元前 27 年 1 月 16 日，元老院授 予屋大维“奥古斯都”的称号，意 思是“可敬的”。 奥古斯都在他的任期内开展了一 次历法改革，给每年增加了两个 月。 这两个月就是七月 (以凯撒大帝 的名字命名)和八月(以奥古斯都 的名字命名)。

paragraphs.en: Augustus). Augustus reigned as Emperor from 27 BC until 14AD. After he died he was succeeded by his stepson Tiberius who ruled from 14 until 37AD. After Tiberius died his nephew Caligula became emperor and ruled from 37 until 41 AD. Unfortunately, Caligula was insane and sadistic. There are many terrible stories from his reign. Eventually, the palace guard assassinated Caligula and replaced him with his uncle Claudius. Claudius was a competent emperor and ruled from 41 until 53 AD. The Romans conquered Britain under his rule. Unfortunately, Claudius was
paragraphs.zh: 奥古斯都于公元前 27 年至公元 14 年在位。 在他死后，他的继子提比略于公元 14 年至 37 年继位。 提比略死后，他的侄子卡利古拉成 为皇帝，于公元 37 年到 41 年间在 位。但卡利古拉是一个疯狂的虐待 狂。在他统治期间发生了许多可怕 的故事。 最终，宫廷卫队暗杀了卡利古拉， 并拥立他的叔叔克劳迪斯即位。克 劳迪斯是一位称职的皇帝，从公元 41年到53年一直统治着这个国家。 罗马人在他的统治下征服了英国。 不幸的是，克劳迪斯被他的第四任

paragraphs.en: poisoned by his fourth wife Agrippina. And then Agrippina's son Nero became the 5th emperor of Rome. Nero became emperor in 54 AD at the age of 17. To help you keep track of these first 5 emperors, here is a family tree to show you how they are all related. Ten years into Nero's reign, in the year 64 AD, there was a terrible fire in Rome. Nero was not a very good leader, and he loved playing the lyre for people. So a rumor began to spread that Nero had played the lyre and sung while the city burned. And another rumor spread that Nero had started the fire himself. To protect himself from these
paragraphs.zh: 妻子阿格里皮娜毒死了。随后阿格 里皮娜的儿子尼禄成为了罗马的 第五任皇帝。公元 54 年，尼禄在 他 17 岁时即位。 通过这个家谱可以了解这前 5 位皇 帝是如何联系在一起的。 尼禄在位十年后，公元 64 年，罗 马发生了一场可怕的火灾。 尼禄不是一个非常称职的领袖，他 热衷于为人们弹奏七弦竖琴。 于是有谣言说城中大火熊熊燃烧 之时，尼禄却在弹琴唱歌。 更有甚者说是尼禄自己放的火。 为了让自己免受这些谣言的影响，

paragraphs.en: rumors Nero decided that he needed to find somebody else to blame for the fire. And he chose a strange new religion called Christianity that had been growing in Rome for the last 30 years. Our best source for this fire is the Roman historian Publius Cornelius Tacitus, who lived from 56 to 120 AD. So he was 8 years old during the fire. Tacitus wrote two works of history that cover this time period: The Histories and the Annals. Here is what Tacitus writes: " ...To get rid of the report (that he started the fire), Nero fastened the guilt and inflicted the most exquisite tortures on a class hated for their
paragraphs.zh: 尼禄决定为这场火灾找个替罪羊。 他把目光投向了在罗马刚刚发展 30 年的一个新宗教--基督教。 我们目前最可靠的史料来源是罗 马历史学家普布里乌斯·克奈里乌 斯·塔西佗，他生活在公元 56 年 到 120 年之间。所以在火灾发生时， 他只有 8 岁。 塔西佗有两本涉及这一时期的历 史著作:《历史》和《编年史》。 塔西佗写道: “…为了免受指责(是他放的火)， 尼禄将罪责强加于被民众称为“基 督徒”的这个阶级之上，并让他们 因这一可憎的行为受尽了酷刑折

paragraphs.en: abominations, called Christians by the populace." Tacitus then goes on to describe the cruel way that Nero treated these Christians. He said that Nero had them crucified, or torn apart by dogs or burned alive. This story by Tacitus is the earliest non-Biblical mention of the Christian religion. So who were these Christians who now appear for the first time in the historical record? Well, that will be the topic of our next class! Thank you very much. Okay friends,I know I went a little long with this lesson,but I couldn’t help it!There was just so much good stuff to talk about.
paragraphs.zh: 磨。” 他还描述了尼禄对待这些基督徒 的残忍方式。他说尼禄把他们钉在 十字架上，让狗撕咬他们，或者活 活烧死他们。 塔西佗写下的这个故事，是基督教 第一次出现在非圣经书籍中。 那么，这些在历史记载中首次出现 的基督徒是谁呢? 那将是我们下节课的主题! 谢谢大家。 好了同学们，我知道这节课有点 长，但有那么多的好故事可以讲， 我实在情不自禁！

paragraphs.en: I hope you will want to learn more! As I said before,you could listen to the Podcast Hardcore History by Dan Carlin.This history in covered in the series”Death Throes of the Republic”. There is also the fantastic BBC series about the first 5 emperos called/Claudius.It is somewhat fictionalized but stays pretty close to the history.Its all about the palace intrigue surrounding the Imperial family. Then there is the primary source,The Histories and The Annals by Tacitus. And you can also read Julius Caesars own words and thoughts about the Gallic War and the Civil War.
paragraphs.zh: 我希望你还想了解更多！ 我之前也提到过，你可以听听丹·卡 林的播客“硬核历史”。这段历史 被收录在“共和国之殇”系列中。 BBC 有一个很棒的连续剧叫做 《我·克劳迪乌斯》，讲的是有关 前五位皇帝的故事。它有一些虚构 的成分，但总体上与历史非常接 近。故事是围绕黄石的宫廷阴谋展 开的。 还有就是我们最主要的史料来源， 塔西佗的《历史》和《编年史》这 两本书。 你也可以读一读尤利乌斯·凯撒关 于高卢战争和罗马内战的言论和 想法。

paragraphs.en: Finally,there is also a lot of information about many of these figures in Plutarch’s Live of the Noble Greeks and Romans. And,this is finally,William Shakespear was so inspired by reading Plutarch in the late 1500s that he wrote two plays based on this history:Julius Caesar as well as Antony and Cleopatra. Okay that’s all for now,thank you very much!
paragraphs.zh: 在普鲁塔克的《希腊罗马名人传》 一书中，也有很多关于这些人物的 信息。 十六世纪晚期的威廉·莎士比亚深 受鲁塔克作品的启发，根据这段历 史创作了两部喜剧：《凯撒大帝》 与《安东尼与克里奥佩特拉》 好了，今天的课就讲到这里，谢谢 大家，再见！

titleZh: 第十九讲：基督教|耶稣的神迹

paragraphs.en: Hello everyone, welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Today we are going to be learning about Christianity. This story will focus on the years between 1 and 64AD. Here is what that looks like on our timeline. One problem with discussing early Christianity from a historical perspective, is that almost all of our sources are written by Christians. So it’s impossible to get a really objective understanding. Look at this timeline to see what I mean. Jesus Christ lived from 1AD until 36AD. The records that we have about him
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到我们的世界历史 系列课程。今天我们要学习的内容 是基督教。 从时间轴上可以看到，故事集中发 生在公元 1-64 年。 从历史的角度讨论早期基督教，有 一个问题：我们现在几乎所有的资 料都是由基督徒写的。 所以我们 不可能从中得到真正客观的理解。 看看这个时间轴，你就明白我的意 思了。 耶稣基督生活在公元元年到公元 36 年之间。 关于耶稣的记录写于公元 50-100

paragraphs.en: now were written between 50 and 100AD. These records were all written by Christians and they form the part of the Bible called the "New Testament". The two oldest non-Christian sources that mention Christianity are the Roman historian Tacitus, who wrote around 116AD, and the Jewish historian Josephus who wrote around 94AD. Both of these histories mention Christianity, but only very briefly for one or two paragraphs. It is just enough to prove that the religion existed by that point, but gives us no detail. So we are forced to rely on the Christian tradition for the details. It will give you the story of Christianity from the Christian point
paragraphs.zh: 年之间。 这些记录都是由基督徒 写的，这一部分构成了圣经中的 《新约》。 除基督教以外，提到基督教的两个 最古老的资源来自于罗马历史学 家塔西佗于公元116年左右写的历 史记录，以及犹太历史学家约瑟夫 斯在公元94年左右写的历史记录。 这两段历史记录都提到了基督教， 但只有一两个段落。这只能够证明 宗教在那个时候就存在了，但除此 之外没有给我们任何细节。 所以我们不得不依靠基督传统知 识去获取细节。 我会从基督教的角度给你们讲基 督教的故事，就像我之前从佛教的

paragraphs.en: of view, just like I told you the story of Buddhism from the Buddhist point of view and will tell you the story of Islam from the Muslim point of view. With that said, let's learn the story of Jesus according to the New Testament! The first thing you should know is that the New Testament was originally written in Greek. This is important to know because a lot of Christian terms come from Greek as a result. The New Testament was written in Greek because the earliest Christian communities were mostly in the Eastern Part of the Roman Empire. You can see that for yourself in this map.
paragraphs.zh: 角度给你们讲佛教一样，之后我还 会从穆斯林的角度给你们讲伊斯 兰教的故事。 说到这里，让我们根据新约来学习 耶稣的故事吧! 关于《新约》，你需要知道的第一 件事就是，最开始它是用希腊语写 成的。 这一点很重要，因为其结果是许多 基督教词条都来源于希腊语。 《新约》之所以是用希腊语写就， 是因为最早的基督教团体大多出 现在罗马帝国的东部。 你可以自行查看这张地图。

paragraphs.en: Now this map shows you in blue all the areas where Greek was the main language. And even though the official language of the Roman Empire was Latin, the Roman respected the Greek language just like they admired the rest of Greek culture. In fact, you could not consider yourself an educated Roman if you could not speak Greek. Now that we know about about the language of the New Testament, let's look at the content. New Testaments are usually published as the second half of Christian Bibles,or sometimes as separate books. Like the Old Testament, the New Testament is actually a collection of
paragraphs.zh: 地图中所有的地方都标有不同深 浅的蓝色，而这些地方的主要语言 就是希腊语。 即使罗马帝国的官方语言是拉丁 语，但人们尊重希腊语，就像他们 尊崇其他的希腊文化一样。 事实上在当时，如果你不会说希腊 语，那么你甚至不能自视为受过教 育的罗马人。 我们已经知道了《新约》使用的语 言，现在我们一起来看看内容。 《新约》通常作为基督教圣经的第 二部分出版，或者也有些时候会单 独出版。 跟《旧约》类似，《新约》实际上 也是许多本书组成的书集，而非单

paragraphs.en: books rather than just 1 book. If you open up the New Testament and look at the table of contents you will see a list of 27 books. These 27 books can be divided into 5 categories. The first 4 books are called the "gospels", these are biographies of Jesus Christ.According to Christian tradition they were written by four followers of Jesus named Matthew, Mark, Luke and John. But the name of each author does not appear in the text, so we cannot know for sure the identity of each author. The next book is called the "Acts of theApostles.It tells the story of Jesus's earliest followers after Jesus's ministry ends.It was written by the same author as the Gospel of Luke.
paragraphs.zh: 单一本书。 如果你打开《新约》，浏览一下目 录表，你会看到一个包含 27 本书 的列表。 这 27 本书可以分成 5 个类别。 前 4 本书被称为“福音”，这些是 耶稣基督的传记。照基督教的说 法，这几本书是耶稣的四名追随 者：马太、马可、路加和约翰写成 的。但是他们的名字并没有出现在 文本中，所以我们也不能确定每本 书的作者。 下一本书叫做《使徒行传》。它讲 述了耶稣传道结束后最早一批追 随者的故事。它和路加福音是同一 作者。

paragraphs.en: The next 13 books are called the "Pauline Epistles." That's because the word "epistle" means "letter" in ancient Greek and they are letters of advice and instruction from St. Paul, a very important early church leader. The next 8 books are epistles written by other early church leaders like Peter, James, or John. And the final book, Revelations, is probably the strangest book in the collection. This book is a prophecy by the apostle John about the end of the world. It is full of strange metaphors and apocalyptic imagery. I think the book of Revelations is the hardest book to understand. Here is a picture summarizing everything I just told you.
paragraphs.zh: 接下来的 13 本书被称为“保罗使 徒书”。这是因为在古希腊语中， “使徒书”一词的意思是“信”， 而它们是来自一位非常重要的早 期教会领袖——圣保罗的书信建 议和指导。 接下来的8本书是其他的最早教会 领袖比如彼得、雅各和约翰所写的 书信。 最后一本书《启示录》可能是这个 系列中最奇怪的一本。这本书讲了 使徒约翰关于世界末日的预言，书 中充满了奇怪的隐喻和世界末日 图像。我认为《启示录》是最难理 解的一本书。 这张图片涵盖了我刚刚讲述的内 容。

paragraphs.en: So now let's begin our summary. In the gospels the story of Jesus begins with an angel appearing to a young Jewish woman named Mary. The angel tells Mary that she will soon be pregnant with a child even though she is still a virgin. But when Mary gets miraculously pregnant, she is already engaged to be married to a man named Joseph. Obviously, when Joseph find out that his fiancee is pregnant, he assumes that she has been unfaithful to him. So Joseph decides to call off the marriage, but do it quietly so as not to humiliate Mary. But then an angel appears to Joseph in a dream and tells him to go ahead with the marriage and to name the
paragraphs.zh: 现在我们开始简要地了解一下故 事情节。在福音书中，耶稣的故事 以一个天使出现在一个名叫玛利 亚的年轻犹太妇女面前开始。 天使告诉玛利亚，尽管她还是处 女，但她很快就会怀孕。 但是当玛利亚奇迹般怀孕时，她已 经和一个叫约瑟的男人订婚了。 很明显，当约瑟发现未婚妻怀孕 时，他认为她对他不忠。 于是约瑟决定取消这桩婚事，但要 悄悄地去做，免得羞辱玛利亚。 但是后来，一个天使出现在约瑟梦 中，告诉他继续结婚，并给孩子取 名为“耶稣”。

paragraphs.en: child "Jesus." Jesus is the Greek form of the Hebrew name "Ye-shua", which means "The Lord Saves." Then Mary and Joseph go back to Joseph's hometown Bethlehem, and there Marygoes into labor. There was no room for the family at the Inn, so Mary has to give birth in a stable. This story is the origin of Christmas.Although the actual birth may have happened in the spring instead of on December 25th. When Jesus was about 30 years old, he began his ministry. First he went to the Jordan River and was baptized by a holy man named John the Baptist. This is why today the first step to
paragraphs.zh: 耶稣是个希腊语名字，希伯来语是 “约书亚”，意思是“救世主”。 于是玛利亚和约瑟回到约瑟的家 乡伯利恒，玛利亚在那里分娩了。 他们在旅馆里没有房间，所以玛丽 只好在马厩里生孩子。 这个故事就是圣诞节的由来。虽然 实际上耶稣出生的时间可能是在 春天，而不是 12 月 25 日。 当耶稣 30 岁左右的时候，他成了 一名牧师。 他先往约旦河接受了一个名叫约 翰的圣人的洗礼。 这就是为什么如今成为基督徒第

paragraphs.en: becoming a Christian is a baptism. Then Jesus goes out into the desert and fasts for 40 days and 40 nights. After he came out of the desert, Jesus began teaching at a synagogue in the town of Capernaum. In the synagogue a man possessed by a demon begins screaming and yelling when he sees Jesus until Jesus says "come out of him" and drives the demon out of the man without harming him. Several times in the gospel accounts, Jesus performs these exorcisms on people possessed by demons. In addition to driving out demons, Jesus also begins performing numerous miracles of healing on people afflicted with various
paragraphs.zh: 一步就是接受洗礼。 然后耶稣在旷野里禁食了 40 个昼 夜。 然后，耶稣从旷野出来，在迦百农 城的教堂里施教。 在教堂里，一个被魔鬼附身的人看 见耶稣，就不停地喊叫，直到耶稣 说“从他身上出来”，魔鬼就被赶 出去了，那人也毫发无损。 福音书中多次记载了耶稣为被魔 鬼附身的人驱魔的故事。 除了驱除魔鬼，耶稣也神奇般地治 愈了许多受不同疾病折磨的人。

paragraphs.en: diseases. Soon word spreads among the people that a miracle worker is travelling around Israel and healing people.Alarge following begins to develop around Jesus. Then Jesus begins to recruit a core following of 12 disciples. His first disciples are 3 fisherman named Peter, James and John. They had been fishing all day without results, but Jesus tells them to throw their nets into the water one more time, and then they pull up so many fish that their nets begin to break. Peter realizes that he is in the presence of some sort of supernatural person so he falls to his knees, saying that he is too sinful to
paragraphs.zh: 消息很快就传开了，说有一个奇迹 制造者周游以色列各地医治人们。 耶稣周围开始出现了大批追随者。 然后耶稣着手招募一个由 12 个门 徒组成的核心跟随团。 他最早的门徒是三个渔夫，分别是 彼得、雅各和约翰。 他们捕了整整一天鱼，却一无所 获，但是在耶稣告诉他们再把网扔 到水里后，他们捕到了太多鱼，甚 至连鱼网都要撑破了。 彼得意识到出现在自己面前的是 一个超自然的人，于是他跪下说自 己有罪，不配接近这样一个好人。

paragraphs.en: be near such a good person. Jesus tells him to put away his nets because now he will be a fisher of men. One by one, Jesus gathers a core following of 12 disciples who are with him all the time. Then for the next 3 years, Jesus travels around Israel teaching and performing miracles. He preaches his most famous sermon called the Sermon on the Mount. This sermon is found in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter 6: verses 27 to 36. I include it here because it is a good summary of the core Christian ethic. As you can see, these concepts of mercy, forgiveness and love are
paragraphs.zh: 耶稣让他把鱼网收起来，因为从现 在开始他要捕人了。 一个接一个，耶稣最终集齐了 12 个全天候和他一起的核心跟随团。 在接下来的 3 年里，耶稣周游以色 列，四处传教，并展示自己的神力。 他最著名的布道词是 “登山宝 训”。 这篇布道词可以在马太福音第6章 第 27 -36 节找到。我把它写在这 里是因为它很好地总结了基督教 的核心伦理。 如你所见，这些关于仁慈、宽恕、 爱的概念几乎就完全是将帝国建

paragraphs.en: almost the opposite of the Roman values that created an empire built on violence and slavery. Unfortunately, as you will see in our future history classes, Christians seem to repeatedly ignore this teaching, and many other things that Jesus said as well. Here is a Chinese translation of that part, since the style of language is a little different from the type of English you may be used to. After the Sermon on the Mount Jesus continues to travel around Israel healing and teaching. He heals a sick woman. He restores sight to a blind man. Jesus even brings two people back from the dead.Ayoung girl who had just died and a man named
paragraphs.zh: 立在暴力和奴隶制基础上的罗马 人价值观的对立面。 不幸地是，正如你在接下来的课程 中将要看到的那样，基督教徒好像 一而再再而三地忽视了这一教诲， 和忽视许多耶稣所讲的别的话一 样。 这是刚才那篇布道词的中文翻译， 因为它的语言风格和你们现在习 惯的英语不太一样。 在山上讲道结束后，耶稣继续周游 以色列，治病、布道。 他治愈了一个生病的女人。 他让一个盲人重见光明。 耶稣甚至复活了两个死人。一个是 刚死的年轻女孩，一个是已经死了 三天的名叫拉撒路的男人。

paragraphs.en: Lazarus who had been dead for 3 days. There is another miracle that happens later when Jesus walks on water in view of his disciples. Towards the end of the gospels Jesus goes to Jerusalem riding on a donkey with his disciples, and large crowds are there to greet the miracle worker. But according to the gospels, Jesus has upset the religious authorities with his teaching and strange ideas, so they make a plan to kill him. That night, at their last supper Jesus tells his disciples that one of them will betray him. This famous painting by Leonardo DaVinci captures that scene. It shows the moment just after Jesus
paragraphs.zh: 后来，门徒们又看到了新的神迹， 那就是耶稣可以在水中行走。 福音书的结尾，耶稣骑着驴，和他 的门徒们一起去了耶路撒冷，无数 的人们在那里欢迎这位奇迹制造 者。 但是通过福音书我们可以得知，耶 稣的布道与奇怪的想法惹怒了宗 教势励，所里他们计划杀掉耶稣。 那天晚上最后的晚餐，耶稣告诉他 的门徒，说是在座的有一位将会背 叛他。 达芬奇著名的绘画作品《最后的晚 餐》就描绘了这一场景。它抓住了 耶稣话音刚落后，门徒们都表现出

paragraphs.en: announces that one of them will betray him, and the disciples are all reacting in shock. Jesus also predicts that Peter will deny him three times before the rooster crows the next morning. Judas is the one who agreed to betray Jesus in exchange for thirty pieces of silver. Judas arrives with soldiers to arrestJesus. Since it is dark he identifies Jesus to the soldiers by kissing him on the cheek. This might seem a little strange, but the kiss was actually the standard greeting between friends in ancient Rome. Here however, this sign of friendship was turned into an act of betrayal and the soldiers seize Jesus.
paragraphs.zh: 震惊的一瞬间。 耶稣也预言彼得会在第二天公鸡 打鸣前会否认三次。 犹大就是那个答应背叛耶稣以此 换取 30 件银饰的人。 犹大和士兵一起逮捕了耶稣。因为 天太黑了，所以他将耶稣误认为士 兵并亲吻了耶稣的脸颊。 这可能听着有些奇怪，但在古罗 马，亲吻是朋友之间正常的问候方 式。 然而，这个本是象征着友谊的吻， 在这儿却变成了背叛，最后，士兵 们抓住了耶稣。

paragraphs.en: While Jesus is awaiting trial, Peter is lingering outside in the courtyard when several people ask him if he is with Jesus. But Peter is afraid and denies knowing Jesus to three different people. Then the rooster crows and Peter remembers Jesus's prediction, he goes off and cries bitterly. The religious authorities find Jesus guilty and then turn him over to Pontius Pilate, the Roman governor of Israel, for punishment. The religious authorities tell Pilate that Jesus was planning a rebellion against Rome. Pilate is baffled as to why this man is being punished, but the religious authorities have gathered a mob outside calling for Jesus's death.
paragraphs.zh: 耶稣等待审判期间，许多人来问彼 得耶稣是否与他在一起，而彼得正 忧心忡忡地在外面徘徊。 但是彼得不确定，所以他向三个不 同的人否认知道耶稣在哪。然后公 鸡打鸣了，这时彼得记起了耶稣的 预言，他走到一边，流下了苦涩的 泪水。 宗教势力认定耶稣有罪，为了惩罚 他，把他交给了以色列的罗马总督 本丢彼拉多。 宗教势力告诉彼拉多耶稣计划背 叛罗马。 彼拉多不太清楚为什么耶稣要被 处罚，但是宗教势力聚集了一大帮 暴民在外面大喊处死耶稣。

paragraphs.en: So to please the crowd Pilate agrees to execute Jesus. Jesus is then whipped and made to carry a cross up to a hill called Golgotha. And there Jesus is executed by crucifixion, a terrible slow death that the Romans reserved for rebels. The disciples are scattered, confused and shocked by the death of their leader. Many of them had thought Jesus would lead a military rebellion against Rome like the Maccabees 200 years before. They take Jesus's body and bury it in a tomb, and roll a rock in front of it. But 3 days later Jesus comes back to life and rises from the grave. This happens on a Sunday morning, and
paragraphs.zh: 所以，为了取悦众人，彼拉多同意 处死耶稣。 于是耶稣被鞭笞，并被带到了各各 他山顶。 正是在那儿，耶稣被钉死在十字架 上，这是罗马人为叛徒准备的可怕 的慢速死亡的刑罚。 门徒们被驱散了，他们对领袖之死 既困惑又震惊。他们中的许多人都 认为耶稣会像200年前的马加比一 样对罗马发动军事叛乱。 他们带走并埋葬了耶稣的身体，还 在坟墓前面放了一块大石头。 但是，三天后，耶稣死而复生，从 坟墓里起来了。这发生在一个周日 的早晨，而这就是基督教节日复活

paragraphs.en: this is the event celebrated by the Christian holiday of Easter. The first to see Jesus is one of his female followers, a woman named Mary Magdalene, but Jesus tells her not to touch him, since he has not yet returned to his father. Later on, Jesus appears to other disciples as they are walking along the road talking about him. Then, after appearing to his disciples many more times, Jesus gives his followers a great commission. He tells them to go out and make disciples from people in every nation on earth. Then several weeks after his resurrection, Jesus ascends towards the sky until he is lost from sight. 50 days after Jesus’resurrection,
paragraphs.zh: 节所庆祝的事情。 第一个看到耶稣的事他的一个女 性跟随者，抹大拉的玛丽亚，但是 耶稣跟她说不要碰他，因为他还没 有变化成他的父亲。 过了一会儿，耶稣就出现在其他门 徒眼前，而他们正在边走边讨论耶 稣。 随后，在多次显现之后，耶稣给 了他的追随者一个伟大的使命。他 告诉他们走出去，让全世界每个国 家都能有门徒。 复活几周后，耶稣就飞升上天，消 失不见了。 耶稣复活 50 天后，上帝将圣灵赐

paragraphs.en: God sends the Holy Spirit to all of Jesus's followers who go out an begin telling the world about him. This once again angers the religious authorities who think that these new people are spreading heretical ideas. One of these religious authorities is a man named Paul. Thus, Paul sets to work trying to destroy this new religion.As a deeply religious Jew he thinks that these beliefs are wrong and must be wiped out. But then as Paul is traveling north to Damascus to hunt down all the Christians living in that city, he sees a vision of Jesus and is blinded. For three days Paul cannot see until a Christian named Ananias goes to his house and prays for him.
paragraphs.zh: 给所有耶稣的追随者，这些追随者 开始走向各地传播耶稣的故事。 这再一次激怒了宗教当局，他们认 为这些新人在传播异端思想。 其中一位宗教当权者名叫保罗。 因此，保罗开始努力摧毁这个新宗 教。作为一个深深信仰宗教的犹太 人，他认为这些信仰是错误的，必 须消灭。 但是，当保罗向北骑马赶到大马士 革去追捕住在那个城市的所有基 督徒时，他看到了耶稣，并且失明 了。 保罗整整三天都没能看见东西，直 到一名叫做亚拿尼亚的基督徒到 来并为他祈祷。

paragraphs.en: Then Paul can see again, and he goes from trying to destroy the religion to becoming one of its most important leaders! Paul then makes four journeys around the Eastern Mediterranean to spread this new religion. He makes the first trip from 46 to 49AD. The next trip from 49 - 52AD. The third trip from 53 to 58AD. And then his final journey takes him to Rome where he builds up the church in that city. This brings us toAD 64 and the great fire in Rome, when the emperor Nero is looking for someone to blame.
paragraphs.zh: 然后保罗就恢复了视力。此后，他 就从一个试图摧毁基督教的人变 成了基督教最重要的领导者之一！ 自此保罗在东地中海周围四次出 去传播这一新宗教。 他第一次传教是在公元 46－49 年 之间。 第二次传教是在公元 49－52 年之 间。 第三次传教是在公元 53－58 年之 间。 他最后一次传教去了罗马，并在罗 马城里建了教堂。 这就要说一说公元 64 年罗马的那 一场大火了。当时尼禄皇帝正在找 一个要怪罪的人。

paragraphs.en: He decides to use this weird new religion as his scapegoat. And this brings us back to what I told you in the last lesson, when Nero burns Christians alive in his garden for entertainment. It is during this first great persecution of the Church that St. Peter is arrested and executed. Peter is also crucified, but upside down, since he did not feel worthy of dying the same way that Jesus did. The Christians in Rome retrieve Peters body and bury it in a small tomb. Today the church of St. Peter's Basilica, the headquarters of the Catholic Church, sits above that tomb.
paragraphs.zh: 他决定让这个奇怪的新宗教去当 替罪羊。 这就回到了我们上节课讲的，尼禄 为了消遣娱乐，在花园中活活烧死 了基督教徒。 正是在对教会的第一次大的迫害 中，圣彼得被逮捕并被处决。 彼得也被钉在十字架上，但却是倒 挂的，因为他觉得自己不配和耶稣 一样死去。 罗马的基督教徒取下了彼得的身 体，并把他埋在了一个小小的坟墓 里。 今天，天主教堂的总部圣彼得大教 堂就坐落在那座坟墓之上。

paragraphs.en: St. Paul is also beheaded at this time. But even though Nero killed two of the main leaders, he could not eradicate this odd little religion, nor could he get rid of the appeal it seemed to have for many Romans. By 200AD, there were Christians living all across the Roman Empire, mainly in the large cities. This map shows you in green the location of all the major Christian communities in that year. And this religion would continue to grow and survive persecution until it eventually conquered the Roman empire from within. Now, however, we must bring this series to a close with our final lesson.
paragraphs.zh: 圣保罗此时也被斩首了。 即使尼禄杀死了基督教两位主要 领导人，他也无法消除这种奇怪的 小宗教，无法摆脱它对许多罗马人 的吸引力。 到了公元 200 年，基督徒遍布了整 个罗马帝国，主要是在大城市里。 这张地图的绿色部分显示了当年 所有主要基督教的聚集点。 这种宗教将继续成长，并在迫害中 顽强生存，最终将征服罗马帝国。 现在，无论如何，等最后一节课上 完，我们这一系列的课程就要收尾 了。

paragraphs.en: So please join me next time for the Fall of Rome! I hope you enjoyed that lesson and want to learn more. If you do, I have some recommendations for you. First, I recommend that you read the New Testament for yourself. You can find it translated into most of the worlds languages. This website is also a useful resource. You can look up any Bible passage on it and see that passage in multiple translations. I also recommend Rodney Stark's book: The Rise of Christianity. It tries to explain how Christianity became the dominant belief system of the Roman empire in 3 centuries. That's all for now, see you next time!
paragraphs.zh: 所以请下次继续加入我们的课程 一起学习罗马的衰落！ 我希望你们喜欢这一课，并且想了 解更多。如果需要的话，我给你们 推荐一些资源。 首先，我推荐你们自主阅读《新 约》。你会发现，它被翻译成了多 种语言。 这个网站也是一个有用的资源。你 可以浏览任一圣经的片段，还可以 看到这个片段的多语言翻译。 我也推荐罗德尼·斯达克的书：《基 督教的崛起》。这本书试图解释基 督教是如何在3个世纪中成长为罗 马帝国的主要宗教的。 就是这些了，下次见！

titleZh: 第二十讲：罗马陷落|帝国的荣耀与挽歌

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome to our lecture series on world history. Today is the final class and we will be using it to discuss the fall of the Roman Empire. We will be learning about the time between 64 and 476AD. Here is what those 4 centuries look like on our timeline. When we last looked at Rome before our lesson on Christianity, we were learning about the Emperor Nero and his persecution of the Christians after the Great Fire of Rome in 64AD. Aside from his cruelty to the Christians, Nero was a strange man and a very ineffective leader.
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎来到世界历史系列课 程。今天是最后一节课，我们来讲 讲罗马帝国的覆灭。 本节课的学习内容发生在公元 64 到 476 年之间。 这四个世纪在我们的时间线上是 这样的。 在之前关于罗马的课程里，我们正 讲到尼禄皇帝，以及他在公元 64 年罗马大火之后是如何迫害基督 徒的。 尼禄不仅仅对基督徒无比残忍，还 性格古怪，领导无方。

paragraphs.en: Eventually he did such a bad job leading the Empire that a general named Galba rebelled against him in 68AD. Pretty soon, large numbers of the army began joining Galba, including the captain of the palace guard. Seeing that his power had collapsed, Nero ran away and committed suicide at the age of 30. After Nero died, a civil war began in Rome and in 69AD, four different men proclaimed themselves emperor. First was Galba, who was overthrown by Otho, who was overthrown by Vitellius, who was overthrown by Vespasian. Vespasian was the most effective of
paragraphs.zh: 他的统治工作做得一塌糊涂，以至 于一位名叫加尔巴的将军在公元 68 年背叛了他。 很快，大批军队加入了加尔巴，其 中甚至包括宫廷卫队的队长。 看到自己倒台，尼禄逃跑并自杀， 年仅 30 岁。 尼禄死后，内战爆发，在公元 69 年这一年内，罗马帝国出现了四个 皇帝。 首先是加尔巴，但他被奥托推翻， 后者又被维特里乌斯推翻，最终则 是韦帕芗夺得皇位。 韦帕芗是这些人中最为实干的。他

paragraphs.en: these men. In 41AD, He was an important general during the conquest of Britain. Then in 66AD, there was a Jewish revolt in Israel. Vespasian took the army to Jerusalem and crushed the rebellion over the next 3 years. Vespasian's son Titus finished the war by capturing Jerusalem in 70 AD and destroying the second temple. Today all that is left of that second temple is a portion of its western wall. Today that is a very sacred place for Jews all around the world. Vespasian was the emperor for 10 years from 69 to 79AD. Then he was succeeded by his son Titus from 79 to 81AD and then by his other son Domitian from 81 to
paragraphs.zh: 在公元 41 年征服英国期间发挥了 重要的领导作用。 公元 66 年，以色列发生了犹太起 义。韦帕芗率领军队来到耶路撒 冷，在接下来的三年里镇压了叛 乱。 韦帕芗的儿子提图斯在公元 70 年 占领耶路撒冷并摧毁第二圣殿，结 束了战争。 今天第二圣殿只剩下西墙的一部 分。但是对全世界的犹太人来说， 这是一个非常神圣的地方。 韦帕芗于公元 69 年到 79 年这 10 年间在位。 他的儿子提图斯从公元 79 年到 81 年继承了皇位，他的另一个儿子图 密善从公元 81 年到 96 年在位。

paragraphs.en: 96AD. Historians call these three emperors the "Flavian Dynasty". Domitian was a very authoritarian ruler and reduced the power of the Senate during his time in office. But then Domitian was assassinated by court officials in 96AD. After Domitian died, he was succeeded by Nerva. Nerva was declared emperor by the Roman Senate when he was already 66 years old. Nerva didn't have any natural-born sons, so he adopted a man named Trajan as his heir, restored some of the freedoms taken away by Domitian, but then died after only 15 months on the throne.
paragraphs.zh: 历史学家称这三位皇帝为“弗拉维 王朝” 图密善是一个非常专制的统治者， 在他执政期间削弱了元老院的权 力。 但图密善在公元 96 年被法院官员 暗杀。 图密善死后，涅尔瓦接替了他的皇 位。 涅尔瓦在 66 岁时被罗马元老院宣 布为皇帝。 涅尔瓦没有子嗣，所以他收养了一 个名叫图拉真的继承人，恢复了一 些图密善剥夺的自由权利，但他仅 仅在位 15 个月就去世了。

paragraphs.en: Nerva began a tradition of Emperors adopting a successor rather than just choosing their son. There were five emperors who maintained this tradition and all of them were good capable rulers. They were Nerva, Trajan, Hadrian, Antoninus Pius, and Marcus Aurelius. The Political Philosopher Niccolo Machiavelli coined the term the "five good emperors" to describe this period which lasted from 96 to 180AD. During this 84-year period, the Roman Empire was peaceful and prosperous and reached the height of its power. The borders even reached as far as
paragraphs.zh: 涅尔瓦开启了一个先河，即皇帝通 过收养来指定继承人，而不是直接 让儿子继位。 有五位皇帝都保持了这一传统，他 们都是很有能力的统治者。 他们分别是涅尔瓦、图拉真、哈德 良、安东尼·庇护和马可·奥勒留。 政治哲学家尼古拉·马基雅维利创 造了“五贤帝”一词来形容公元 96 年到公元 180 年的这段时期。 在这 84 年的时间里，罗马帝国和 平繁荣，达到了鼎盛时期。 在公元 117 年图拉真皇帝的统治

paragraphs.en: Mesopotamia for one year under emperor Trajan in 117AD. This was the greatest extent of Roman power. This time of European peace is known as the "Pax Romana" or "Roman Peace". The Romans built extensive infrastructure to connect their empire together. They are perhaps most famous for their roads some of which still exist. Here is a surviving section of the Appian Way today, this was the main Roman road through Italy. The roads lasted so long because there were carefully engineered and built out of different layers of rock, gravel, and sand, which allowed for good drainage and durability. Roman roads were built to last!
paragraphs.zh: 下，罗马帝国的边界甚至达到了美 索不达米亚长达一年之久。这是罗 马政权能够扩张到的最大程度。 这一欧洲的和平时期被称为“罗马 和平”。 罗马人广泛建造基础设施，用以将 他们的帝国连接在一起。其中的一 些道路至今尚存，闻名于世。 这是现存的亚壁古道，在当时是穿 过意大利的主要罗马道路。 这些道路之所以能存在如此之久， 是因为它们经过了精心的设计与 施工，并由不同的岩石层、碎石层 和砂石层组成，保证了良好的排水 性和耐久性。罗马的道路是为长远 而修建的！

paragraphs.en: This road network was extensive and allowed the Romans to quickly move soldiers across the empire and respond to threats. It also allowed overland trade to flourish as the whole continent of Europe was connected by merchants and traders using these roads to move good around the empire. The Romans also built strong bridges when those roads came to a river. This here is theAlcantara Bridge in Spain. It was built by the Romans in 104AD, and it is still used today. Of course it has been repaired and fixed several times in 1214, in 1543, in 1762, in 1860 and most recently in 1969.
paragraphs.zh: 罗马的道路网非常广泛，因此罗马 人可以迅速将士兵输送到帝国各 地，对各种威胁做出反应。 这也促进了陆路贸易的蓬勃发展， 因为整个欧洲大陆都被连接起来， 贸易商利用这些道路在帝国各地 运输货物。 罗马人也建造了坚固的桥梁，用于 将道路穿过河流。 这是西班牙的阿尔坎塔拉桥。它由 罗马人在公元 104 年建造，至今仍 在使用。 当然，它在 1214 年，1543 年，1762 年，1860 年和最近的 1969 年都被 修复过几次。

paragraphs.en: But I still think it is remarkable that this bridge was built 2000 years ago by the Romans, and people have been using and taking care of it ever since. There is also this bridge, the Pons Fabricius which was built in 62 BC over part of the Tiber river. This bridge has also been in use ever since! The Romans also built water channels called aqueducts that could bring fresh water dozens of miles to different Roman cities. The water in these aqueducts was all moved by gravity, so the Romans needed the maintain a slight downward slope for the entire distance. Most of the time, these aqueducts
